- **Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR)** — Voluntary initiatives that improve community/environment, often leveraged in PR.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for IMC Mix — "PADS-DP"**
> *Public Relations, Advertising, Direct marketing, Sales promotion, Digital, Personal selling* — the six pillars of the Marketing Communications Mix as per KTU 2024 Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Strategic Role of PR in Marketing Communications

PR contributes to the marketing communications function across **six strategic dimensions**. Each dimension maps directly to a measurable business outcome:

### 2.1.1 Credibility & Trust Generation

- **Mechanism:** Third-party media coverage carries an implicit endorsement that paid ads cannot replicate.
- **Marketing Outcome:** Higher message acceptance rate, lower customer acquisition cost (CAC).
- **Evidence Metric:** **Edelman Trust Barometer** consistently shows earned media is trusted **2x to 3x** more than paid advertising by global consumers.

### 2.1.2 Brand Narrative & Storytelling

- **Mechanism:** PR crafts the *why* behind the brand — mission, founder story, social purpose.
- **Marketing Outcome:** Emotional brand attachment, premium pricing power, customer loyalty.
- **Example:** *Patagonia's* "Don't Buy This Jacket" campaign turned a marketing message into a global sustainability story.

### 2.1.3 Reputation & Risk Management

- **Mechanism:** Proactive relationship-building with journalists, regulators, and communities; reactive crisis response.
- **Marketing Outcome:** Brand resilience during downturns, reduced negative press amplification.
- **Example:** *Johnson & Johnson's Tylenol* crisis (1982) — textbook PR response preserved brand equity.

### 2.1.4 Cost-Efficient Reach Amplification

- **Mechanism:** One well-placed media story can reach millions without media spend.
- **Marketing Outcome:** Higher **Earned Media Value (EMV)** per rupee invested.
- **Benchmarks:** Industry-standard **EMV multiplier is typically 3x to 10x** the actual PR investment.

### 2.1.5 Stakeholder Relationship Building

- **Mechanism:** Two-way symmetrical communication with employees, investors, community, government.
- **Marketing Outcome:** Internal advocacy, investor confidence, regulatory goodwill.
- **Frameworks:** Grunig's *Four Models of PR* (Press Agentry, Public Information, Two-way Asymmetrical, Two-way Symmetrical).

### 2.1.6 Amplification of Other MarCom Tools

- **Mechanism:** PR-driven editorial coverage boosts advertising effectiveness, lifts sales promotion redemption, and improves SEO.
- **Marketing Outcome:** Synergistic IMC effect — the whole is greater than the sum of parts.

## 2.2 Grunig's Four Models of PR (Theoretical Backbone)

| Model | Communication Direction | Purpose | Modern Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Press Agentry** | One-way (Org → Public) | Propaganda, attention-grabbing | Sports, entertainment, political stunts |
| **Public Information** | One-way (Org → Public) | Dissemination of truthful info | Government bulletins, annual reports |
| **Two-way Asymmetrical** | Two-way, persuasion-based | Scientific persuasion, attitude change | Persuasive campaigns (e.g., anti-smoking) |
| **Two-way Symmetrical** | Two-way, mutual understanding | Building long-term relationships | Modern stakeholder engagement, CSR comms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Favourite:** Grunig's model is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in any MarCom exam. Memorize the *purpose*, *direction*, and *example* for each of the four models.

## 2.3 Types / Functions of PR in Marketing

| Type | Focus | Marketing Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Media Relations** | Press, TV, digital media | Product launch coverage |
| **Product Publicity** | Promoting specific products/services | Movie promos, car launches |
| **Corporate Communications** | Company-wide image | Annual report, CEO interviews |
| **Crisis Management** | Reputational defense | Product recall, data breach |
| **Community Relations** | Local community engagement | Plant openings, local CSR |
| **Investor Relations** | Shareholders, analysts | Quarterly earnings, AGM |
| **Government Relations** | Public policy, regulators | Lobbying, compliance comms |
| **Internal Communications** | Employees | Town halls, newsletters |
| **Lobbying** | Legislative influence | Policy advocacy |
| **Public Affairs** | Public issues, social causes | Environmental campaigns |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Since this is a management course, "formulas" here are **analytical metrics and frameworks** used in PR measurement and IMC planning:

| Metric / Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Benchmark | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Earned Media Value (EMV)** | $\text{EMV} = \text{Number of mentions} \times \text{Equivalent ad rate}$ | Currency (INR/USD) | Quantifying PR ROI |
| **PR Return on Investment (ROI)** | $\text{PR ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Net PR Benefit} - \text{PR Cost}}{\text{PR Cost}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | Campaign justification |
| **Cost Per Impression (CPI)** | $\text{CPI} = \dfrac{\text{Total PR Spend}}{\text{Total Impressions Earned}}$ | Currency / 1000 impressions | Comparing channels |
| **Share of Voice (SOV)** | $\text{SOV} = \dfrac{\text{Brand Mentions}}{\text{Total Category Mentions}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | Competitive PR benchmarking |
| **Media Reach** | $\text{Reach} = \sum (\text{Audience size of each outlet})$ | Number of unique people | Coverage planning |
| **Ots (Opportunities to See)** | $\text{OTS} = \text{Reach} \times \text{Frequency}$ | Total exposures | Combined media planning |
| **Message Pull-Through Rate** | $\text{Pull-Through} = \dfrac{\text{Articles mentioning key message}}{\text{Total articles}}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | Message effectiveness |
| **Sentiment Score** | $\text{Sentiment} = \dfrac{\text{Positive mentions} - \text{Negative mentions}}{\text{Total mentions}}$ | Ratio (-1 to +1) | Reputation health |
| **AIDA Model (Communication Effect)** | Attention → Interest → Desire → Action | Sequential funnel | Evaluating campaign stages |
| **DAGMAR Model** | Awareness → Comprehension → Conviction → Action | Sequential | Advertising/PR goal setting |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Safety Protocol:** In the formulas above, vertical bars and pipes have been **strictly avoided** inside table cells. All conditional notation uses $\dfrac{}{}$ (display fraction) form. Do NOT use $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table — it breaks the parser.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of PR Metrics

- **PR ROI** is used by CMOs to defend PR budgets against finance teams.
- **EMV** allows PR agencies to prove value in monetary terms that CFOs understand.
- **SOV** is the competitive intelligence gold standard — used in *FMCG, telecom, and pharma* sectors for share-of-voice wars.
- **Sentiment Analysis** is now automated through AI tools (Brandwatch, Meltwater, Sprinklr) and feeds real-time reputation dashboards.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & PR Planning Process

## 3.1 The RACE Model of PR (Operational Backbone)

The **RACE model** is the standard PR planning framework used globally. Each step has a clear input, process, and output. KTU examiners frequently ask for this as a **14-mark structured essay question**.

### Step 1 — RESEARCH

**Objective:** Understand the situation, the public, and the environment.

- **Activities:**
  - Stakeholder mapping and audience segmentation
  - Media audit (which journalists cover your beat)
  - Sentiment analysis of current brand perception
  - SWOT analysis of the brand's public image
  - Competitor PR benchmarking
- **Output:** A *Situation Analysis Report* with clear communication objectives.

### Step 2 — ACTION (Strategy & Planning)

**Objective:** Define what the PR program will do.

- **Activities:**
  - Define SMART communication objectives (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound)
  - Identify key messages and positioning
  - Choose target publics and prioritize
  - Select PR tools and channels
  - Budget allocation
- **Output:** A *PR Strategy Document* with objectives, messages, and tactical plan.

### Step 3 — COMMUNICATION (Execution & Tactics)

**Objective:** Implement the planned PR activities.

- **Activities:**
  - Draft and distribute press releases, media kits, and pitch letters
  - Organize press conferences, media briefings, and product launches
  - Coordinate interviews, op-eds, and guest articles
  - Run social media PR campaigns
  - Engage in community events and CSR activities
  - Manage crisis communication if needed
- **Output:** *Tangible deliverables* — coverage, mentions, engagement metrics, event outcomes.

### Step 4 — EVALUATION

**Objective:** Measure outcomes against objectives.

- **Activities:**
  - Media monitoring and clip counting
  - Reach, impressions, and OTS calculation
  - Sentiment and tone analysis
  - Survey-based attitude and perception tracking
  - PR ROI and EMV calculation
  - Lessons-learned report for the next cycle
- **Output:** An *Evaluation Report* that closes the loop and informs the next RACE cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-Step Logic:** RACE is *iterative and cyclical*. The Evaluation phase of one cycle feeds into the Research phase of the next — making PR a continuous improvement discipline, not a one-off activity.

## 3.2 PR Planning Process — Detailed Step-by-Step Breakdown

For examination purposes, the **PR planning process** can be expanded into 8 steps as per KTU 2024 Module 1 expectations:

### Step 1: Define the PR Problem or Opportunity
- Identify whether the situation is *proactive* (opportunity-based) or *reactive* (problem-based).
- Example problem: A product recall. Example opportunity: A 50-year anniversary.

### Step 2: Conduct Research
- Use **formative research** (before action) and **evaluative research** (after action).
- Tools: Focus groups, surveys, media audits, content analysis.

### Step 3: Establish Objectives and Goals
- Objectives must align with overall **marketing objectives** (e.g., awareness, perception, loyalty).
- Use the **DAGMAR framework** (Awareness → Comprehension → Conviction → Action) for goal structuring.

### Step 4: Define the Target Public
- Segment publics by **stake, geography, demographics, psychographics**.
- Different publics = different messages = different channels.

### Step 5: Choose Communication Channels and Messages
- **Channels:** Earned media, owned media, shared media, events, community forums.
- **Messages:** Must be clear, consistent, credible, and customized per public.

### Step 6: Implement the PR Plan
- Execute tactics, manage timelines, coordinate with internal teams and external agencies.

### Step 7: Evaluate the PR Program
- Use the metrics from Section 2.4 to assess effectiveness.

### Step 8: Refine and Adjust
- Apply learnings to the next campaign cycle.

## 3.3 PR Integration within the Marketing Mix (4Ps + PR)

PR does not replace marketing — it *amplifies* the 4Ps:

| Marketing Mix Element | PR's Role |
|---|---|
| **Product** | Product launch publicity, reviews, technical briefings, influencer seeding |
| **Price** | Value communication, premium positioning through editorial validation |
| **Place** | Distribution channel PR, retail partner announcements |
| **Promotion** | Cross-channel amplification of advertising, sales promo support |

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Example — PR Budget Justification

**Scenario:** A FMCG brand in Kerala launches a new ayurvedic shampoo. The PR agency proposes a 90-day campaign with the following:

- Total PR Spend: ₹10,00,000
- Estimated earned media: 50 mentions in regional newspapers, 5 TV features, 20 digital influencer posts
- Average newspaper readership: 2,00,000; Average TV viewership: 5,00,000; Average influencer followers: 50,000
- Equivalent ad rate: ₹1,50,000 per newspaper page, ₹5,00,000 per TV minute, ₹75,000 per influencer post

**Step 1 — Calculate Total Reach:**

$$
\text{Reach}_{\text{newspaper}} = 50 \times 2{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Reach}_{\text{TV}} = 5 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 25{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Reach}_{\text{influencer}} = 20 \times 50{,}000 = 10{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Total Reach} = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 + 25{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}35{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Earned Media Value (EMV):**

$$
\text{EMV}_{\text{newspaper}} = 50 \times 1{,}50{,}000 = 75{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{EMV}_{\text{TV}} = 5 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 25{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{EMV}_{\text{influencer}} = 20 \times 75{,}000 = 15{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Total EMV} = 75{,}00{,}000 + 25{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}15{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate PR ROI:**

$$
\text{PR ROI} = \dfrac{1{,}15{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 1050\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Every ₹1 spent on PR generated ₹11.50 in equivalent advertising value — a compelling argument to defend the PR budget at the next board meeting.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always convert large numbers to **Indian numbering format (lakhs, crores)** when writing answers in KTU exams. ₹1,35,00,000 reads more naturally than 13,500,000 in Indian academic context.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
