(Penalty: -1 mark.) > 2. **Listing only three of the five DOI factors.** Examiners expect all five (Relative Advantage, Compatibility, Complexity, Trialability, Observability). Writing only three attracts partial credit. (Penalty: -2 marks in a 7-mark sub-question.) > 3. **Forgetting that Complexity has an INVERSE relationship.** Many students write "high complexity = high adoption," which is the *exact opposite* of the truth. This is a high-frequency trap question. (Penalty: full mark loss on that sub-part.) > 4. **Ignoring the 2.5 / 13.5 / 34 / 34 / 16 percentage split** of adopter categories. Memorize this — KTU loves asking "what percentage are Early Adopters?" (Answer: 13.5%.) (Penalty: -0.5 mark.) > 5. **Writing "Mass media drives adoption" as a standalone statement.** Always add that *interpersonal channels* drive the actual adoption *decision*. (Penalty: -1 mark.) > 6. **Not labelling the 5-stage Innovation-Decision Process** correctly (Knowledge → Persuasion → Decision → Implementation → Confirmation). Skipping "Confirmation" stage is a common omission. (Penalty: -1 mark.) > 7. **Mixing up Bass Model coefficients.** $p$ = mass media / innovation effect; $q$ = word-of-mouth / imitation effect. Reversing them is a guaranteed mark loss. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read 3 times before the exam):** * ✅ **Adoption** = Individual decision to use an innovation; **Diffusion** = Spread of that innovation across a social system. * ✅ **Five DOI Attributes** (memorize in order): **R**elative **A**dvantage, **C**ompatibility, **C**omplexity, **T**rialability, **O**bservability. (Mnemonic: **R-A-C-C-T-O**) * ✅ **Complexity is the ONLY attribute with an inverse relationship** to the rate of adoption. All other four are positive. * ✅ **Three Types of Innovation:** Continuous (fast), Dynamically Continuous (moderate), Discontinuous (slow). Discontinuous = more IMC budget needed. * ✅ **Five-Stage Adoption Process:** Knowledge → Persuasion → Decision → Implementation → Confirmation. (Add "Confirmation" — it is frequently forgotten.) * ✅ **Adopter Categories (with exact percentages):** Innovators (2.5%), Early Adopters (13.5%), Early Majority (34%), Late Majority (34%), Laggards (16%). Total = 100%. * ✅ **Critical Mass** = 10%–25% adoption threshold. Below it, IMC must be heavy. Above it, adoption becomes self-sustaining. * ✅ **Bass Diffusion Model:** $p$ = innovation coefficient (mass media), $q$ = imitation coefficient (word-of-mouth). * ✅ **Communication Channels:** Mass media creates *awareness*; Interpersonal channels create *attitude change* and *adoption*. * ✅ **Other Adoption Factors:** Perceived risk (5 types: financial, functional, physical, social, psychological), cost, image, social system norms, and adopter's individual readiness. * ✅ **Strategic Levers (which factor → which IMC tool):** * High complexity → Tutorials, demos * Low compatibility → Testimonials, cultural context * Low trialability → Free trials, money-back guarantee * Low observability → UGC, brand communities * Low relative advantage → Comparative advertising * ✅ **Laggards are NOT irrational** — they are rational within their reference frame (tradition, limited resources, low information access). * ✅ **Mass media is necessary but not sufficient** — it generates awareness; interpersonal channels close the deal. > **Final Examiner Tip:** When answering a 14-mark question on Factors Influencing Adoption, structure your answer as **(i) Theoretical Foundation → (ii) Application to the Given Product → (iii) IMC Recommendation → (iv) Conclusion with Critical Mass mention.** This four-part structure aligns with KTU's outcome-based evaluation rubric and consistently fetches full marks.

topics.title: Purchase Decision Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT742 - MARKETING COMMUNICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Purchase Decision Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
The **Purchase Decision Process** is a structured, sequential model in consumer behaviour that describes the cognitive, emotional, and behavioural stages a buyer traverses from the initial recognition of an unmet need to the post-purchase evaluation of the chosen product or service. In the context of KTU's Marketing Communications framework (PECBT742), this process is the central pillar upon which all Integrated Marketing Communications (IMC) campaigns are designed, because each communication touchpoint is engineered to influence a specific stage of the consumer's decision journey.

The most widely accepted academic version is **Philip Kotler's Five-Stage Model of the Consumer Buying Decision Process**, which represents the canonical reference for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 2):** Students must memorise Kotler's 5-Stage Model along with the **Engel-Kollat-Blackwell (EKB) Model**, as both are listed in the PECBT742 syllabus under the "Consumer Decision Making" cluster.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Coffee Machine Buying Journey"
Imagine you are buying a new coffee machine for your home.

- **Stage 1 (Need Recognition):** Your old machine breaks down. You suddenly *realise* you need a replacement.
- **Stage 2 (Information Search):** You ask friends, browse Amazon, watch YouTube reviews, and visit showrooms.
- **Stage 3 (Evaluation of Alternatives):** You compare Nespresso vs. Breville vs. De'Longhi on price, features, and warranty.
- **Stage 4 (Purchase Decision):** You finally tap "Buy Now" on the Breville model.
- **Stage 5 (Post-Purchase Behaviour):** You enjoy the coffee (satisfaction) or regret not buying Nespresso (cognitive dissonance).

**Intuition:** Marketers do not "sell" at a single moment — they *orchestrate* communication across the entire 5-stage timeline. A TV ad creates *need*, a Google ad answers *search*, a comparison blog supports *evaluation*, a discount coupon triggers *purchase*, and a thank-you email manages *post-purchase*.

### Key Standard Metrics in this Module

| Metric | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Internal Stimulus** | A need arising from within the consumer (e.g., hunger, thirst). |
| **External Stimulus** | A need triggered by outside influences (e.g., advertisement, peer pressure). |
| **Evoked Set** | The small group of brands a consumer seriously considers during evaluation. |
| **Cognitive Dissonance** | Post-purchase mental tension caused by doubt over the choice made. |
| **Confirmation Bias** | The tendency to seek information that validates the purchase decision. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Marketing Communications:** Every piece of brand communication (an ad, a billboard, a sales call, a tweet) is mapped to *one* stage of this process. Misalignment is the #1 reason campaigns fail.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Kotler's Five-Stage Purchase Decision Model — Structured Logic

The model is **sequential but iterative**. A consumer can revisit Stage 2 (Information Search) after Stage 3 (Evaluation) and even loop back to Stage 1 (Need Recognition) if a new stimulus appears.

#### Stage 1 — Need Recognition (Problem Recognition)
- **Why it happens:** The consumer perceives a gap between their *actual state* and their *desired state*.
- **How it's triggered:**
  - **Internal Stimuli:** Basic human needs (hunger, thirst, comfort) or psychological needs (status, belonging).
  - **External Stimuli:** Advertising, social media, word-of-mouth, packaging design, in-store displays.
- **Marketing Lever:** The communication must *activate* the need. Tools include storytelling, problem-agitation, and lifestyle aspiration.
- **Outcome Metric:** **Awareness Lift** — measured as the percentage increase in consumers who identify the problem post-campaign.

#### Stage 2 — Information Search
- **Two sub-types:**
  - **Internal Search:** Memory recall of past experiences with the product category.
  - **External Search:** Active gathering from personal sources (family, friends), public sources (reviews, ratings), and marketer-dominated sources (websites, brochures).
- **Marketing Lever:** Ensure the brand is **findable, credible, and dominant** in the channels the consumer uses. SEO, influencer marketing, and PR are the dominant 2024 tools.
- **Outcome Metric:** **Search Share of Voice** — the brand's visibility proportion in the channels actively consulted by the consumer.

#### Stage 3 — Evaluation of Alternatives
- The consumer defines an **Evoked Set** (typically 3–5 brands) and evaluates them against:
  - **Objective Criteria:** Price, features, warranty, durability.
  - **Subjective Criteria:** Brand perception, emotional appeal, peer approval.
- **Marketing Lever:** Position the brand favourably against the **determinant attribute** — the single most important criterion in the consumer's mind.
- **Outcome Metric:** **Consideration Rate** — the percentage of aware consumers who include the brand in their evoked set.

#### Stage 4 — Purchase Decision
- The consumer commits to a brand, but two factors can still intervene:
  - **Attitudes of Others:** A friend's negative review at the last moment.
  - **Unanticipated Situational Factors:** Stock-out, price hike, store closure.
- **Marketing Lever:** **Point-of-Purchase (POP) communication**, scarcity messaging ("Only 2 left!"), and seamless checkout UX.
- **Outcome Metric:** **Conversion Rate** — the percentage of evaluating consumers who complete the purchase.

#### Stage 5 — Post-Purchase Behaviour
- The consumer experiences either:
  - **Satisfaction:** Performance meets or exceeds expectation → brand loyalty, repurchase, advocacy.
  - **Dissatisfaction:** Performance falls short of expectation → returns, complaints, negative word-of-mouth.
  - **Cognitive Dissonance:** Post-purchase doubt, especially for high-involvement purchases.
- **Marketing Lever:** Follow-up emails, warranty registration, customer care, loyalty programmes.
- **Outcome Metric:** **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** — the industry-standard loyalty index.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** Always map a marketing communication activity to *the specific stage* it targets. Saying "advertising increases sales" loses marks. Saying "this TV commercial targets Stage 1 by creating need recognition through the problem-agitation hook" earns full marks.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Logical Expression | Variables Explained | Practical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Customer Satisfaction Index** | $CSI = Perception - Expectation$ | Perception is the actual performance experienced; Expectation is the pre-purchase belief. | Quantifies post-purchase gap to design corrective communication. |
| **Confirmation Level** | $CL = Performance - Expectation$ | Positive $CL$ = Confirmation; Negative $CL$ = Disconfirmation. | Oliver's Expectation-Confirmation Theory (1980). |
| **Cognitive Dissonance Intensity** | $CDI = \vert Importance \times \vert Decidedness - Closeness \vert \vert$ | Importance = personal stake; Decidedness = irrevocability; Closeness = similarity of rejected options. | Higher $CDI$ → urgent need for reassurance communication. |
| **Evoked Set Size Limit** | $ES_{max} \approx 3$ to $5$ brands | Working memory constraint of consumers. | Brand must be inside this set to be purchased. |
| **Conversion Funnel Efficiency** | $CFE = \frac{N_{purchase}}{N_{awareness}} \times 100\%$ | $N_{purchase}$ = buyers; $N_{awareness}$ = aware prospects. | Measures total IMC campaign effectiveness. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Markdown Rule Applied:** All absolute value bars inside the table use the LaTeX `\vert` command — never the raw pipe symbol — to preserve the table's structural integrity.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of the Purchase Decision Process in Engineering & Management Fields
- **Retail Industry:** Designing omnichannel journeys where every touchpoint corresponds to a stage.
- **Product Design:** Embedding *post-purchase reassurance features* (e.g., easy returns, onboarding tutorials) directly into the product.
- **B2B Sales Engineering:** Long industrial sales cycles (often 6–18 months) are extended versions of this 5-stage model, justifying heavy investment in Stages 2 and 3 communication.
- **Digital Marketing Analytics:** Google Analytics and Meta Ads Manager track users across *Awareness → Consideration → Conversion* funnels that map directly to Stages 1–4.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Logical Derivation of the Five-Stage Decision Flow

We derive the consumer's path by mapping the **information-processing sequence** of the human brain onto the buying act. Each transition is justified by a cognitive operation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Stimulus Arrival} &\rightarrow S_{ext} \in \text{External Stimuli}, \quad S_{int} \in \text{Internal Stimuli} \\
\text{Step 2: Need Recognition} &\rightarrow \text{Gap} = \text{Desired State} - \text{Actual State} > 0 \\
\text{Step 3: Internal Search} &\rightarrow I_{int} = \text{Memory Retrieval}(\text{Prior Brand Knowledge}) \\
\text{Step 4: External Search} &\rightarrow I_{ext} = f(\text{Peers, Media, Online, Salesperson}) \\
\text{Step 5: Evoked Set Formation} &\rightarrow ES = \{ B_1, B_2, B_3, B_4, B_5 \}, \quad \vert ES \vert \le 5 \\
\text{Step 6: Multi-Attribute Evaluation} &\rightarrow U(B_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot x_{ij} \\
\text{Step 7: Purchase Intention} &\rightarrow PI = f(\text{Attitude, Normative Pressure, Situational Factors}) \\
\text{Step 8: Actual Purchase} &\rightarrow P = 1 \text{ if } PI > 0, \text{ else } P = 0 \\
\text{Step 9: Post-Purchase Consumption} &\rightarrow \text{Performance}(P) = \text{Utility Experienced} \\
\text{Step 10: Confirmation Check} &\rightarrow CL = \text{Performance} - \text{Expectation} \\
\text{Step 11: Satisfaction / Dissonance} &\rightarrow \text{If } CL \geq 0: \text{Satisfaction}; \quad \text{If } CL < 0: \text{Dissatisfaction} \\
\text{Step 12: Feedback Loop} &\rightarrow \text{Post-purchase feedback modifies Stage 1 for next cycle}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation of Each Step:**
- **Step 2** is the *trigger* — without a recognised gap, no purchase process begins.
- **Steps 3 & 4** are the *information acquisition phase* — internal memory is always checked first because it is costless.
- **Step 5** is the *funnel narrowing* — humans cannot evaluate 50 brands; they shortlist.
- **Step 6** uses **multi-attribute utility theory (MAUT)** — the consumer weighs each brand on weighted attributes.
- **Steps 7 & 8** capture the *final commitment*, which is *not deterministic* — external noise (a friend saying "don't buy that") can derail it.
- **Steps 9–11** are the *evaluation phase* — the actual product is consumed and compared to expectations.
- **Step 12** closes the loop — every purchase experience rewires the consumer's mental schema for the *next* buying decision in the same category.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Marketing Communication Levers per Stage

| Stage | Buyer's Mindset | Buyer's Central Question | Marketing Communication Lever | Optimal IMC Channel (2024) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1. Need Recognition** | Unaware / Problem-Aware | "Do I have a problem?" | Problem-agitation storytelling | TV, YouTube, Instagram Reels, OOH |
| **2. Information Search** | Solution-Aware | "What are my options?" | Educational content, SEO, reviews | Google Search, Blogs, Influencer content |
| **3. Evaluation of Alternatives** | Brand-Aware | "Which is the best for me?" | Comparison content, testimonials, demos | Landing pages, video reviews, case studies |
| **4. Purchase Decision** | Decision-Ready | "Why should I act NOW?" | Scarcity, urgency, trust signals | Email, retargeting ads, POS displays |
| **5. Post-Purchase Behaviour** | Owner / User | "Did I make the right choice?" | Reassurance, onboarding, loyalty rewards | WhatsApp support, NPS surveys, loyalty apps |

### 3.3 Extended Models — EKB and Howard-Sheth (For KTU Depth)

**Engel-Kollat-Blackwell (EKB) Model** extends Kotler's model with explicit *feedback loops* and separates *information processing* from *decision process*. The EKB model has **7 stages**:
1. Need Recognition
2. Search for Information
3. Pre-Purchase Alternative Evaluation
4. Purchase
5. Consumption
6. Post-Consumption Evaluation
7. Divestment (Disposal)

**Howard-Sheth Model** treats buying as a continuous *learning process*, distinguishing between *routinised response behaviour* (everyday low-involvement purchases like milk) and *extensive problem solving* (high-involvement purchases like a car).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 14-Mark Strategy:** When asked to "explain the consumer decision process," students should write Kotler's 5-Stage Model as the **spine** (≈10 marks worth of content) and add a brief 3–4 line comparison with the EKB model to demonstrate depth (≈4 marks).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Kotler's 5-Stage Purchase Decision Process

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **inputs, processing stages, outputs, and feedback loops** of the consumer buying decision system. This serves as a KTU-board-ready visual aid that respects the alphanumeric node-ID and label-formatting safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([External Stimulus: Ad, Peers, Social Media]) --> nodeB
    nodeC([Internal Stimulus: Hunger, Status, Comfort]) --> nodeB
    nodeB[Stage 1: Need Recognition] --> nodeD
    nodeD{Is Gap Significant?}
    nodeD -- No --> nodeE[No Purchase Action]
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeF[Stage 2A: Internal Information Search]
    nodeF --> nodeG{Sufficient Information?}
    nodeG -- Yes --> nodeI[Stage 3: Evaluation of Alternatives]
    nodeG -- No --> nodeH[Stage 2B: External Information Search]
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Build Evoked Set: 3 to 5 Brands]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Apply Multi Attribute Utility Model]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Stage 4: Purchase Decision]
    nodeL --> nodeM{Purchase Completed?}
    nodeM -- No --> nodeN[Aborted: Stock Out, Peer Dissent, Price Hike]
    nodeM -- Yes --> nodeO[Stage 5: Post Purchase Consumption]
    nodeO --> nodeP[Compare Performance vs Expectation]
    nodeP --> nodeQ{Confirmation Level}
    nodeQ -- Positive --> nodeR[Satisfaction, Loyalty, Advocacy, Repurchase]
    nodeQ -- Negative --> nodeS[Dissatisfaction, Returns, Negative WOM]
    nodeR --> nodeT[Feedback to Stage 1: Memory Updated]
    nodeS --> nodeT
    nodeT --> nodeB
```

### 4.2 IMC Channel Mapping Diagram — Communication Levers Across the Funnel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Stage 1: Need Recognition]
        n1A[TV Commercial] --> n1B[Problem Agitation Hook]
        n1C[Instagram Reel] --> n1B
    end
    subgraph S2[Stage 2: Information Search]
        n2A[Google SEO Blog] --> n2B[Education and Awareness]
        n2C[Influencer Review] --> n2B
    end
    subgraph S3[Stage 3: Evaluation of Alternatives]
        n3A[Comparison Landing Page] --> n3B[Brand Positioning]
        n3C[Customer Testimonial] --> n3B
    end
    subgraph S4[Stage 4: Purchase Decision]
        n4A[Retargeting Ad] --> n4B[Urgency and Scarcity Trigger]
        n4C[Email Coupon] --> n4B
    end
    subgraph S5[Stage 5: Post Purchase]
        n5A[Onboarding Email] --> n5B[Loyalty and Reassurance]
        n5C[NPS Survey] --> n5B
    end
    n1B --> n2B
    n2B --> n3B
    n3B --> n4B
    n4B --> n5B
    n5B -.Feedback Loop.-> n1B
```

### 4.3 Factors Influencing the Decision Process (Adapted Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeInput[Buyer Decision Process] --> nodeCULT[Cultural Factors: Culture, Subculture, Social Class]
    nodeInput --> nodeSOC[Social Factors: Reference Groups, Family, Roles]
    nodeInput --> nodePERS[Personal Factors: Age, Lifestyle, Income, Occupation]
    nodeInput --> nodePSY[Psychological Factors: Motivation, Perception, Learning, Beliefs]
    nodeCULT --> nodeOut[Stage 1 Outcome]
    nodeSOC --> nodeOut
    nodePERS --> nodeOut
    nodePSY --> nodeOut
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
*Define the term "Need Recognition" in the context of Kotler's Purchase Decision Process. Differentiate between internal and external stimuli with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Need Recognition is the **first stage** of Kotler's Purchase Decision Process, in which the consumer perceives a significant gap between their **actual state** and their **desired state**, thereby triggering a buying decision.

The two stimulus types are:

1. **Internal Stimulus** — A need originating from within the consumer, such as physiological drives. *Example:* Feeling hungry leads to the need to buy food.
2. **External Stimulus** — A need triggered by external environmental cues. *Example:* Watching a pizza advertisement on television creates a craving to order pizza.

The marketer's role at this stage is to design communications that **activate** the relevant stimulus and elevate the perceived gap above the consumer's threshold for action. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for internal example, 1 mark for external example.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*What is Cognitive Dissonance? How can a marketer reduce it through post-purchase communication?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Cognitive Dissonance is the **post-purchase mental tension or doubt** that a consumer experiences when they perceive an inconsistency between their chosen brand and the rejected alternatives, particularly in high-involvement purchases.

**Marketer's strategies to reduce it (any three):**
1. Sending a **welcome email** that reaffirms the product's superior features.
2. Sharing **customer testimonials** from existing users to validate the choice.
3. Providing **warranty documentation** to enhance perceived security.
4. Offering a **loyalty programme** to embed the brand within the consumer's identity.
5. Following up with **usage tips and tutorials** to ensure the product performs as expected, raising confirmation level.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for valid communication strategies.)*

---

### Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
*(a)* Explain in detail Kotler's Five-Stage Model of the Purchase Decision Process. For each stage, identify the **central question in the consumer's mind** and suggest **one IMC tool** most suited to address it. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A premium smartwatch brand observes a sharp drop in Stage 5 satisfaction scores despite strong sales. Diagnose the likely communication failure and propose a **post-purchase IMC campaign** to address it. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Kotler's Five-Stage Model (7 marks):**

| Stage | Consumer's Central Question | Suggested IMC Tool | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. Need Recognition | "Do I have a problem worth solving?" | Problem-Agitation TV/YouTube Ad | Creates awareness of the gap between current state and ideal state. |
| 2. Information Search | "What are my available options?" | SEO-optimised Blog and Google Ads | Ensures brand visibility in the consumer's active research channel. |
| 3. Evaluation of Alternatives | "Which option is best for me?" | Comparison Landing Page with Testimonials | Directly addresses determinant attributes and reduces perceived risk. |
| 4. Purchase Decision | "Why should I buy NOW from THIS brand?" | Retargeting Ad with Scarcity Timer | Triggers FOMO and provides the final push. |
| 5. Post-Purchase Behaviour | "Did I make the right choice?" | Onboarding Email Series and Loyalty Programme | Reassures the consumer, reinforces the smart choice, and drives repurchase. |

The five stages are **sequential but iterative**, with strong feedback from Stage 5 to Stage 1. *(1 mark for table, 1 mark for explanation of inter-stage logic, 1 mark for IMC tool justification, balance for additional depth.)*

**Part (b) — Post-Purchase IMC Campaign (7 marks):**

**Diagnosis of Failure:** The brand is *selling* well but *failing to retain* satisfaction. This indicates that **performance is falling short of the expectations created during Stages 1–3** — a classic case of **negative disconfirmation** (where $CL < 0$). The communication failure lies in Stages 1–3, where over-promising created inflated expectations that the product cannot match in real-world usage.

**Proposed Post-Purchase IMC Campaign — "SmartCare 360" (3 pillars):**

1. **Day 0 — Welcome Email + Setup Guide:** Re-anchor expectations by showing the watch's *realistic* capabilities with tutorial videos. Set proper use-case expectations. *(2 marks)*

2. **Day 7 — Community Activation:** Invite the buyer to a private WhatsApp or Discord community of premium smartwatch users. **Peer reassurance** from satisfied owners reduces cognitive dissonance. *(2 marks)*

3. **Day 30 — Personalised Health Report:** Push a personalised "Your First 30 Days" health insights report, showing tangible value derived from the product. **Demonstrates realised utility** and shifts the consumer's frame from price to value. *(2 marks)*

4. **Day 60 — Feedback Survey + Surprise Loyalty Reward:** Collect Voice of Customer (VoC) data and instantly reward completion with loyalty points. **Closes the feedback loop** and gives the brand intelligence to fix Stage 1–3 expectation setting. *(1 mark)*

**Expected Outcome:** The campaign converts a passive buyer into an active advocate, raising the brand's NPS by an estimated 15–25 points within one quarter.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**
*(a)* Compare Kotler's Five-Stage Model with the Engel-Kollat-Blackwell (EKB) Model. Highlight **three key differences** in terms of structure, feedback, and divestment handling. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Apply the Purchase Decision Process to a student purchasing a laptop for engineering college. Identify which stages will take the longest and design **one IMC intervention per stage** optimised for a student demographic. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Comparison Kotler vs EKB (7 marks):**

| Dimension | Kotler's 5-Stage Model | EKB Model |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Number of Stages** | 5 stages | 7 stages (extends Kotler) |
| **Information Processing** | Treated implicitly within Stage 2 | Explicitly broken into Internal vs. External search with separate processing modules |
| **Feedback Loops** | Implicit; mentioned only between Stage 5 and Stage 1 | Explicit feedback arrows between Consumption, Evaluation, and the next purchase decision |
| **Divestment Stage** | Not included | Stage 7 explicitly addresses product disposal / resale / recycling |
| **Theoretical Origin** | Kotler's Marketing Management textbook | Engel, Kollat and Blackwell's Consumer Behaviour textbook (1968) |

**Three Key Differences (3 marks):**
1. **Granularity:** EKB splits information processing into discrete cognitive sub-steps, whereas Kotler groups them into one stage.
2. **Feedback:** EKB has explicit, named feedback loops; Kotler only implies them.
3. **Divestment:** EKB adds a 7th stage for product disposal — important for sustainability-marketing contexts (very relevant for KTU's 2024 sustainability-aligned syllabus).

**Part (b) — Application to a Student Buying a Laptop (7 marks):**

| Stage | Expected Duration for Student | IMC Intervention Optimised for Students |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. Need Recognition | 1–2 weeks (triggered by college admission letter) | YouTube Shorts + Reels showing "Day 1 of Engineering College" featuring laptops |
| 2. Information Search | 2–4 weeks (intensive — long stage) | Reddit AMAs, YouTube tech-review channels, college WhatsApp groups |
| 3. Evaluation of Alternatives | 1–2 weeks (comparing specs, price, brand) | Influencer comparison videos, "Best Laptops Under ₹60,000" SEO blog |
| 4. Purchase Decision | 1–3 days (decision-ready, high involvement) | Student-Discount Banner on Amazon/Flipkart + EMI option highlighted |
| 5. Post-Purchase Behaviour | 30+ days (high dissonance due to price) | "Setup Your Engineering Laptop" email series + 1-year extended warranty offer |

**Stage Expected to Take Longest: Stage 2 (Information Search)** — because students have low prior knowledge, rely heavily on peer reviews, and conduct extensive online research.

**Total: 7 marks distributed across table (4 marks) and explanation of longest stage + rationale (3 marks).**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Writing the stages but not linking them to communication strategy** — This is the most common pitfall. Always pair each stage with at least one IMC tool.
> 2. **Confusing "Need" with "Want"** — Need is essential; Want is desire-driven. Examiners will deduct marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the feedback loop from Stage 5 to Stage 1** — Many students present the 5 stages as a linear pipeline, losing depth marks.
> 4. **Omitting Cognitive Dissonance** in post-purchase discussions — It is a high-frequency favourite question.
> 5. **Ignoring situational factors** in Stage 4 (e.g., stock-out, peer influence at the last minute) — Examiners expect you to mention at least two disrupting factors.

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Core Model:** Kotler's 5-Stage Model — Need Recognition → Information Search → Evaluation of Alternatives → Purchase Decision → Post-Purchase Behaviour.
- **Extended Model:** EKB (7 stages) — adds a Divestment stage; KTU 2024 syllabus expects awareness.
- **Two Stimulus Types:** Internal (from within) and External (from outside environment) — both trigger Stage 1.
- **Two Search Types:** Internal (memory) and External (peers, media, online) — both fuel Stage 2.
- **Evoked Set Size:** Typically limited to **3–5 brands** — marketers fight for inclusion here.
- **Multi-Attribute Utility Model:** $U(B_i) = \sum w_j \cdot x_{ij}$ — consumer evaluates brands on weighted attributes.
- **Oliver's Expectation-Confirmation Theory:** $CL = \text{Performance} - \text{Expectation}$ — negative $CL$ causes dissatisfaction.
- **Cognitive Dissonance Formula:** $CDI = \vert Importance \times \vert Decidedness - Closeness \vert \vert$ — higher CDI demands more reassurance communication.
- **Four Influencing Factor Categories:** Cultural, Social, Personal, Psychological — frame all discussion answers with these.
- **IMC Channel Mapping 2024:** YouTube/Instagram (Stage 1) → SEO/Influencers (Stage 2) → Comparison Pages (Stage 3) → Retargeting/POP (Stage 4) → Onboarding/Loyalty (Stage 5).
- **Feedback Loop:** Stage 5 outcome modifies Stage 1 in the *next* purchase cycle — *never* present the model as linear.
- **High-Involvement Purchases** (cars, laptops, houses) follow the full 5 stages; **Low-Involvement Purchases** (milk, salt) often skip Stages 2 and 3.
- **Examiner's Favourite Quote:** *"Marketing communication does not create needs; it activates latent needs and channels them toward the brand."* — Use this line for impact.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hierarchy of Needs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT742 - MARKETING COMMUNICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hierarchy of Needs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
The **Hierarchy of Needs** is a psychological motivational theory proposed by American psychologist **Abraham H. Maslow** in his 1943 paper *"A Theory of Human Motivation"* (published in *Psychological Review*, Vol. 50). It is structured as a five-tier pyramid model of human needs, arranged in a hierarchy of prepotency, where needs at the lower levels must be substantially satisfied before an individual is motivated to pursue needs at the next higher level.

In the discipline of **Marketing Communications (PECBT742)**, the Hierarchy of Needs is operationalized as a **consumer behavior diagnostic framework** that helps communication strategists, brand managers, and creative directors understand the *underlying motivational driver* of purchase decisions, enabling the design of value propositions, advertising appeals, and integrated marketing communication (IMC) campaigns that resonate with the target audience's dominant unmet need.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Ladder of Human Motivation"**
>
> Imagine a person climbing a ladder. They cannot reach the 3rd rung until they have firmly planted their foot on the 1st and 2nd rungs. Similarly, a hungry person (Physiological need) will not be motivated to seek admiration on Instagram (Esteem need). They will first look for food.
>
> **In marketing terms:** A luxury car advertisement focusing on "prestige and status" (Esteem appeal) will fail in a market where buyers are worried about their next meal (Physiological appeal needed). The communication must "speak the language" of the dominant unmet need.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Within Module 2 of Marketing Communications, the Hierarchy of Needs is studied as a foundational model of *consumer motivation*, directly feeding into the construction of the **Consumer Decision Journey** and the **AIDA + Maslow integration model** used in advertising copywriting.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants
| Constant / Metric | Value / Standard | Application Context |
|---|---|---|
| Number of tiers in original model | **5 Levels** | Standard psychological framework |
| Extended levels (later work by Maslow, 1969–1971) | **8 Levels** | Includes cognitive, aesthetic, transcendence |
| Hierarchical principle | **Prepotency** | Lower need precedes higher need motivationally |
| Dominant driver in IMC | **Unmet Need Identification** | Basis of appeal selection |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Levels — Exhaustive Theoretical Breakdown

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The pyramid moves from **deficit needs (D-needs)** at the bottom to **being needs (B-needs)** at the top. Lower-level needs are *deficiency-driven* (their absence causes tension); higher-level needs are *growth-driven* (their pursuit yields fulfillment).

#### **Level 1 — Physiological Needs**
- **What:** Survival fundamentals — air, water, food, shelter, sleep, clothing, reproduction.
- **Why it matters in Marketing Comm:** These are the most primitive and universally powerful motivators. They are the *default driver* in FMCG (Fast-Moving Consumer Goods), food & beverage, housing, and pharmaceutical marketing.
- **How it works:** Ads targeting this level use appeals of *urgency, relief, satisfaction, sustenance*. E.g., a Maggi ad showing steaming hot noodles satisfies a hunger appeal.
- **Communication Tonality:** Direct, visceral, functional, benefit-led.

#### **Level 2 — Safety Needs**
- **What:** Personal security, financial security, health, employment, property, stability, order, law.
- **Why it matters in Marketing Comm:** Drives the entire **insurance, banking, security systems, healthcare, automotive (ABS/airbags) sectors.** A consumer who feels economically insecure will not buy luxury — they will buy assurance.
- **How it works:** Communications emphasize *trust, protection, warranty, stability, "we've got you covered"*.
- **Communication Tonality:** Reassuring, factual, evidence-based, risk-removal.

#### **Level 3 — Love and Belonging (Social Needs)**
- **What:** Friendship, family, intimacy, affection, romantic relationships, social groups, community acceptance.
- **Why it matters in Marketing Comm:** Forms the bedrock of *community-driven brands* (e.g., Coca-Cola's "Open Happiness," telecom brands, social media platforms). Drives word-of-mouth and brand communities.
- **How it works:** Ads leverage *tribal identity, togetherness, "join us," shared moments*.
- **Communication Tonality:** Emotional, warm, inclusive, "you belong here."

#### **Level 4 — Esteem Needs**
- **What:** Self-respect, self-confidence, achievement, recognition, status, respect of others, power, prestige.
- **Why it matters in Marketing Comm:** The *engine of the premium and luxury market* — watches, automobiles, fashion, real estate, education, B-school branding. Two sub-categories: **lower esteem** (self-respect, independence) and **higher esteem** (status, prestige).
- **How it works:** Communications showcase *aspiration, success, achievement, recognition, "you deserve this"*.
- **Communication Tonality:** Aspirational, empowering, prestige-laden, exclusive.

#### **Level 5 — Self-Actualization**
- **What:** Realizing personal potential, self-fulfillment, personal growth, peak experiences, morality, creativity, problem-solving, acceptance of facts.
- **Why it matters in Marketing Comm:** The *ultimate differentiator* in saturated markets where competitors already satisfy Levels 1–4. Brands like Apple ("Think Different"), Nike ("Just Do It"), and Harley-Davidson sell self-actualization narratives.
- **How it works:** Communications focus on *transformation, empowerment, unlocking potential, "be the best version of yourself"*.
- **Communication Tonality:** Inspirational, philosophical, identity-affirming.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Conceptual Cheat Sheet

| Level | Need Type | Dominant Emotion | Product Categories | Communication Appeal | Sample Brand Tagline |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Physiological (D) | Hunger, thirst, comfort | Food, water, shelter, pharma | Survival, relief | *"Have a Break, Have a Kit Kat"* |
| 2 | Safety (D) | Fear, insecurity | Insurance, banking, healthcare | Security, trust | *"With you, always"* (HDFC Life) |
| 3 | Social (D) | Loneliness, rejection | Telecom, beverages, fashion | Belonging, community | *"Tum hi ho bandhu"* (Coca-Cola) |
| 4 | Esteem (D) | Inferiority, low status | Luxury cars, watches, B-schools | Status, achievement | *"The Best or Nothing"* (Mercedes) |
| 5 | Self-Actualization (B) | Stagnation, unrealized self | Tech, adventure, education | Growth, purpose | *"Think Different"* (Apple) |

> [!NOTE]
> **D = Deficiency Needs (lower 4 layers), B = Being Needs (top layer).** This distinction is a high-frequency KTU exam point.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering / Management Fields
- **Segmentation Strategy:** Marketers segment the market by identifying which level of need a target consumer is currently pursuing.
- **Positioning Strategy:** A product's positioning statement is anchored to a specific level (e.g., Volvo = Level 2, BMW = Level 4).
- **Creative Brief Development:** The "single most powerful benefit" in a creative brief is often mapped to a Maslow layer.
- **Crisis Communication:** During recessions, brands shift messaging from Esteem/Self-Actualization back to Safety/Physiological.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Application Framework & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note to Students:** Since "Hierarchy of Needs" is a behavioral/managerial model rather than a mathematical theorem, the "derivation" below is the **logical construction of the marketing application framework** — i.e., the systematic process of converting the theory into a MarComm strategy. Every step is shown explicitly per V10 protocol.

### 3.1 The Five-Step Maslow-to-MarComm Translation Framework

#### **Step 1: Diagnose the Consumer's Dominant Unmet Need**
$$N_{\text{dominant}} = \arg\max_{i} \left( \text{Deficit}_i \right)$$

*Logic:* Identify which level $i$ (1 through 5) has the *largest deficit* (gap between desired state and actual state) in the consumer's mind. This becomes the **dominant unmet need** $N_{\text{dominant}}$.

*Textual explanation:* A fresh engineering graduate in Kerala earning ₹30,000/month has minimal deficit at Level 1 (food is sorted) but a **maximum deficit at Level 4** (status, recognition) — therefore, his dominant unmet need is *Esteem*.

#### **Step 2: Map the Need to a Product/Service Value Proposition**
$$V_{\text{proposed}} = f(N_{\text{dominant}})$$

*Logic:* Design a value proposition $V_{\text{proposed}}$ that directly addresses the dominant need identified in Step 1.

*Textual explanation:* For the graduate above, the value proposition is *"A premium smartphone that signals success"* — a product fulfilling the esteem deficit, not a basic keypad phone (Level 1 utility).

#### **Step 3: Select the Communication Appeal**
$$A_{\text{appeal}} \in \left\{ A_{\text{survival}}, A_{\text{security}}, A_{\text{belonging}}, A_{\text{prestige}}, A_{\text{growth}} \right\}$$

*Logic:* The creative appeal $A_{\text{appeal}}$ is selected from the five-tier appeal set, where each appeal corresponds to a Maslow level. The chosen appeal must match the level of $N_{\text{dominant}}$.

*Textual explanation:* For the smartphone case, $A_{\text{appeal}} = A_{\text{prestige}}$ — the ad will show the product in aspirational settings, with successful, recognized users.

#### **Step 4: Construct the Message Tone and Channel**
$$M_{\text{message}} = \left( A_{\text{appeal}}, \; T_{\text{tone}}, \; C_{\text{channel}} \right)$$

*Logic:* The final message $M_{\text{message}}$ is a tuple of three vectors — appeal, tone, and channel. The tone $T_{\text{tone}}$ is calibrated to the emotional register of the need (e.g., fear for safety, love for social, pride for esteem). The channel $C_{\text{channel}}$ is selected based on where the target consumer seeks need-fulfillment cues (e.g., Instagram for esteem, YouTube for self-actualization).

*Textual explanation:* For the smartphone: $T_{\text{tone}} = \text{confident, aspirational}$, $C_{\text{channel}} = \text{Instagram Reels + YouTube tech-reviewers}$.

#### **Step 5: Measure the Communication Effectiveness**
$$E_{\text{eff}} = \frac{\Delta N_{\text{perceived}} + \Delta B_{\text{brand}}}{C_{\text{communicative effort}}}$$

*Logic:* Effectiveness $E_{\text{eff}}$ is the ratio of (perceived need-reduction $\Delta N_{\text{perceived}}$ plus brand affinity gain $\Delta B_{\text{brand}}$) to the communicative effort expended (budget, time, impressions). A campaign is deemed effective if $E_{\text{eff}} \geq 1$.

*Textual explanation:* After running the campaign for 3 months, the brand measures: (a) did consumers report reduced need-deficit? (b) did brand affinity scores rise? (c) was the cost justified? If yes to all three, the campaign succeeded.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Industries to Maslow Levels

| Industry / Sector | Dominant Maslow Level | Communication Hook | Real Brand Example |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---|
| FMCG / Food Delivery | Level 1 (Physiological) | *"Satisfy your hunger in 30 mins"* | Swiggy, Zomato |
| Insurance / Banking | Level 2 (Safety) | *"Your family is protected"* | LIC, SBI Life |
| Telecom / Beverages | Level 3 (Social) | *"Stay connected with loved ones"* | Airtel, Coca-Cola |
| Luxury / Premium Auto | Level 4 (Esteem) | *"The mark of achievement"* | Mercedes-Benz, Rolex |
| Tech / Education / Adventure | Level 5 (Self-Actualization) | *"Unlock your true potential"* | Apple, NIIT, Royal Enfield |

### 3.3 Critical Assumptions and Limitations (Mandatory KTU Discussion Point)

> [!WARNING]
> The model assumes a *strict, universal hierarchical order*. In practice, **cultural, generational, and individual variations** exist. KTU examiners expect students to critique the model — e.g., Indian students often prioritize *family security (Level 2/3)* over *individual self-actualization (Level 5)*, and artists may pursue self-actualization despite unmet physiological needs. Always state these limitations in 14-mark answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs with Marketing Communication Mapping

```mermaid
graph TD
    L5["LEVEL 5: SELF ACTUALIZATION"] --> L4["LEVEL 4: ESTEEM NEEDS"]
    L4 --> L3["LEVEL 3: LOVE AND BELONGING"]
    L3 --> L2["LEVEL 2: SAFETY NEEDS"]
    L2 --> L1["LEVEL 1: PHYSIOLOGICAL NEEDS"]

    L1a["Food Water Shelter Sleep"] -.-> L1
    L2a["Security Health Stability Employment"] -.-> L2
    L3a["Family Friends Relationships Community"] -.-> L3
    L4a["Status Recognition Achievement Respect"] -.-> L4
    L5a["Personal Growth Purpose Creativity Fulfillment"] -.-> L5

    L1 --> C1["MARCOM APPEAL: SURVIVAL AND RELIEF"]
    L2 --> C2["MARCOM APPEAL: SECURITY AND TRUST"]
    L3 --> C3["MARCOM APPEAL: BELONGING AND TOGETHERNESS"]
    L4 --> C4["MARCOM APPEAL: STATUS AND PRESTIGE"]
    L5 --> C5["MARCOM APPEAL: GROWTH AND PURPOSE"]

    C1 --> EX1["Example: Swiggy Maggi 2 Min Noodles"]
    C2 --> EX2["Example: LIC Jeevan Anand Policy"]
    C3 --> EX3["Example: Airtel Family Recharge Plan"]
    C4 --> EX4["Example: Mercedes Benz S Class"]
    C5 --> EX5["Example: Apple Think Different Campaign"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — MarComm Translation Flowchart

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Step 1: Identify Dominant Unmet Need"] --> B["Step 2: Map Need to Value Proposition"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Select Communication Appeal"]
    C --> D["Step 4: Construct Message and Channel"]
    D --> E["Step 5: Measure Effectiveness"]

    E --> F{"Effective? E greater than 1?"}
    F -- Yes --> G["Campaign Validated: Scale It"]
    F -- No --> H["Return to Step 1: Re-diagnose Need"]

    A --- A1["Survival Security Social Esteem Growth"]
    C --- C1["Functional Emotional Aspiration Inspirational"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Hierarchy as a Decision Filter for Creative Strategy

```mermaid
graph TD
    START["New MarComm Campaign Brief"] --> Q1{"Which level of Maslow is the target consumer operating at?"}

    Q1 -- Level 1 --> R1["Use Functional Benefit Led Copy with Sensory Cues"]
    Q1 -- Level 2 --> R2["Use Rational Reassurance with Trust Badges"]
    Q1 -- Level 3 --> R3["Use Emotional Storytelling with Group Identity"]
    Q1 -- Level 4 --> R4["Use Aspirational Imagery with Status Symbols"]
    Q1 -- Level 5 --> R5["Use Purpose Driven Narrative with Transformation Arc"]

    R1 --> OUT["Final Creative Output"]
    R2 --> OUT
    R3 --> OUT
    R4 --> OUT
    R5 --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> These schematics are designed for **Mermaid v10+ compatibility**. The node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `L1`, `C1`, `EX1`) and all labels are in plain uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting, per the V10 compilation safeguard.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs. List its five levels in ascending order.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs, proposed by Abraham Maslow in 1943, is a motivational theory that arranges human needs in a five-tier pyramid where lower-level deficiency needs must be substantially satisfied before higher-level growth needs emerge as motivators. The five levels in ascending order are:
1. Physiological Needs (food, water, shelter)
2. Safety Needs (security, stability)
3. Love and Belonging Needs (family, relationships)
4. Esteem Needs (status, recognition)
5. Self-Actualization (personal growth, fulfillment)
*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the five levels.)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between 'Deficit Needs' and 'Being Needs' in Maslow's theory. Give one example of each in a marketing context.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Deficit Needs (D-Needs):** The lower four levels (Physiological, Safety, Social, Esteem). Their absence creates tension, and their satisfaction removes the tension. *Marketing Example:* Hunger relief messaging by Zomato (Physiological).
- **Being Needs (B-Needs):** The top level (Self-Actualization). Their pursuit is growth-driven, not tension-driven. *Marketing Example:* Apple's "Think Different" campaign encouraging personal creativity.
*(2 marks for distinction, 1 mark for examples.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs in detail. How does the concept of *prepotency* govern the transition from one level to the next?

**Model Answer:**
Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs is a five-tier motivational model proposed by Abraham Maslow in 1943. The five levels, from base to apex, are: Physiological, Safety, Love/Belonging, Esteem, and Self-Actualization. The base four are *deficit needs*; the apex is a *being need*. The hierarchy operates on the principle of **prepotency** — the idea that a need becomes a dominant motivator *only* when the need below it is substantially (not necessarily fully) satisfied. For example, a person facing job loss (Safety deficit activated) is unlikely to pursue prestige (Esteem), no matter how much they desired it earlier. Prepotency ensures motivational energy flows upward in a generally sequential manner, though Maslow himself acknowledged exceptions (e.g., artists, monks, idealists who sacrifice lower needs for higher ones). The model assumes human motivation is *systematic, layered, and progressive*. *(7 marks: 2 marks for levels, 3 marks for prepotency explanation with example, 2 marks for exceptions and conclusion.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Critically analyze the application of Maslow's Hierarchy in designing marketing communication campaigns. Use **two real-world brand examples** to illustrate your answer.

**Model Answer:**
Maslow's Hierarchy serves as a powerful *diagnostic tool* in marketing communication (MarComm) by helping strategists identify the *dominant unmet need* of a target segment and align the messaging, tone, and channel accordingly.

**Example 1 — Zomato (Level 1 Appeal):** Zomato's marketing communication consistently targets the *Physiological need* for food. Its ad copies ("Khana khao, life jeeyo"), bright visuals of hot food, and 30-minute delivery promise all reinforce hunger relief. The tonality is *urgent, sensory, and functional*. This works because a major segment of its user base has an unmet hunger need and seeks instant gratification.

**Example 2 — Mercedes-Benz (Level 4 Appeal):** Mercedes targets the *Esteem need* — status, achievement, and prestige. The brand's iconic tagline *"The Best or Nothing"* positions its cars as symbols of accomplishment. Visuals feature successful professionals, luxury settings, and engineering excellence. The tonality is *aspirational, exclusive, and confident*. This works because the target buyer has satisfied lower-order needs and now seeks social recognition.

**Critical Analysis:** While the model offers a clean framework, its limitations include: (i) cultural variations (collectivist cultures like India may prioritize family/social needs over individual self-actualization), (ii) the rigid hierarchy is often violated in real life, and (iii) modern consumers operate at *multiple levels simultaneously* (a single ad can mix safety + esteem appeals). Nonetheless, it remains a foundational strategic tool in IMC planning. *(7 marks: 2 marks for framework explanation, 3 marks for two real-world examples, 2 marks for critical analysis.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** "Maslow's Hierarchy is more relevant in MarComm strategy formulation than in MarComm creative execution." Discuss with suitable examples.

**Model Answer:**
The statement holds partial truth. **In strategy formulation**, Maslow's Hierarchy is highly relevant because it helps marketers *segment, target, and position* a brand based on the dominant unmet need of the consumer. For instance, an insurance company segments customers by their *Safety need awareness* and positions its policies accordingly.

However, **in creative execution**, the model is less directly applicable because creative work often transcends a single need-level. Modern advertising frequently *layers multiple needs* in a single narrative. For example, a Surf Excel ad ("Daag acche hain") blends *Social belonging* (community acceptance of stains) with *Esteem* (child's confidence) and even *Self-actualization* (parent's pride in raising a good human). The creative team uses emotional storytelling rather than a rigid need-category.

Thus, Maslow's Hierarchy is *strategically foundational but creatively inspirational* — a guiding beacon, not a creative cage. *(7 marks: 3 marks for strategy relevance, 3 marks for creative limitations, 1 mark for balanced conclusion.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design a MarComm campaign brief for a **new electric scooter brand targeting young Indian professionals**, using Maslow's Hierarchy as your strategic foundation.

**Model Answer:**

**Campaign Brief:**

- **Target Audience:** Urban Indian professionals, aged 22–32, earning ₹5–12 LPA.
- **Dominant Unmet Need (Maslow Level 3 + Level 4):** *Social belonging* (peer community of eco-conscious riders) + *Esteem* (status as a smart, modern professional).
- **Value Proposition:** *"Ride Smart. Ride Proud. Ride the Change."*
- **Communication Appeal:** Hybrid Social-Esteem appeal — emphasizing both community and personal pride.
- **Tone:** Confident, energetic, modern, slightly rebellious.
- **Channels:** Instagram Reels, YouTube tech-reviewers, LinkedIn thought-leadership content, urban OOH (bus shelters, metro stations).
- **Key Message:**
  1. Primary: *"Join the new generation of riders shaping India's clean future."*
  2. Secondary: *"Be seen. Be smart. Be the change."*
- **Hashtag Campaign:** #RideTheChange
- **KPI Targets:** 5M+ impressions, 20% brand recall lift, 15% test-ride bookings.
- **Maslow Mapping Justification:** The brief explicitly targets Levels 3 and 4 because the audience has satisfied Levels 1 and 2 (stable jobs, basic needs met) and is now seeking peer identity and personal prestige — making them the *strategic sweet spot* for an aspirational yet community-driven EV brand.
*(7 marks: full credit for explicit Maslow mapping, 2-tier appeal justification, channel-tonality alignment, and measurable KPIs.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Listing levels without explaining prepotency** → 2–3 marks lost. Always define *prepotency* when asked to "explain the hierarchy."
> 2. **No real-world brand example in 14-mark answers** → 2–3 marks deducted. Always cite at least *two* Indian or global brand campaigns.
> 3. **Failing to critique the model** → 1–2 marks lost. Examiners expect at least one line on limitations (cultural variation, hierarchy violation).
> 4. **Conflating AIDA with Maslow** → Keep them distinct: AIDA is a *communication process model*; Maslow is a *consumer motivation model*.
> 5. **Writing generic "ads use emotional appeal"** without tying it to a *specific Maslow level* — loses precision marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Summary:**

- ☐ **Originator:** Abraham H. Maslow, 1943 paper *"A Theory of Human Motivation"* — NOT a marketing theorist, but a psychologist whose work was adopted by marketers.
- ☐ **Five Levels (Base → Apex):** Physiological → Safety → Love/Belonging → Esteem → Self-Actualization.
- ☐ **Key Concept — Prepotency:** A higher-level need becomes a motivator *only after* the lower-level need is *substantially* satisfied.
- ☐ **Two Categories:** Lower 4 = **Deficit Needs (D-needs)**; Top 1 = **Being Needs (B-needs)**.
- ☐ **MarComm Use:** Foundation for *segmentation, targeting, positioning (STP)*, and *creative appeal selection*.
- ☐ **5-Step Translation Framework:** Diagnose → Map → Appeal → Message → Measure.
- ☐ **Limitation to State in Answers:** Cultural variability, hierarchy not always strict, multiple needs active simultaneously.
- ☐ **Sample Brands to Memorize:** Zomato (L1), LIC (L2), Airtel (L3), Mercedes (L4), Apple (L5).
- ☐ **High-Yield Phrases:** "Hierarchy of prepotency," "deficit-driven vs. growth-driven needs," "needs-based segmentation," "aspirational appeal."
- ☐ **Always pair Maslow with one Indian brand example** — examiners reward cultural-contextualization.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wants and Buying Motives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT742 - MARKETING COMMUNICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wants and Buying Motives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition — Want**
> A *want* is an unfulfilled human desire for a specific satisfier (product, service, or experience) that arises once basic **needs** have been recognized. Wants are shaped by an individual's personality, culture, society, reference groups, and personal experiences. They represent the *form* that human needs take as they are moulded by culture and individual upbringing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition — Buying Motive**
> A *buying motive* (also termed a *purchase motive* or *consumer motive*) is an internal psychological drive, impulse, or need that compels a consumer to take action and purchase a particular product or service. It is the *reason why* a consumer behaves in a specific way during the decision-making process, functioning as the link between a *need* (the underlying state of deprivation) and a *want* (the directed desire toward a specific satisfier).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Ice Cream Van Analogy**
> Imagine a hot summer afternoon. You feel thirsty. The *need* is "thirst relief" — a universal biological state shared by all humans. But the *want* is highly personal: you crave a **chocolate sundae**, not water, not lime soda. Why? Because of your childhood memories, your cultural upbringing, an advertisement you saw yesterday, and the social setting.
> The *buying motive* is the **internal push** that drives your hand to reach for your wallet — perhaps comfort, indulgence, social approval, or simply the desire to cool down.
> In short: **Need = the gap**, **Want = the chosen solution**, **Buying Motive = the engine that moves you from gap to solution.**

## 1.3 Key Distinctions (KTU Board Standard)

| Term | Nature | Scope | Example |
|------|--------|-------|---------|
| **Need** | Biogenic / Psychogenic | Universal | Hunger, Shelter, Safety |
| **Want** | Cultural / Individual | Specific | Biryani over Rice, SUV over Sedan |
| **Demand** | Want + Purchasing Power | Economic | Willingness + Ability to pay |
| **Buying Motive** | Psychological Driver | Internal Push | Status, Comfort, Fear, Pride |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric Highlighted**
> In KTU 2024 Scheme Marketing Communications, the textbook classification places *motive* at the heart of the **AIDA model (Attention → Interest → Desire → Action)**. Motives drive the *Desire* and *Action* stages. **Marketers must identify dominant buying motives** to craft messages that resonate.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown — Wants

### A. The Hierarchy of Wants (Based on Maslow's Need Theory)
Human wants are not random; they are structured and emerge in a layered fashion:

- **Physiological Wants** → Food, water, sleep, shelter.
- **Safety Wants** → Security, protection, stability, financial safety.
- **Social Wants** → Belonging, love, friendship, acceptance.
- **Esteem Wants** → Recognition, status, respect, self-confidence.
- **Self-Actualization Wants** → Personal growth, fulfilment, creativity.

### B. Types of Wants (KTU Syllabus Mandate)

1. **Biogenic Wants** — Biological, inborn (food, drink, rest).
2. **Psychogenic Wants** — Learned through experience and environment (status, prestige, love).
3. **Utilitarian Wants** — Functional, practical (durability, efficiency).
4. **Hedonic Wants** — Pleasure, sensory enjoyment (luxury, indulgence).
5. **Latent Wants** — Unrecognized by the consumer; triggered by innovation (smartphone before its invention).
6. **Manifest Wants** — Clearly recognized and expressed wants.

## 2.2 Theoretical Breakdown — Buying Motives

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Motive Equation (Conceptual)**
> $$\text{Buying Behaviour} = f(\text{Needs} + \text{Wants} + \text{Motives} + \text{Perceived Value})$$
> In marketing communications, *motives* act as the **catalyst** that transforms passive desire into active purchase.

### A. Classification of Buying Motives (KTU High-Yield Framework)

#### I. Product Motives (Rational + Emotional)
- **Rational Product Motives**
  - Economy / Price (cost-saving)
  - Durability (long-lasting)
  - Efficiency (time/energy saving)
  - Dependability / Reliability
  - Convenience / Utility

- **Emotional Product Motives**
  - Pride of ownership
  - Pleasure / Comfort
  - Fear of scarcity
  - Imitation / Trend following
  - Aesthetic appeal (beauty, design)

#### II. Patronage Motives (Rational + Emotional)
- **Rational Patronage Motives**
  - Price competitiveness
  - After-sales service
  - Location convenience
  - Product variety / assortment
  - Return/exchange policy

- **Emotional Patronage Motives**
  - Brand loyalty / Trust
  - Habit / Custom
  - Social approval
  - Personal rapport with seller
  - Store atmosphere

### B. The Rational vs. Emotional Motive Dichotomy

> [!NOTE]
> **The 80/20 Rule of Consumer Decision-Making (Industry Heuristic)**
> Approximately **80% of consumer purchase decisions** are driven by *emotional motives*, while only **20%** are justified by *rational* logic. However, **rational motives dominate high-involvement purchases** (cars, houses, insurance) while **emotional motives dominate low-involvement purchases** (snacks, cosmetics, fashion).

## 2.3 High-Yield Formula Sheet / Conceptual Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Classification | Marketing Lever | Typical Communication Tone |
|---------|----------------|-----------------|----------------------------|
| Need | Biogenic / Psychogenic | Identify & Address | Functional |
| Want | Manifest / Latent | Activate | Aspirational |
| Rational Motive | Logical, Economic | Feature-benefit copy | Informative |
| Emotional Motive | Psychological, Sensory | Storytelling, imagery | Aspirational |
| Product Motive | Linked to item | USP emphasis | Performance |
| Patronage Motive | Linked to brand/store | Loyalty programs | Relational |
| Positive Motive | Pulls toward purchase | Reward framing | "You deserve it" |
| Negative Motive | Pushes away from non-purchase | Fear appeals | "Don't miss out" |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Alert:** In KTU valuation, students often confuse *wants* with *motivations*. Remember: **wants are what the consumer desires; motives are why they desire it.**

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Marketing Communications

- **Copywriting**: Headlines are designed to appeal to **dominant buying motives** (e.g., "Save 50%" = rational; "Be the envy of your friends" = emotional).
- **Segmentation**: Markets are segmented by **motive clusters** (status-seekers, bargain-hunters, convenience-seekers).
- **Brand Positioning**: Positioning statements map products against **emotional vs. rational axes** to occupy a unique mental slot.
- **Media Planning**: Emotional motive appeals → *visual-heavy* media (Instagram, TV); Rational appeals → *detail-heavy* media (brochures, comparison sites).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Framework

## 3.1 The Motive-to-Action Conversion Process (KTU Analytical Model)

> [!NOTE]
> **Purpose:** To systematically map how a buying motive travels from psychological state to market transaction — the exact logical sequence tested in KTU University Exams.

### Step 1 — Recognition of Need
$$\text{Stimulus (Internal/External)} \rightarrow \text{Need Awareness}$$

*Logic:* A consumer perceives a gap between their current state and desired state. **Example:** A working professional feels fatigued after long commute → recognizes need for relaxation.

### Step 2 — Want Formation
$$\text{Need} + \text{Cultural Filter} + \text{Personal Experience} \rightarrow \text{Specific Want}$$

*Logic:* The need takes a culturally-shaped form. **Example:** The professional doesn't just want "rest" — they want a *weekend resort package in Wayanad*.

### Step 3 — Motive Activation
$$\text{Want} + \text{Motive Trigger} \rightarrow \text{Purchase Intent}$$

*Logic:* A motive (emotional or rational) energizes the want. **Example:** Emotional motive: *"I deserve this break"* + Rational motive: *"This package is 30% off"*.

### Step 4 — Information Search
$$\text{Motive Strength} \rightarrow \text{Search Intensity}$$

*Logic:* Stronger motives drive deeper search. **Example:** Comparing three resorts on MakeMyTrip, reading reviews.

### Step 5 — Evaluation of Alternatives
$$\text{Attributes} \times \text{Motive Weight} = \text{Perceived Value}$$

*Logic:* Each alternative is scored against the dominant motive.

### Step 6 — Purchase Decision
$$\text{Perceived Value} > \text{Threshold} \rightarrow \text{Action}$$

### Step 7 — Post-Purchase Behaviour
$$\text{Expectation vs. Reality} \rightarrow \text{Satisfaction/Dissonance}$$

*Logic:* Future motives are shaped by this outcome.

## 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Rational vs. Emotional Motives

| Parameter | Rational Motives | Emotional Motives |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Origin** | Logic, intellect, calculation | Feelings, instincts, senses |
| **Decision Speed** | Slower, deliberate | Faster, impulsive |
| **Involvement Level** | High | Low to medium |
| **Communication Appeal** | Facts, data, ROI | Stories, imagery, music |
| **Justification** | Easy to verbalize | Often subconscious |
| **KTU Typical Example** | "This AC saves 30% electricity" | "Feel the luxury, own the pride" |
| **Loyalty Outcome** | Switchable on price | Deeply rooted |

## 3.3 Detailed Classification Tree (Step-by-Step)

```
BUYING MOTIVES
│
├── 1. PRODUCT MOTIVES
│   │
│   ├── A. Rational Product Motives
│   │   ├── i. Economy (Price, Value for Money)
│   │   ├── ii. Durability (Longevity)
│   │   ├── iii. Efficiency (Performance)
│   │   ├── iv. Dependability (Trust)
│   │   └── v. Convenience (Ease of Use)
│   │
│   └── B. Emotional Product Motives
│       ├── i. Pride of Ownership
│       ├── ii. Comfort & Pleasure
│       ├── iii. Imitation (Bandwagon Effect)
│       ├── iv. Distinctiveness (Snob Effect)
│       └── v. Aesthetic Appeal
│
└── 2. PATRONAGE MOTIVES
    │
    ├── A. Rational Patronage Motives
    │   ├── i. Price Competitiveness
    │   ├── ii. Service Quality
    │   ├── iii. Variety & Assortment
    │   ├── iv. Location Convenience
    │   └── v. Return Policy
    │
    └── B. Emotional Patronage Motives
        ├── i. Brand Loyalty
        ├── ii. Habit / Custom
        ├── iii. Social Approval
        ├── iv. Personal Rapport
        └── v. Store Atmosphere
```

## 3.4 The Positive vs. Negative Motive Framework

### Positive Motives (Approach-Driven)
- Promise of reward, pleasure, gain, or status.
- Communication: "Achieve more with..." / "Enjoy the pride of..."
- Examples: Aspirational advertising (luxury cars, premium watches).

### Negative Motives (Avoidance-Driven)
- Fear, anxiety, loss aversion, social embarrassment.
- Communication: "Don't let your family suffer..." / "Avoid the risk of..."
- Examples: Insurance ads, health products, security systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Most KTU questions on this topic require students to **identify, classify, and provide examples** for each type. Memorize the 5 sub-categories under each branch to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Need-Want-Motive-Action Causal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stimulus Internal or External] --> B[Need Recognition]
    B --> C[Cultural and Personal Filter]
    C --> D[Want Formation Specific Desired Object]
    D --> E[Buying Motive Activation Rational or Emotional]
    E --> F[Information Search]
    F --> G[Evaluation of Alternatives]
    G --> H{Purchase Decision}
    H --> I[Post Purchase Outcome Satisfaction or Dissonance]
    I --> J[Future Motive Reinforcement]
```

## 4.2 Maslow's Hierarchy Mapped to Wants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M5[Self Actualization Personal Growth and Fulfilment]
    M4[Esteem Recognition and Status]
    M3[Social Belonging and Love]
    M2[Safety Security and Stability]
    M1[Physiological Food Water Shelter]
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> M4
    M4 --> M5
```

## 4.3 Rational vs. Emotional Motive Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RM[Buying Motives]
    RM --> PM[Product Motives]
    RM --> PTM[Patronage Motives]
    PM --> RPM[Rational Product Motives Economy Durability Efficiency]
    PM --> EPM[Emotional Product Motives Pride Pleasure Imitation Aesthetics]
    PTM --> RPTM[Rational Patronage Motives Price Service Location Variety]
    PTM --> EPTM[Emotional Patronage Motives Loyalty Habit Rapport Atmosphere]
```

## 4.4 Motive-Driven Marketing Communication Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Consumer
    participant M as Marketer
    participant Md as Media Channel
    C->>M: Latent Need Exists
    M->>M: Identify Dominant Buying Motive
    M->>Md: Craft Message Aligned to Motive
    Md->>C: Deliver Communication
    C->>C: Motive Activated Want Formed
    C->>M: Purchase Action
    M->>C: Post Purchase Reinforcement
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define buying motives and distinguish between rational and emotional buying motives with one example each.**
> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Model Question Paper]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **buying motive** is an internal psychological drive that compels a consumer to purchase a particular product or service. It is the *reason why* the consumer acts.

- **Rational Buying Motives** are logical, calculative, and based on economic or functional considerations such as price, durability, efficiency, and value for money.
  - *Example:* A consumer choosing a **Bajaj Pulsar** because of its high mileage and affordable spare parts.

- **Emotional Buying Motives** are based on feelings, sentiments, instincts, and psychological needs such as pride, fear, love, status, or aesthetic appeal.
  - *Example:* The same consumer choosing a **Royal Enfield** because of the emotional pull of "legacy, pride, and road presence."

> **Mark Splitting:** Definition (1 mark) + Rational explanation + example (1 mark) + Emotional explanation + example (1 mark).

---

### **Question 2: What are latent wants? Explain with a suitable marketing example.**
> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Latent wants** are unrecognised, dormant desires that consumers are not yet consciously aware of. They exist as a gap the consumer cannot articulate until an innovative product or service *awakens* them. They differ from *manifest wants*, which are openly expressed and recognised.

- *Marketing Example:* Before the launch of **Ola / Uber**, urban commuters had a latent want for "hassle-free, app-based, cashless, on-demand ride booking." They knew only taxis and auto-rickshaws. The innovation revealed the latent want, transforming it into a massive market demand.

> **Mark Splitting:** Definition (1 mark) + Characteristics distinguishing latent from manifest (1 mark) + Example with explanation (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Module internal choice — student answers *either* Question A *or* Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): "Wants and Buying Motives"**
> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 | Module 2 Question Choice 1]`

#### Part (a) — Explain in detail the various types of buying motives with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Introduction (1 Mark)**
Buying motives are the internal drives that direct consumer behaviour toward purchase. They are classified into two broad categories: **Product Motives** and **Patronage Motives**, each further divided into rational and emotional types.

**Step 2 — Product Motives (3 Marks)**

- *Rational Product Motives:*
  - **Economy:** Price-conscious buyer choosing a **Maruti Suzuki Alto** for fuel economy.
  - **Durability:** Buyer choosing a **Lloyd washing machine** for its 10-year compressor warranty.
  - **Efficiency:** Choosing a **MacBook** for faster processing.
  - **Dependability:** Choosing **Amul butter** for trusted quality.
  - **Convenience:** Choosing **Swiggy Instamart** for 10-minute grocery delivery.

- *Emotional Product Motives:*
  - **Pride of Ownership:** Buying a **BMW** for the status symbol.
  - **Pleasure / Comfort:** Buying a **Recliner chair** for post-work relaxation.
  - **Imitation:** Buying the same smartphone brand as one's peer group (Bandwagon Effect).
  - **Distinctiveness:** Buying a niche designer label to stand out (Snob Effect).
  - **Aesthetic Appeal:** Buying **Tanishq jewellery** for design beauty.

**Step 3 — Patronage Motives (2 Marks)**

- *Rational:* Price competitiveness, after-sales service, location, variety, return policy.
- *Emotional:* Brand loyalty, habit, social approval, personal rapport, store atmosphere.

**Step 4 — Conclusion (1 Mark)**
Marketers must identify the *dominant motive cluster* of their target segment to craft resonant communication strategies. Successful advertising speaks to motive, not just product features.

#### Part (b) — Discuss Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs and its relevance to understanding consumer wants. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Maslow's Theory Overview (1 Mark)**
Abraham Maslow (1943) proposed that human needs are arranged in a hierarchical order, from the most basic (physiological) to the highest (self-actualization). A lower-level need must be substantially satisfied before the next higher level becomes a strong motivator.

**Step 2 — The Five Levels (3 Marks)**

- **Physiological Needs:** Food, water, sleep, shelter. *Marketing example:* Restaurant chains, real estate.
- **Safety Needs:** Financial security, health, stability. *Marketing example:* Insurance companies like **LIC**, home security systems.
- **Social Needs (Love/Belonging):** Affection, friendship, acceptance. *Marketing example:* Social networking apps, family-focused ad campaigns.
- **Esteem Needs:** Self-respect, status, recognition. *Marketing example:* Luxury cars, premium fashion brands.
- **Self-Actualization:** Personal growth, creativity, fulfilment. *Marketing example:* Adventure tourism, lifelong learning platforms.

**Step 3 — Relevance to Consumer Wants (2 Marks)**

- Helps marketers **identify which need level** their product satisfies.
- Provides a framework for **market segmentation by motivation**.
- Explains *why* consumers upgrade purchases as lower needs are met.
- Guides **advertising copy** to target the appropriate need level.

**Step 4 — Limitations + Conclusion (1 Mark)**
Critics note the model is Western-centric and not strictly hierarchical in practice. Nonetheless, it remains a foundational tool in marketing communications planning.

> **Mark Splitting Summary:** (a) = 1 + 3 + 2 + 1 = 7 marks. (b) = 1 + 3 + 2 + 1 = 7 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Choice**
> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 | Module 2 Question Choice 2]`

#### Part (a) — Distinguish between needs, wants, and demands. How does this distinction help in designing marketing communications? **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definitions (2 Marks)**

- **Need:** A state of felt deprivation — basic, universal, biogenic or psychogenic (e.g., hunger, shelter).
- **Want:** The specific form a need takes, shaped by culture, personality, and environment (e.g., biryani over plain rice).
- **Demand:** Wants backed by purchasing power and willingness to buy (e.g., the ability to actually afford biryani at a restaurant).

**Step 2 — Tabular Distinction (2 Marks)**

| Basis | Need | Want | Demand |
|-------|------|------|--------|
| Nature | Universal | Specific | Economic |
| Origin | Biological/Social | Cultural/Personal | Income-based |
| Money required? | No | No (desire only) | Yes |
| Scope | Broad | Narrow | Action-ready |

**Step 3 — Relevance to Marketing Communications (2 Marks)**

- Identifying the **need** helps position the *category* (e.g., "stay hydrated").
- Activating the **want** differentiates the *brand* (e.g., "choose Paper Boat for natural hydration").
- Converting the **demand** drives the *transaction* (e.g., "Only ₹20 — buy now").
- Communication strategy flows: **Need Awareness → Want Stimulation → Demand Activation → Purchase**.

**Step 4 — Conclusion (1 Mark)**
The need-want-demand hierarchy is the backbone of segmentation, targeting, and positioning (STP). Ignoring any layer results in communication that is either too generic (need-level) or too sales-y (demand-level) without building brand love.

#### Part (b) — Explain the role of emotional buying motives in advertising. Provide three real-world advertising examples that successfully triggered emotional motives. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Emotional Motives Defined (1 Mark)**
Emotional buying motives are non-rational, feeling-based drivers such as pride, fear, love, nostalgia, belonging, status, and happiness. They dominate low-involvement and lifestyle purchases.

**Step 2 — Why Emotional Motives Work (2 Marks)**
- Emotions create *memory anchors* — ads evoking strong feelings are recalled longer.
- They bypass analytical scrutiny, enabling faster purchase decisions.
- They build brand affinity and loyalty beyond functional comparison.

**Step 3 — Three Advertising Examples (3 Marks)**

- **Nostalgia Motive — Cadbury's "Kuch Meetha Ho Jaye" campaign** triggered nostalgia for shared family celebrations, linking the brand to emotional warmth.
- **Fear Motive — Life Insurance Corporation (LIC) "Zindagi ke Saath bhi, Zindagi ke Baad bhi"** activated the emotional motive of family protection and post-death security.
- **Pride / Status Motive — Royal Enfield's "Made Like a Gun"** campaign tapped masculine pride, rugged individuality, and ownership pride.

**Step 4 — Conclusion (1 Mark)**
Emotional motives, when ethically deployed, create deeper brand-consumer connections than rational appeals alone. Modern advertising increasingly blends both for optimum impact.

> **Mark Splitting Summary:** (a) = 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 = 7 marks. (b) = 1 + 2 + 3 + 1 = 7 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
