- **Influencer Tiers:** Nano (1K–10K), Micro (10K–100K), Macro (100K–1M), Mega (1M+). Micro-influencers often have the highest trust and engagement.

- **Bass Diffusion Model:** Adoption rate = $p$ (innovation, mass media) + $q$ (imitation, social influence). Peak adoption at $t^{*} = \dfrac{1}{p+q} \cdot \ln(q/p)$.

- **Logit Choice Model with Social Influence:** $P_{ij} = \dfrac{e^{V_{ij} + \alpha S_{ij}}}{\sum e^{V_{ik} + \alpha S_{ik}}}$. Coefficient $\alpha$ measures social sensitivity.

- **Cultural Distance Formula:** Use weighted sum of cultural index differences to assess cross-cultural promotion risk.

- **Key Insight for KTU 2024:** Every social influence must be **explicitly linked to a promotional decision** — message design, media choice, or campaign evaluation. Pure theory without promotional application loses marks.

- **Always remember the Feedback Loop:** Consumer response is not the end; it becomes the *next round* of social influence via UGC, reviews, and WOM.

- **Bonus Buzzwords for Examiners:** *Authenticity, social currency, FOMO (fear of missing out), tribe marketing, community-driven promotion, parasocial relationships, algorithmic amplification* — use 2–3 of these in each answer to demonstrate currency of knowledge.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Promotion Planning and Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT742 - MARKETING COMMUNICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Promotion Planning and Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — PROMOTION PLANNING AND TECHNIQUES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Promotion Planning** is the strategic process of designing, coordinating, and executing a comprehensive set of communication activities that inform, persuade, and remind target audiences about an organisation's products, services, or ideas, ultimately driving the desired behavioural response (purchase, trial, loyalty, or advocacy).

According to **Philip Kotler** and the **KTU 2024 PECBT742 syllabus**, promotion planning is an integral component of the broader **Integrated Marketing Communications (IMC)** framework and operates within the tactical layer of the marketing mix (the **4Ps — Product, Price, Place, Promotion**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Promotion Mix (also called the Marketing Communications Mix)** is the specific blend of promotional tools that an organisation uses to deliver its value proposition to the target audience. The five classical elements are: **Advertising, Sales Promotion, Personal Selling, Public Relations (PR), and Direct Marketing**. Digital Marketing is the modern sixth element under the 2024 scheme.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Theatre Play"

> [!TIP]
> **🎭 Intuition Check:** Imagine you are directing a grand theatrical play.
> - The **script** = your marketing strategy and message.
> - The **stage** = the channel (TV, social media, retail counter).
> - The **actors** = your sales force and brand ambassadors.
> - The **lighting and sound** = advertising and PR.
> - The **free popcorn and discount coupons** distributed at the entrance = sales promotion.
>
> **Promotion Planning** is the **director's workbook** — it specifies *what* will be said, *who* will say it, *how* it will be said, *where* it will be said, and *when* it will be said, all timed to maximise audience engagement. Without a plan, the play descends into chaos — actors forget lines, lighting fails, and the audience walks out.

---

### 1.3 Scope and Importance in KTU 2024

| Dimension | Why It Matters in Marketing Communications |
|---|---|
| **Strategic** | Aligns promotional spend with business objectives (sales, awareness, loyalty). |
| **Tactical** | Decides *which mix* (ad vs. PR vs. digital) to deploy for each product life-cycle stage. |
| **Financial** | Promotional budgets can range from **5% to 20%** of revenue for FMCG firms. |
| **Customer-Centric** | Ensures the right message reaches the right customer at the right time (the **3R Rule**). |
| **Measurable** | Provides KPIs (CTR, ROAS, GRPs) for evaluating communication effectiveness. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key 3R Rule of Promotion Planning:** Right **Message** → Right **Audience** → Right **Time**. KTU examiners frequently award marks to students who explicitly mention this rule in 14-mark questions.

---

### 1.4 Glossary of Essential Terms

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **IMC** | Integrated Marketing Communications — coordinated use of all promotional tools. |
| **AIDA** | Attention, Interest, Desire, Action — classic customer response hierarchy. |
| **DAGMAR** | Defining Advertising Goals for Measured Advertising Results (Colley, 1961). |
| **CPM (Cost Per Mille)** | Cost per thousand impressions of an advertisement. |
| **GRP (Gross Rating Point)** | Reach × Frequency ÷ 100 — measures advertising weight. |
| **BOGOF** | Buy One Get One Free — a common sales promotion technique. |
| **Pull Strategy** | Marketing aimed at the end consumer to "pull" demand. |
| **Push Strategy** | Marketing aimed at channel intermediaries to "push" the product. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Promotion Planning Process (Six-Stage Model)

A KTU-mandated, universally accepted six-stage model is the foundation of every promotion planning question. Master these stages sequentially:

### 🔹 Stage 1 — Identify the Target Audience
- **Why:** Promotional efforts must be sharply focused; "marketing to everyone" wastes budget.
- **How:** Use demographic, psychographic, geographic, and behavioural segmentation.
- **Output:** A clear, quantifiable audience profile (e.g., "urban women, 25–35, SEC A, Instagram users").

### 🔹 Stage 2 — Establish Communication Objectives
- **Why:** Objectives must precede tactics (a cardinal KTU valuation principle).
- **How:** Use the **DAGMAR** framework: Awareness → Comprehension → Conviction → Action.
- **Output:** SMART objectives (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound).

### 🔹 Stage 3 — Design the Message
- **Why:** Message content determines whether the audience pays attention.
- **How:** Apply the **AIDA** model. Use the four message-generation routes: **Emotional, Rational, Moral, Fear/Humour**.
- **Output:** Message content, structure, format, and source (celebrity/brand ambassador).

### 🔹 Stage 4 — Select the Promotion Mix (Channels)
- **Why:** Different tools perform different jobs. A media-neutral plan is impossible.
- **How:** Match tools to objectives:
  - **Awareness** → Advertising, PR, Digital.
  - **Trial** → Sales Promotion, Sampling, Coupons.
  - **Loyalty** → Personal Selling, Direct Marketing, CRM.
  - **Brand Image** → PR, Sponsorship, Publicity.
- **Output:** A defined promotional mix with channel-wise weightage.

### 🔹 Stage 5 — Determine the Promotion Budget
- **Why:** Every KTU question tests the ability to compute and justify budget allocation.
- **How:** Apply one or more of the five standard methods (see Section 2.3).
- **Output:** A quantified, justifiable budget with channel-wise allocation.

### 🔹 Stage 6 — Evaluate, Implement, and Control
- **Why:** Promotion is a measurable activity, not a black hole of expenditure.
- **How:** Pre-test, post-test, A/B testing, sales-impact analysis.
- **Output:** ROI, ROAS (Return on Ad Spend), brand equity lift, customer acquisition cost.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common 14-mark question is *"Explain the steps involved in developing a promotional plan."* Always present your answer as **six sequential stages** with a real brand example (e.g., Zomato, Apple, Maggi) to score full marks.

---

## 2.2 Elements of the Promotion Mix (Detailed)

### 🔸 A. Advertising
- **Definition:** Any paid form of non-personal presentation and promotion of ideas, goods, or services by an identified sponsor.
- **Sub-types:** TV, Print, Radio, Outdoor (OOH), Digital (Display, Search, Social).
- **Strengths:** High reach, brand-building power, controlled message.
- **Limitations:** High cost, low feedback, one-way communication.

### 🔸 B. Sales Promotion (SP)
- **Definition:** Short-term incentives to encourage purchase or trial of a product/service.
- **Sub-types:**
  - *Consumer SP* — Coupons, discounts, BOGOF, samples, loyalty rewards, contests.
  - *Trade SP* — Off-invoice discounts, free goods, display allowances, co-op advertising.
  - *Sales Force SP* — Contests, bonuses, sales rallies, incentives.
- **Strengths:** Quick sales boost, measurable, flexible.
- **Limitations:** Brand erosion if overused, short-lived impact.

### 🔸 C. Personal Selling
- **Definition:** Oral presentation in a conversation with one or more prospective purchasers for the purpose of making sales.
- **Sub-types:** Field sales, retail selling, tele-selling, key account management.
- **Strengths:** Direct feedback, relationship building, customisation.
- **Limitations:** Expensive per contact, limited reach, training-intensive.

### 🔸 D. Public Relations (PR)
- **Definition:** Building good relations with the company's various publics by obtaining favourable publicity, building a good corporate image, and handling unfavourable rumours.
- **Tools:** Press releases, press conferences, sponsorships, events, CSR, lobbying.
- **Strengths:** High credibility, low cost (editorial), long-term reputation.
- **Limitations:** Lack of direct control, slow impact.

### 🔸 E. Direct Marketing
- **Definition:** Direct communication with carefully targeted individual consumers to obtain an immediate, measurable response.
- **Tools:** Email, SMS, catalogues, telemarketing, direct mail, personalised digital ads.
- **Strengths:** Measurable, personalised, builds database.
- **Limitations:** Intrusive, opt-out fatigue, list management cost.

### 🔸 F. Digital Marketing (Modern Add-on)
- **Definition:** Promotional activities delivered through digital channels such as search engines, social media, email, and websites.
- **Tools:** SEO, SEM, Content Marketing, Influencer Marketing, Affiliate Marketing, Programmatic Ads.
- **Strengths:** Hyper-targeting, real-time analytics, low entry cost.
- **Limitations:** Ad fatigue, privacy regulations, technical complexity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Push vs Pull Strategy (KTU Favourite):**
> - **Push Strategy** = *Pushing* the product through intermediaries (manufacturer → wholesaler → retailer → consumer). Heavy use of **personal selling + trade promotion**.
> - **Pull Strategy** = *Pulling* the consumer directly to demand the product. Heavy use of **advertising + consumer sales promotion**.
> - **Example:** Coca-Cola uses a **hybrid strategy** — heavy TV advertising (pull) plus retailer margins (push).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Promotion Budget & Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **exam-ready** and frequently tested as 3-mark numerical sub-questions or 7-mark computational cases.

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbol | Computation | Engineering / Business Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Percentage of Sales Method** | $B$ | $B = r \times S$ | $B$ = Budget, $r$ = fixed %, $S$ = past/forecast sales |
| 2 | **Objective-and-Task Method** | $B$ | $B = \sum (T_i \times C_i)$ | Sum of (Task $i$ cost × No. of tasks) |
| 3 | **Cost Per Mille (CPM)** | $\text{CPM}$ | $\text{CPM} = \dfrac{C}{I} \times 1000$ | $C$ = Cost, $I$ = Impressions |
| 4 | **Reach × Frequency (GRPs)** | $\text{GRP}$ | $\text{GRP} = R \times F$ | $R$ = Reach %, $F$ = Avg. exposures |
| 5 | **Return on Ad Spend** | $\text{ROAS}$ | $\text{ROAS} = \dfrac{\text{Revenue} - \text{Ad Cost}}{\text{Ad Cost}} \times 100$ | KPI for digital campaigns |
| 6 | **Sales Promotion Lift** | $L$ | $L = \dfrac{S_p - S_{pre}}{S_{pre}} \times 100$ | $S_p$ = Sales during promo, $S_{pre}$ = Pre-promo sales |
| 7 | **Customer Acquisition Cost** | $\text{CAC}$ | $\text{CAC} = \dfrac{\text{Total Promo Cost}}{\text{New Customers}}$ | Marketing efficiency |
| 8 | **Break-Even Response Rate** | $R_{be}$ | $R_{be} = \dfrac{\text{Campaign Cost}}{\text{Profit per Response}}$ | Minimum campaign viability |
| 9 | **Sales Response Function** | $S$ | $S = a + b \cdot A^n$ | Diminishing returns on ad spend |
| 10 | **Competitive Parity Budget** | $B_{cp}$ | $B_{cp} = \bar{B}_{competitors}$ | Match industry-average spend |

---

## 2.4 KTU-Relevant Promotion Techniques

| Technique | Industry Use | KTU-Expected Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Sampling** | FMCG, Food | Free product trial to induce first purchase. |
| **Couponing** | Retail, E-com | Discount vouchers distributed via print/digital. |
| **Loyalty Programmes** | Airlines, Banks | Points/rewards for repeat purchase. |
| **POP Displays** | Retail | In-store displays to influence point-of-purchase decision. |
| **Sponsorship** | Sports, Events | Brand visibility through event association. |
| **Celebrity Endorsement** | FMCG, Fashion | Trust transfer from celebrity to brand. |
| **Influencer Marketing** | Beauty, Tech | Social-media creators promoting products. |
| **Cause-Related Marketing** | CSR-led brands | Linking product purchase to a social cause. |
| **Referral Marketing** | SaaS, Telecom | Existing customers refer new ones for rewards. |
| **Content Marketing** | B2B, Education | Educating customers to build authority. |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** A KTU student should be able to *apply* these techniques in a case study — e.g., "Reliance Jio used free sampling (free SIM + 4G data) to acquire **100 million subscribers in 170 days** (Sep 2016 – Mar 2017)."

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides **exhaustive, marker-friendly derivations** for the most commonly tested numerical and analytical questions in KTU ESE and internal assessments. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Promotion Budget using Percentage of Sales Method

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style Numerical]**
> ABC Pharma Ltd. forecasts sales of **₹50 crores** for FY 2025–26. The company follows the *Percentage of Sales* method and allocates **12%** of sales to promotion. Calculate the promotion budget. If the company wishes to spend an additional **₹2 crores** on a digital campaign, what is the new effective percentage?

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Identify the given parameters.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Forecast Sales }(S) &= \text{₹50,00,00,000} \\
\text{Allocation Rate }(r) &= 12\% = 0.12
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the percentage of sales formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= r \times S \\
B &= 0.12 \times 50,00,00,000 \\
B &= \text{₹6,00,00,000} = \text{₹6 Crores}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Add the additional digital campaign cost.

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{new}} &= B + \text{Digital Spend} \\
B_{\text{new}} &= 6,00,00,000 + 2,00,00,000 \\
B_{\text{new}} &= \text{₹8,00,00,000} = \text{₹8 Crores}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the new effective percentage.

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{\text{new}} &= \frac{B_{\text{new}}}{S} \times 100 \\
r_{\text{new}} &= \frac{8,00,00,000}{50,00,00,000} \times 100 \\
r_{\text{new}} &= 16\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Original budget = **₹6 crores (12%)**; New budget = **₹8 crores (16%)**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Cost Per Mille (CPM) Comparison

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
> A firm has two media options for a national campaign:
> - **Magazine X:** Cost = ₹4,50,000 for 3,00,000 readers.
> - **TV Channel Y:** Cost = ₹12,00,000 for 80,00,000 viewers.
> Which medium is more cost-efficient on a CPM basis?

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Apply CPM formula for Magazine X.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPM}_X &= \frac{C_X}{I_X} \times 1000 \\
&= \frac{4,50,000}{3,00,000} \times 1000 \\
&= 1.5 \times 1000 \\
&= \text{₹1,500 per 1,000 readers}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply CPM formula for TV Channel Y.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPM}_Y &= \frac{C_Y}{I_Y} \times 1000 \\
&= \frac{12,00,000}{80,00,000} \times 1000 \\
&= 0.15 \times 1000 \\
&= \text{₹150 per 1,000 viewers}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compare the two CPMs.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\text{CPM}_X}{\text{CPM}_Y} &= \frac{1,500}{150} = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Decision logic.

> The TV channel is **10 times more cost-efficient** on a pure reach basis. However, the manager should *not* conclude based on CPM alone. **Magazine readers** typically have higher SEC-A concentration, longer dwell time, and stronger brand recall. Hence, a **qualitative trade-off** (target audience match × CPM) is essential.

**Final Answer:** TV Channel Y is more cost-efficient (**₹150 vs ₹1,500 per 1,000 impressions**); however, target-audience relevance should be the final decision driver.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Break-Even Response Rate for a Direct Mail Campaign

> **[KTU Internal Test — Model Question]**
> A retailer plans to send **50,000 direct mail pieces** at a total cost of **₹15,00,000**. The average profit per converted customer is **₹750**. What is the minimum response rate required to break even? If the actual response rate is **0.6%**, what is the campaign's profit?

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Define variables.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= 50,000 \text{ (mail pieces)} \\
C_{total} &= \text{₹15,00,000} \\
P_{resp} &= \text{₹750 (profit per response)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply break-even formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{be} &= \frac{C_{total}}{P_{resp}} \\
&= \frac{15,00,000}{750} \\
&= 2,000 \text{ responses}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute break-even response rate as a percentage.

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{be} &= \frac{R_{be}}{N} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{2,000}{50,000} \times 100 \\
&= 4\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Calculate actual responses and profit.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{actual} &= 0.6\% \times 50,000 = 300 \text{ responses} \\
\text{Revenue}_{\text{actual}} &= 300 \times 750 = \text{₹2,25,000} \\
\text{Loss} &= \text{Revenue}_{\text{actual}} - C_{total} \\
&= 2,25,000 - 15,00,000 \\
&= -\text{₹12,75,000} \text{ (Loss)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Break-even rate = **4% (2,000 responses)**. At **0.6%** actual response, the campaign incurs a loss of **₹12,75,000**. The campaign is **not viable** under current parameters.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Promotion Mix Selection by Product Life Cycle (PLC)

> **KTU-mandated conceptual framework** (often tested as a 7-mark question).

| PLC Stage | Primary Objective | Recommended Promotion Mix | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Introduction** | Build awareness, induce trial | Heavy **Advertising** + **Sampling** + **PR** | Maggi's relaunch after 2015 ban |
| **Growth** | Differentiate, expand distribution | **Advertising** + **Sales Promotion** + **Personal Selling** | boAt audio products |
| **Maturity** | Defend market share, build loyalty | **Sales Promotion** + **Direct Marketing** + **Loyalty Programs** | Tata Tea |
| **Decline** | Harvest, phase out | Minimal **PR** + **Discount Sales Promotion** | BlackBerry phones |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Always justify the mix choice using the *objective–tool* linkage. Saying *"advertising in maturity"* without reasoning costs marks. Saying *"advertising builds awareness; in maturity, awareness is already high, so we shift spend to sales promotion to defend share"* scores full marks.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Push vs Pull Strategy Selection

| Factor | Push Strategy | Pull Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **Target** | Channel intermediaries | End consumer |
| **Tools** | Trade allowances, sales force, dealer incentives | Mass advertising, coupons, sampling |
| **Best for** | Industrial goods, low-involvement, complex channels | FMCG, consumer durables, brand-led products |
| **Cost** | Variable, sales-force intensive | Fixed, media-intensive |
| **Control** | High (over distribution) | Lower (over retailer) |
| **Example** | Maruti Suzuki to dealers | Apple iPhone launch event |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Promotion Planning Process (Six-Stage Sequential Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage1: Identify Target Audience] --> B[Stage2: Establish Communication Objectives]
    B --> C[Stage3: Design the Message AIDA Model]
    C --> D[Stage4: Select the Promotion Mix]
    D --> E[Stage5: Determine Promotion Budget]
    E --> F[Stage6: Implement Measure and Control]
    F --> G{Objectives Met?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Continue and Scale]
    G -- No --> I[Re-strategise and Loop Back to Stage 2]
    I --> B
    H --> J[Brand Growth and ROI]
```

> **Figure Interpretation:**
> - The flow is **sequential** but **iterative** — feedback from Stage 6 loops back to Stage 2.
> - This is a **closed-loop** control system, identical in logic to engineering feedback control (KTU examiners love this analogy).

---

## 4.2 The Six Elements of the Promotion Mix

```mermaid
graph TD
    PM[Promotion Mix] --> ADV[Advertising]
    PM --> SP[Sales Promotion]
    PM --> PS[Personal Selling]
    PM --> PR[Public Relations]
    PM --> DM[Direct Marketing]
    PM --> DIG[Digital Marketing]
    
    ADV --> ADV1[TV Print Radio OOH Digital Ads]
    SP --> SP1[Consumer SP]
    SP --> SP2[Trade SP]
    SP --> SP3[Sales Force SP]
    PS --> PS1[Field Sales]
    PS --> PS2[Retail Selling]
    PS --> PS3[Key Account Management]
    PR --> PR1[Press Releases]
    PR --> PR2[Sponsorships]
    PR --> PR3[Events and CSR]
    DM --> DM1[Email]
    DM --> DM2[SMS]
    DM --> DM3[Tele Marketing]
    DIG --> DIG1[SEO and SEM]
    DIG --> DIG2[Social Media]
    DIG --> DIG3[Influencer Marketing]
```

---

## 4.3 AIDA Model — Customer Response Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[A: Attention] --> I[I: Interest]
    I --> D[D: Desire]
    D --> AC[A: Action]
    
    A1[Catchy Visual Headline] -.-> A
    I1[Engaging Content Story] -.-> I
    D1[Emotional Persuasion USP] -.-> D
    AC1[Strong CTA Offer Deadline] -.-> AC
```

> **Block-Level Interpretation:**
> - **Attention** is grabbed by visual or auditory stimuli (e.g., Zomato's witty push notifications).
> - **Interest** is sustained by informative content.
> - **Desire** is built through emotional or rational persuasion.
> - **Action** is triggered by a clear Call-To-Action (CTA): "Order Now", "Limited Period", "Buy 1 Get 1".

---

## 4.4 DAGMAR Model — Advertising Goal Setting

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U[Unaware] --> A[Aware]
    A --> C[Comprehension]
    C --> CV[Conviction]
    CV --> AC[Action]
    
    style U fill:#f9d6d6
    style A fill:#f9e4b6
    style C fill:#d6f9d6
    style CV fill:#b6d6f9
    style AC fill:#d6b6f9
```

> **Interpretation:** DAGMAR (Colley, 1961) requires that every advertisement move the consumer from **Unawareness → Awareness → Comprehension → Conviction → Action**. This provides **measurable, laddered communication objectives** — the gold standard for KTU advertising questions.

---

## 4.5 Push vs Pull Strategy — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PushStrategy
        P1[Manufacturer] -->|Trade Discount| P2[Wholesaler]
        P2 -->|Margin| P3[Retailer]
        P3 -->|Shelf Space| P4[Consumer]
    end
    
    subgraph PullStrategy
        L1[Manufacturer] -->|Mass Advertising| L2[Consumer]
        L2 -->|Demand| L3[Retailer]
        L3 -->|Order| L4[Wholesaler]
        L4 -->|Stock| L1
    end
```

> **Real-world Mapping:**
> - **Coca-Cola** uses a **push-pull hybrid**: aggressive retail margins (push) + celebrity-led advertising (pull).
> - **Infosys** in B2B sales relies almost entirely on **push** (relationship-driven personal selling).
> - **iPhone** launches are textbook **pull** — Apple creates consumer hype, and retailers queue to stock.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> All questions are aligned with the **KTU 2024 End Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern: Part A (2-mark short answers, scaled to 3 marks under revised scheme) and Part B (14-mark long answers with internal choice).

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Style]**

### **Q1. Define the term 'Promotion Mix' and list its six elements.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> The Promotion Mix is the strategic combination of communication tools a marketer uses to convey value to target customers. Its six elements are:
> 1. **Advertising** — Paid, non-personal mass communication.
> 2. **Sales Promotion** — Short-term incentives for purchase.
> 3. **Personal Selling** — Face-to-face oral persuasion.
> 4. **Public Relations** — Building favourable public image.
> 5. **Direct Marketing** — Direct contact for measurable response.
> 6. **Digital Marketing** — Online channels like SEO, social media, influencer marketing.

### **Q2. What is the 'Percentage of Sales' method of promotion budgeting? State one advantage and one limitation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> Under the **Percentage of Sales** method, the promotion budget is set as a fixed percentage of either past or forecast sales.
>
> **Formula:** $B = r \times S$
>
> - **Advantage:** Simple, financially safe, easy to justify to top management.
> - **Limitation:** Treats promotion as a *result* of sales rather than a *cause* — discourages counter-cyclical spending when sales dip.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **six stages of developing a promotional plan**, with a suitable example of a consumer brand of your choice. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **Push and Pull strategies** of promotion. Under what conditions would a company prefer a hybrid approach? Illustrate with a real-world example. **(7 Marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Q(a) — Six Stages of Promotional Plan

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Promotion planning is the structured process of designing, executing, and evaluating marketing communication activities. It ensures the *right message reaches the right audience through the right channel at the right time*.

**Stage 1 — Identify the Target Audience (1 Mark):**
Example: **Nike India** targets urban youth aged 15–28, SEC A & B, who are fitness-conscious, tech-savvy, and active on Instagram and YouTube. Segmentation is done demographically (age, income), psychographically (lifestyle, attitudes), and behaviourally (usage rate).

**Stage 2 — Establish Communication Objectives (1 Mark):**
Using the **DAGMAR** model, Nike's objective for its Air Max launch is: *"Achieve 60% aided awareness among target youth in Tier-1 cities within 60 days, and drive 2 lakh website visits."* Objectives are SMART — Specific (60% awareness), Measurable (aided recall test), Achievable (based on prior campaign benchmarks), Relevant (aligned with brand-building strategy), Time-bound (60 days).

**Stage 3 — Design the Message (1 Mark):**
Message follows the **AIDA** model. *Attention* via a viral teaser video featuring Virat Kohli. *Interest* via product storytelling. *Desire* via emotional appeal ("Just Do It"). *Action* via a limited-period 10% discount code and QR code on screen.

**Stage 4 — Select the Promotion Mix (1 Mark):**
- **Advertising:** 40% — YouTube pre-rolls, Instagram reels.
- **Sales Promotion:** 20% — Discount codes, BOGOF on second pair.
- **Personal Selling:** 15% — In-store体验 at Nike outlets.
- **Digital Marketing:** 20% — Influencer collaborations, SEO.
- **PR:** 5% — Press release on Virat Kohli association.

**Stage 5 — Determine the Promotion Budget (1 Mark):**
Using the **Objective-and-Task Method**, Nike estimates: Awareness task (₹3 Cr) + Trial task (₹1.5 Cr) + Loyalty task (₹0.5 Cr) = **₹5 Cr total**.

**Stage 6 — Implement, Measure, and Control (1 Mark):**
- Pre-test: Focus groups for ad creative.
- Post-test: Aided awareness survey, CTR (Click-Through Rate) on digital ads, sales lift in pilot cities.
- KPI dashboard updated weekly; budget reallocation to high-ROAS channels.

**Conclusion (0.5 Marks):** A structured six-stage plan transforms promotional spending from a cost centre into a measurable revenue driver.

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Q(b) — Push vs Pull Strategy

**Definitions (2 Marks):**

| Push Strategy | Pull Strategy |
|---|---|
| Manufacturer persuades **intermediaries** to stock and promote the product. | Manufacturer builds **consumer demand** so they actively seek the product. |
| Tools: trade discounts, dealer incentives, sales-force push. | Tools: mass advertising, consumer sales promotion, branding. |

**Tabular Comparison (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Push | Pull |
|---|---|---|
| **Target** | Wholesalers, Retailers | End Consumer |
| **Cost Structure** | Variable, sales-force heavy | Fixed, media heavy |
| **Control** | Over distribution | Over demand creation |
| **Speed of Impact** | Slow (channel-dependent) | Fast (if media works) |
| **Best Suited For** | Industrial goods, B2B, complex channels | FMCG, brand-led consumer goods |

**Conditions for Hybrid Strategy (1 Mark):**
A hybrid strategy is preferred when:
- The product is sold through long, multi-tiered distribution channels (e.g., FMCG, telecom).
- The brand needs both **availability** (push) and **aspiration** (pull).
- The market is competitive, requiring shelf visibility *and* consumer recall.

**Real-World Example (1 Mark):**
**Coca-Cola India** uses a hybrid: (i) **Push** — heavy trade margins for kirana stores, free coolers for retailers, sales-force visits; (ii) **Pull** — celebrity-led TV ads during IPL, "Share a Coke" campaign, Diwali packaging. Result: Coca-Cola reaches over 2 million retail outlets in India while maintaining top-of-mind brand recall.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern, Adapted]**

**(a)** What is **sales promotion**? Explain its three categories (Consumer, Trade, and Sales Force) with at least two examples for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A company plans a **direct mail campaign of 1,00,000 pieces** at a total cost of **₹30,00,000**. The expected profit per converted customer is **₹1,200**. If the actual response rate is **0.8%**, calculate:
  (i) The **break-even response rate**,
  (ii) The **actual profit or loss**, and
  (iii) The **Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC)**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Q(a) — Sales Promotion

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Sales promotion consists of **short-term incentives**, usually aimed at accelerating sales or stimulating trial, that are not part of the regular advertising or personal-selling effort.

**Three Categories (6 Marks):**

**(i) Consumer Sales Promotion (2 Marks):**
Targeted at the end-user to induce trial or repeat purchase.
- **Coupons:** Discount vouchers distributed via newspapers or apps (e.g., Amazon Pay coupons).
- **Sampling:** Free product samples (e.g., Lifebuoy soap samples during Swachh Bharat).
- **BOGOF / Discounts:** Buy-One-Get-One-Free offers (e.g., Pepsi 1.5L + 250ml free).
- **Loyalty Programs:** Points-based rewards (e.g., Titan Encircle).
- **Contests/Sweepstakes:** "Win a car on purchase" (e.g., Onam contests by Kerala retailers).

**(ii) Trade Sales Promotion (2 Marks):**
Targeted at wholesalers and retailers to ensure stock availability and visibility.
- **Off-invoice Discount:** Reduced invoice price for bulk purchase.
- **Free Goods:** Buy 10 get 2 free (e.g., ITC offering extra stock to distributors).
- **Display Allowances:** Payment for premium shelf space.
- **Co-operative Advertising (Co-op Ad):** Manufacturer shares ad cost with retailer (e.g., Nokia store branding).
- **Sales Contests:** Wholesalers compete for prizes on volume sold.

**(iii) Sales Force Promotion (2 Marks):**
Targeted at the company's own sales personnel to boost morale and performance.
- **Incentive Bonuses:** Cash or travel rewards for exceeding targets.
- **Sales Contests:** Spiffs (Sales Person Incentive Funds) for promoting specific SKUs.
- **Sales Rallies:** Annual award ceremonies to recognise top performers.
- **Training-cum-Incentive Trips:** Top 10 performers win a sponsored trip (very common in pharma and insurance).

**Conclusion (0.5 Marks):** A well-designed sales promotion mix accelerates short-term sales and complements long-term brand-building.

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Q(b) — Direct Mail Campaign Numerical

**Given Data (0.5 Marks):**
- Number of mail pieces, $N = 1,00,000$
- Total campaign cost, $C_{total} = \text{₹30,00,000}$
- Profit per response, $P_{resp} = \text{₹1,200}$
- Actual response rate, $r_{actual} = 0.8\%$

**(i) Break-Even Response Rate (2.5 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Calculate break-even number of responses.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{be} &= \frac{C_{total}}{P_{resp}} \\
&= \frac{30,00,000}{1,200} \\
&= 2,500 \text{ responses}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Express as a percentage.

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{be} &= \frac{R_{be}}{N} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{2,500}{1,00,000} \times 100 \\
&= 2.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**∴ Break-even response rate = 2.5% (2,500 responses).**

**(ii) Actual Profit or Loss (2.5 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Calculate actual responses.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{actual} &= 0.8\% \times 1,00,000 = 800 \text{ responses}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate actual revenue.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Revenue} &= R_{actual} \times P_{resp} \\
&= 800 \times 1,200 \\
&= \text{₹9,60,000}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate profit or loss.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Profit/Loss} &= \text{Revenue} - C_{total} \\
&= 9,60,000 - 30,00,000 \\
&= -\text{₹20,40,000} \quad \text{(Loss)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**∴ Actual loss = ₹20,40,000** (since 0.8% is well below the 2.5% break-even).

**(iii) Customer Acquisition Cost (1.5 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CAC} &= \frac{C_{total}}{R_{actual}} \\
&= \frac{30,00,000}{800} \\
&= \text{₹3,750 per customer}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion (0.5 Marks):**
Since the CAC (**₹3,750**) is more than 3× the profit per customer (**₹1,200**), the campaign is **financially unviable**. Management should either (a) improve the response rate via better creative/targeting, or (b) abandon the channel for this product.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Promotion Planning Questions:**
> 1. **Skipping the "Why" before "How":** Students often list the six stages mechanically. Examiners award higher marks when you justify *why* each stage precedes the next (logical flow).
> 2. **No brand example:** A generic answer scores 9/14. Adding a real brand (Nike, Zomato, Maggi) and a numerical anchor lifts you to 12–14.
> 3. **Confusing Push with Pull:** A frequent error is calling *"retailer discounts"* as Pull strategy. Remember: Push targets **intermediaries**; Pull targets **consumers**.
> 4. **Ignoring the digital sixth element:** Under KTU 2024 scheme, treating promotion mix as only five elements is dated. Always include **Digital Marketing**.
> 5. **Numerical errors in CPM/CAC:** Students forget to multiply by 1,000 in CPM or divide correctly in CAC. Always state the formula first, then substitute.
> 6. **No conclusion:** Ending abruptly costs 0.5–1 mark. Always close with a one-line **managerial insight**.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must Memorise for KTU 2024):**

- ✅ Promotion is **one of the 4Ps**; it is the *voice* of the marketing mix.
- ✅ Six elements: **Advertising, Sales Promotion, Personal Selling, Public Relations, Direct Marketing, Digital Marketing**.
- ✅ Six-stage process: **Target → Objectives → Message → Mix → Budget → Evaluation**.
- ✅ AIDA: **Attention → Interest → Desire → Action**.
- ✅ DAGMAR: **Unawareness → Awareness → Comprehension → Conviction → Action** (Colley, 1961).
- ✅ Push targets **intermediaries**; Pull targets **consumers**; Hybrid combines both.
- ✅ Promotion mix varies by **Product Life Cycle (PLC)** stage.
- ✅ Budget methods: **Percentage of Sales, Objective-and-Task, Competitive Parity, Affordable, ROI-based**.
- ✅ Key formulas: $B = r \times S$, $\text{CPM} = (C/I) \times 1000$, $\text{GRPs} = R \times F$, $\text{CAC} = \text{Cost} / \text{New Customers}$, $R_{be} = C / P_{resp}$.
- ✅ Modern 2024 emphasis: **Digital Marketing, Influencer Marketing, Data-driven Personalisation, AI-based targeting, Programmatic Advertising, Sustainability messaging**.
- ✅ Always include a **real brand example** to score full marks.
- ✅ Always **justify your mix choice** with the objective–tool linkage.
- ✅ A promotion plan is **iterative**, not linear — feedback loops back to objectives.
- ✅ Sales promotion is **short-term** and **tactical**; advertising is **long-term** and **strategic**.
- ✅ Public relations builds **credibility**; advertising builds **awareness**; personal selling builds **relationships**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advertising Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT742 - MARKETING COMMUNICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Advertising Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Advertising Techniques?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Advertising Techniques** are the strategic, creative, and executional methods employed by marketers to craft persuasive messages that capture audience attention, evoke specific psychological responses, communicate brand value, and ultimately drive consumer action. They encompass the *appeals*, *message structures*, *copywriting devices*, *endorsement styles*, and *execution formats* used in advertising.

In the KTU 2024 framework, advertising techniques are studied under **Marketing Communications (PECBT742)** as part of the *creative strategy* layer of the **IMC (Integrated Marketing Communications)** plan, sitting between *message strategy* and *media strategy*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stage Play" Intuition

Imagine advertising as **directing a stage play** for a paying audience:

- The **product** is your lead actor.
- The **advertising technique** is the *genre* of the play — a comedy (humor appeal), a thriller (fear appeal), a documentary (rational/informational), or a romance (emotional appeal).
- The **script** is the *copy*, and the **costumes & props** are the *visuals and creative execution*.

A director chooses a technique based on the **audience (target segment)**, the **story they want to tell**, and the **emotion they want to leave them with**. Just as no single play genre works for every audience, no single advertising technique works for every product or consumer.

### 1.3 Why Advertising Techniques Matter in Marketing Communications

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Purpose of Advertising Techniques:**
> 1. **Cut through clutter** — Differentiate the brand in a saturated media landscape.
> 2. **Encode the message** — Translate product attributes into consumer-relevant meaning.
> 3. **Trigger psychological response** — Move the consumer through the **AIDA** hierarchy (Attention → Interest → Desire → Action).
> 4. **Build brand equity** — Reinforce associations, personality, and positioning.
> 5. **Drive purchase intent** — Convert awareness into measurable action.

### 1.4 Broad Classification of Advertising Techniques (KTU 2024 Module 3 Map)

| **S.No** | **Category** | **Core Idea** |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | Informational / Rational Appeals | Logic, facts, features, performance |
| 2 | Emotional Appeals | Feelings — fear, love, joy, guilt, pride |
| 3 | Moral Appeals | Ethics, social good, duty |
| 4 | Humor Appeals | Comedy, wit, satire |
| 5 | Fear Appeals | Risk, danger, loss aversion |
| 6 | Comparative Advertising | Direct/indirect comparison with rivals |
| 7 | Endorsement Styles | Celebrity, expert, typical consumer, CEO |
| 8 | Execution Formats | Slice-of-life, lifestyle, demonstration, fantasy, testimonial |
| 9 | Copywriting Devices | Headlines, sub-heads, body copy, slogans, jingles |
| 10 | Bandwagon, Snob & Plain-Folk | Social conformity, exclusivity, commonality |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Strategic Logic Behind Choosing a Technique

Every advertising technique follows a **4-step decision sequence** rooted in consumer psychology:

1. **Identify the Buying Motive** — Is the purchase driven by *logic* (e.g., insurance, B2B), *emotion* (e.g., perfume, luxury), or *social pressure* (e.g., fashion)?
2. **Map the Consumer's Mental Stage** — Use the **AIDA / DAGMAR** hierarchy:
   - **Attention** → bold visuals, novelty, shock
   - **Interest** → demonstrations, rational facts
   - **Desire** → emotional storytelling, aspiration
   - **Action** → strong CTA, urgency, scarcity
3. **Select the Appeal Type** — Informational, Emotional, Moral, Humor, Fear, or Comparison.
4. **Choose the Execution Format** — Slice-of-life, demonstration, fantasy, lifestyle, testimonial, scientific/technical.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Model — DAGMAR (Defining Advertising Goals for Measured Advertising Results):**
> DAGMAR posits that advertising moves consumers through a hierarchy of **Communication → Conviction → Action** stages. The technique chosen must be mapped to the *current stage* the target consumer is in. Selecting a fear appeal for a consumer who is not even *aware* of the product is a strategic mismatch.

## 2.2 Deep Dive — The Major Advertising Techniques

### 🎯 A. Informational / Rational Appeals

- **Definition:** Appeals based on **facts, data, performance metrics, and functional benefits**.
- **Best For:** High-involvement products (cars, electronics), B2B, durables, technical goods.
- **Logic:** Consumers use *central route processing* (Petty & Cacioppo's Elaboration Likelihood Model).
- **Examples:** Car mileage claims, detergent stain-removal percentages, insurance policy features.

### ❤️ B. Emotional Appeals

- **Definition:** Appeals targeting **feelings, moods, and psychological states** rather than logic.
- **Sub-types (KTU-relevant):**
  - *Love & Affection* — Family bonding ads (e.g., Cadbury, Amul family).
  - *Pride & Patriotism* — Nationalistic campaigns (e.g., "Made in India").
  - *Joy & Happiness* — Coca-Cola's "Open Happiness" philosophy.
  - *Guilt & Nostalgia* — Old Spice, Maggi's "Mummy ke haath jaisa swaad".
- **Logic:** Uses *peripheral route processing* — attitudes are formed through association with positive affect.

### 😱 C. Fear Appeals

- **Definition:** Arousing anxiety about consequences of *not* using the product.
- **Two Variables:** **Severity** (how bad the outcome is) × **Susceptibility** (how likely it is for me).
- **Optimal Level:** Moderate fear + high efficacy message. Excessive fear causes *boomerang* — denial or message avoidance.
- **Example:** Insurance ads showing hospital trauma; seat-belt campaigns.

### 😂 D. Humor Appeals

- **Definition:** Use of comedy, satire, wit, or absurdity to gain attention and likability.
- **Advantages:** High attention, strong memory encoding, positive brand mood.
- **Risks:** Humor can *overshadow the brand message* ("the audience remembers the joke, not the product").
- **Rule of Thumb:** **20% humor, 80% brand-related content.**

### ⚖️ E. Comparative Advertising

- **Definition:** Direct or indirect comparison against a named/unnamed competitor.
- **Direct Comparison:** Names the rival (e.g., Pepsi vs. Coca-Cola).
- **Indirect Comparison:** Implies without naming ("the other leading brand").
- **Regulatory Caveat (KTU 2024 — ASCI Guidelines):**
  - Comparison must be **factual, verifiable, and not disparaging**.
  - In India, **ASCI (Advertising Standards Council of India)** prohibits *disparaging* comparative ads since 2006 amendment.

### 🌟 F. Endorsement Styles

- **Celebrity Endorsement:** High reach, transfer of equity via *Meaning Transfer Model* (McCracken, 1989).
- **Expert Endorsement:** Authority-based credibility (doctors for medicines, engineers for cars).
- **Typical Consumer / "Plain Folks":** Builds relatability (Aamir Khan in Titan Eyecare).
- **CEO / Spokesperson Endorsement:** Trust-based, often for corporate/financial brands.
- **Influencer / UGC:** Modern, native to digital ecosystems.

### 🎬 G. Execution Formats

| **Format** | **Description** | **Best For** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Slice-of-Life | Everyday problem → product solves it | FMCG, household |
| Lifestyle | Aspirational setting | Fashion, luxury |
| Demonstration | Product in action | Cleaning, electronics |
| Fantasy | Imaginary, dreamlike setting | Perfume, beverages |
| Testimonial | Real user story | Services, healthcare |
| Scientific/Technical | Lab-style, data-heavy | Pharma, durables |
| Animation | Cartoons, mascots | Kids' products, complex ideas |

### 📰 H. Copywriting Techniques

- **Headline:** First attention magnet — should communicate the *core promise* in <7 words.
- **Sub-head:** Elaborates the headline.
- **Body Copy:** Builds the argument, story, or facts.
- **Slogan / Tagline:** Memorable phrase anchoring the brand (e.g., Nike — *Just Do It*).
- **Jingle:** Audio mnemonic (e.g., "Hum jolly, hum jolly, Nirma washing powder").
- **Mnemonic Devices:** Alliteration ("Coca-Cola's Classic Charm"), rhyme, repetition.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Model Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following models and ratios are high-yield for KTU 2024 ESE questions on Module 3.**

| **Model / Concept** | **Formula / Notation** | **Application** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| AIDA Hierarchy | $A \rightarrow I \rightarrow D \rightarrow A$ | Stage mapping |
| DAGMAR Stages | $\text{Awareness} \rightarrow \text{Comprehension} \rightarrow \text{Conviction} \rightarrow \text{Action}$ | Goal setting |
| ELM (Petty & Cacioppo) | $\text{Attitude} = f(\text{Route}, \text{Involvement})$ | Route selection |
| Meaning Transfer | $M_{\text{celebrity}} \rightarrow M_{\text{brand}} \rightarrow M_{\text{consumer}}$ | Endorsement logic |
| Fear Appeal Intensity | $F_{\text{opt}} = \text{Severity} \times \text{Susceptibility} \times \text{Efficacy}$ | Optimal fear level |
| Brand Recall Ratio | $R = \dfrac{N_{\text{brand\,recalled}}}{N_{\text{ads\,seen}}} \times 100\%$ | Ad effectiveness |
| Recognition Score | $S_{\text{recog}} = \dfrac{\text{Correct\,identifications}}{\text{Total\,sample}}$ | Copy testing |
| Ad Effectiveness Index | $\text{AEI} = \dfrac{\text{Recall} \times \text{Persuasion}}{\text{Cost\,per\,Thousand}}$ | ROI proxy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary / Sanity Rules for KTU Problems:**
> - Fear appeals: Severity $\in [0,1]$; Susceptibility $\in [0,1]$; Efficacy must be $> 0.5$ to avoid boomerang.
> - Humor should not exceed $\sim 20\%$ of ad duration, otherwise brand message is overshadowed.
> - Comparative ads in India **must avoid disparagement** under ASCI Code (Chapter IV, Clause 4).
> - DAGMAR assumes communication objectives are **specific, measurable, and time-bound**.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Marketing & IMC

| **Industry Sector** | **Preferred Technique** | **Why** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| FMCG (detergents, soaps) | Slice-of-life + Demonstration | Mass reach, simple value proposition |
| Luxury (perfumes, watches) | Lifestyle + Fantasy + Celebrity | Aspirational imagery |
| Insurance & Finance | Fear + Rational + Expert | High involvement, risk-based |
| Healthcare (OTC) | Testimonial + Rational | Trust and efficacy |
| Kids' products | Animation + Mascot + Humor | Attention + simplicity |
| B2B / Industrial | Rational + Technical | Logic-driven purchase |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Decision Matrix

## 3.1 The 5-Step Process of Selecting an Advertising Technique (KTU Analytical Approach)

> [!NOTE]
> **The following step-by-step procedure is a high-frequency KTU 2024 answer structure. Memorize the flow.**

### Step 1 — Diagnose the Target Segment

Identify demographic, psychographic, and behavioral traits. Determine whether the consumer is:
- *Logic-driven* (engineers, finance professionals) → choose **Rational Appeal**.
- *Emotion-driven* (gift buyers, lifestyle consumers) → choose **Emotional Appeal**.
- *Risk-averse* (insurance buyers, parents) → choose **Fear Appeal**.
- *Social-driven* (Gen-Z fashion buyers) → choose **Bandwagon / Influencer**.

### Step 2 — Map to the AIDA / DAGMAR Stage

| **Current Consumer Stage** | **Required Technique Class** |
|:---|:---|
| Awareness | Attention-grabbing — Humor, Shock, Novel visuals |
| Interest | Informational, Demonstration |
| Desire | Emotional, Lifestyle, Fantasy |
| Action | Scarcity, Urgency, Comparative, Strong CTA |

### Step 3 — Apply the ELM (Elaboration Likelihood Model)

If consumer **involvement is HIGH** → **Central route** → Rational + Comparative + Demonstration.

If consumer **involvement is LOW** → **Peripheral route** → Emotional + Humor + Celebrity + Music.

### Step 4 — Select the Execution Format

Match format to product category (refer Section 2.2 G).

### Step 5 — Validate Against Regulatory & Ethical Standards

- **ASCI Code (India)** compliance.
- Avoid *disparaging* comparisons.
- Ensure *substantiation* of all claims.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation — Optimal Fear Appeal Intensity

In fear-based advertising, the **Effectiveness of the Ad ($E$)** is a function of three factors:

$$
E = f(S, \text{Susc}, \text{Eff})
$$

where:
- $S$ = **Severity** (perceived seriousness of the threat)
- $\text{Susc}$ = **Susceptibility** (perceived personal vulnerability)
- $\text{Eff}$ = **Efficacy** (perceived ability of the product to remove the threat)

The expanded KTU-formulation is:

$$
E = (S \times \text{Susc}) \times \text{Eff}
$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Define severity on a normalized scale:**

Let $S \in [0, 1]$, where $0 = \text{no threat perceived}$, $1 = \text{extreme threat}$.

**Step 2 — Define susceptibility similarly:**

Let $\text{Susc} \in [0, 1]$, where $0 = \text{"it can't happen to me"}$, $1 = \text{"very likely to happen to me"}$.

**Step 3 — Compute threat salience:**

$$
\text{Threat Salience} = S \times \text{Susc}
$$

This captures the *intensity* of perceived danger.

**Step 4 — Weight by response efficacy:**

$$
E = \text{Threat Salience} \times \text{Eff} = (S \times \text{Susc}) \times \text{Eff}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Boomerang Threshold:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boomerang Condition (KTU High-Yield Rule):**
> If $S > 0.8$ AND $\text{Eff} < 0.5$, the ad will produce **avoidance, denial, or counter-arguments** — the *boomerang effect*.
> Therefore, the validity region for a successful fear ad is:
> $$
> S \leq 0.8 \quad \text{AND} \quad \text{Eff} > 0.5
> $$

### Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style):

Suppose a road-safety ad has:
- Severity $S = 0.7$
- Susceptibility $\text{Susc} = 0.6$
- Efficacy $\text{Eff} = 0.8$

Compute:

$$
E = (0.7 \times 0.6) \times 0.8
$$

$$
E = 0.42 \times 0.8 = 0.336
$$

Since $S = 0.7 \leq 0.8$ and $\text{Eff} = 0.8 > 0.5$, the fear ad is **within the safe region** — no boomerang expected.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting the Right Technique

Use this decision matrix to determine the optimal technique for any product:

| **Product/Service** | **Involvement** | **Risk** | **Audience Mood** | **Recommended Technique** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| Premium perfume | High | Low | Aspirational | Lifestyle + Celebrity + Fantasy |
| Term insurance | High | High | Anxious | Fear + Expert + Testimonial |
| Detergent | Low | Low | Routine | Slice-of-life + Demonstration |
| Energy drink | Low | Low | Energetic | Humor + Lifestyle + Music |
| Children's cereal | Low | Low | Playful | Animation + Mascot + Humor |
| Industrial CNC machine | High | High | Analytical | Rational + Comparative + Technical |
| Smartphone | High | Medium | Aspirational | Lifestyle + Demonstration + Celebrity |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When a 14-mark question asks "Suggest an advertising technique for XYZ product," the examiner expects:
> 1. Identify the *target audience* and *involvement level*.
> 2. Map to AIDA stage.
> 3. State the **technique** with **justification**.
> 4. Give a **creative brief outline** (headline, visual, body copy).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flow — Advertising Technique Selection Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: New Advertising Campaign] --> B[Step 1: Identify Target Segment and Buying Motive]
    B --> C{Is Purchase Logic Driven?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Choose Rational or Informational Appeal]
    C -- No --> E{Is Purchase Emotion Driven?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Choose Emotional Appeal: Love, Pride, Joy]
    E -- No --> G{Is Purchase Risk Driven?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Choose Fear Appeal with High Efficacy]
    G -- No --> I[Choose Social Appeal: Bandwagon or Snob]
    D --> J[Step 2: Map to AIDA or DAGMAR Stage]
    F --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Step 3: Select Execution Format]
    K --> L[Slice of Life]
    K --> M[Demonstration]
    K --> N[Fantasy or Lifestyle]
    K --> O[Testimonial]
    L --> P[Step 4: Validate Against ASCI Code]
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Step 5: Final Creative Brief and Copywriting]
    Q --> R[End: Ad Ready for Production]
```

## 4.2 AIDA Hierarchy with Matching Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Attention] --> A2[Interest]
    A2 --> A3[Desire]
    A3 --> A4[Action]
    A1 -.Uses.-> T1[Humor and Shock]
    A2 -.Uses.-> T2[Rational and Demonstration]
    A3 -.Uses.-> T3[Emotional and Lifestyle]
    A4 -.Uses.-> T4[Scarcity, Urgency, CTA]
```

## 4.3 Meaning Transfer Model (Celebrity Endorsement Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Celebrity Equity] --> C2[Brand Association]
    C2 --> C3[Consumer Meaning]
    C1 --> M1[Source Credibility]
    M1 --> C2
    C2 --> M2[Brand Trust]
    M2 --> C3
```

## 4.4 ELM Route Selection Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H[High Involvement Consumer] --> CR[Central Route Processing]
    CR --> RA[Rational Appeal]
    CR --> CO[Comparative Advertising]
    CR --> DE[Detailed Demonstration]
    L[Low Involvement Consumer] --> PR[Peripheral Route Processing]
    PR --> EM[Emotional Appeal]
    PR --> HU[Humor]
    PR --> CE[Celebrity Endorsement]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The advertiser's first diagnostic step is to determine the consumer's *involvement level*. High involvement products (cars, insurance, electronics) drive *central route processing* — rational arguments win. Low involvement products (snacks, beverages, cosmetics) rely on *peripheral cues* — emotions, music, celebrities.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🎯 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

**Define advertising appeals. Differentiate between informational and emotional appeals with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Definition (1 Mark):** Advertising appeals are the central persuasive themes or hook points designed to motivate consumer interest and action. They represent the *strategic intent* of the ad message.
- **Informational Appeal (1 Mark):** Based on logic, facts, and product attributes. Targets the *rational mind*. Example: A car ad quoting mileage of 22 km/l and 5-star safety rating.
- **Emotional Appeal (1 Mark):** Triggers feelings like joy, love, pride, or nostalgia. Targets the *heart*. Example: Cadbury's "Kuch Meetha Ho Jaaye" ad evoking family warmth and celebration.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**What is the AIDA model? Explain any two advertising techniques suitable for the "Attention" stage.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **AIDA Definition (1 Mark):** AIDA stands for *Attention → Interest → Desire → Action*. It is a hierarchical model describing the sequential psychological stages a consumer passes through before purchase.
- **Technique 1 (1 Mark):** **Humor Appeal** — A witty, comic opening grabs attention quickly (e.g., Fevicol's absurd humor).
- **Technique 2 (1 Mark):** **Shock or Novel Visual** — A startling image or unusual visual disrupts consumer *ad-clutter blindness* (e.g., PETA's bold campaigns).

---

## 🎯 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice]

**(a)** Explain the **Elaboration Likelihood Model (ELM)** proposed by Petty and Cacioppo. Discuss how it helps marketers select between rational and emotional advertising techniques. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design an advertising campaign for a **new electric scooter** targeted at urban Indian millennials. Specify the appeal, execution format, headline, body copy idea, and media placement. Justify each choice. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution:

**(a) ELM Explanation (7 Marks):**

The **Elaboration Likelihood Model (ELM)**, developed by Richard Petty and John Cacioppo in the 1980s, explains how attitudes are formed and changed through advertising. It proposes **two routes of persuasion**:

**Step 1 — Central Route (1 Mark):**
When consumers are *highly involved* and *motivated*, they engage in careful, thoughtful evaluation of the message. Arguments, evidence, and logic are persuasive. Example: A car buyer comparing safety ratings and mileage.

**Step 2 — Peripheral Route (1 Mark):**
When consumers are *low involvement* or *distracted*, they rely on superficial cues — celebrity faces, music, humor, color, brand logos — to form attitudes. Example: A casual snack buyer choosing a packet based on a celebrity's smile.

**Step 3 — Application to Ad Selection (2 Marks):**

| **Route** | **Involvement** | **Preferred Technique** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Central | High | Rational, Comparative, Demonstration |
| Peripheral | Low | Emotional, Humor, Celebrity |

**Step 4 — Marketer's Decision Rule (1.5 Marks):**
Marketers first assess *involvement level*. High-involvement products (cars, insurance, B2B) → use **central route techniques**. Low-involvement products (FMCG, snacks, soft drinks) → use **peripheral route techniques**.

**Step 5 — Strategic Implication (1.5 Marks):**
ELM tells us that **one ad cannot work for both routes**. Marketers must design *different messages* for the same brand if targeting both high- and low-involvement segments — explaining why a car brand uses both technical specs (for buyers) and celebrity aspirational imagery (for lifestyle buyers).

**(b) Campaign Design for Electric Scooter (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Target Segment Definition (1 Mark):**
Urban Indian millennials, age 22–35, environmentally conscious, tech-savvy, budget ₹1–1.5 lakh, commute 10–25 km daily.

**Step 2 — Buying Motive (1 Mark):**
Hybrid motive — *rational* (cost-saving on fuel) + *emotional* (eco-pride, modern lifestyle).

**Step 3 — Appeal Selection (1 Mark):** **Emotional + Rational Hybrid Appeal** — combining environmental pride (emotional) with cost-per-km (rational).

**Step 4 — Execution Format (1 Mark):** **Lifestyle + Demonstration Hybrid** — show urban millennials riding through city streets (lifestyle), then cut to dashboard display showing ₹0.5/km cost (demonstration).

**Step 5 — Headline (1 Mark):**
*"Charge Less. Breathe More."* — combines economic + environmental twin benefits in 4 words.

**Step 6 — Body Copy Idea (1 Mark):**
"At just ₹0.5/km and zero tailpipe emissions, your daily ride now costs less than your morning chai — and your city thanks you for every silent kilometer."

**Step 7 — Media Placement (1 Mark):**
Instagram Reels, YouTube pre-rolls (mobile-first), OOH billboards near metro stations, and influencer collaborations with eco-tech YouTubers.

---

### **Question B.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice — Alternative]

**(a)** Discuss **fear appeals** in advertising with suitable examples. Under what conditions do fear appeals produce a *boomerang effect*? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Critically analyze **celebrity endorsement** as an advertising technique using the *Meaning Transfer Model*. Identify two risks associated with it. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution:

**(a) Fear Appeals (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 Mark):**
A fear appeal is an advertising technique that arouses anxiety or concern about potential negative consequences of *not* using the product, motivating consumers toward protective action.

**Step 2 — Psychological Basis (1 Mark):**
Rooted in the **Drive Reduction Model** — fear creates a psychological tension (drive) that the consumer reduces by adopting the recommended behavior.

**Step 3 — Components (1 Mark):**
Effective fear appeals have three components:
- **Threat Severity** — How bad is the consequence?
- **Threat Susceptibility** — How likely is it to happen to me?
- **Response Efficacy** — Will the product's solution actually remove the threat?

**Step 4 — Examples (1 Mark):**
- Road safety ads showing accident trauma.
- Anti-smoking campaigns showing lung damage.
- Insurance ads showing hospital bills and family distress.

**Step 5 — Boomerang Effect Definition (1 Mark):**
When fear is **too intense** but efficacy is **too low**, consumers reject the message — they engage in *denial, avoidance, or counter-argument*. This is called the **boomerang effect**.

**Step 6 — Boomerang Conditions (1 Mark):**
Mathematically expressed as:
$$
S > 0.8 \quad \text{AND} \quad \text{Eff} < 0.5
$$
That is, extremely threatening ads with no clear, believable solution cause psychological *reactance*.

**Step 7 — Solution (1 Mark):**
Pair every fear appeal with a **clear, actionable solution** and an **efficacy message** (e.g., "wear a seat-belt — reduces injury by 60%").

**(b) Celebrity Endorsement with Meaning Transfer Model (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Celebrity Endorsement Definition (1 Mark):**
Celebrity endorsement is an advertising technique where a *famous public figure* lends their image, credibility, and equity to a brand to influence consumer attitudes and purchase decisions.

**Step 2 — Meaning Transfer Model — Concept (1.5 Marks):**
Proposed by **Grant McCracken (1989)**, the Meaning Transfer Model explains *how* celebrity endorsement works in three stages:
- **Stage 1:** Celebrity accumulates cultural meanings through films, public life, and reputation.
- **Stage 2:** These meanings transfer to the brand via the endorsement ad.
- **Stage 3:** Consumers acquire those meanings when they purchase and use the brand.

**Step 3 — Model Representation (1 Mark):**
$$
M_{\text{celebrity}} \rightarrow M_{\text{brand}} \rightarrow M_{\text{consumer}}
$$

**Step 4 — Application (1 Mark):**
Example: Shah Rukh Khan endorsers for many brands — his "king of romance" image transfers meanings of love, premium-ness, and aspiration to the endorsed brand, which the consumer then adopts as part of their self-identity.

**Step 5 — Critical Evaluation (1 Mark):**
Celebrity endorsement boosts recall, builds trust rapidly, and enhances brand image. However, it is **expensive**, **risky**, and **short-term** unless aligned with brand positioning.

**Step 6 — Risk 1 — Image Transfer Risk (0.75 Marks):**
If the celebrity's image is damaged (scandal, controversy, legal case), the **negative meaning also transfers** to the brand, potentially causing brand equity damage.

**Step 7 — Risk 2 — Overshadowing Risk (0.75 Marks):**
The celebrity's popularity may *overshadow the product* — consumers remember the star, not the brand. The brand becomes a *prop* in the celebrity's ad rather than the hero.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on Advertising Technique Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "appeal" with "execution format."** Appeal = *what* the ad says (emotional, rational, fear). Execution format = *how* it is shown (slice-of-life, demonstration, fantasy). Examiners deduct marks if these are mixed.
> 2. **Naming techniques without justification.** Writing "use celebrity endorsement" without explaining *why* it suits the product loses 50% of the marks. Always tie technique to target audience + involvement level.
> 3. **Ignoring ASCI / regulatory compliance.** In comparative advertising or claim-based ads, failing to mention *substantiation* and *non-disparagement* is a major deduction trigger.
> 4. **Skipping AIDA / DAGMAR mapping.** Most 14-mark questions expect a *framework-based answer*, not just a list. Frame every answer around a model.
> 5. **Forgetting the boomerang condition.** When writing about fear appeals, students often describe *only the benefits* and forget to discuss *when fear fails*.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Advertising Techniques (Module 3):**
>
> ✅ Advertising techniques are **strategic tools** used to encode brand messages and trigger consumer response.
>
> ✅ Major **appeal categories**: Informational, Emotional, Moral, Humor, Fear, Comparative.
>
> ✅ **AIDA model** — Attention → Interest → Desire → Action — guides stage-wise technique selection.
>
> ✅ **DAGMAR** — Awareness → Comprehension → Conviction → Action — sets measurable communication objectives.
>
> ✅ **ELM (Petty & Cacioppo)** — Central route (rational) for high involvement; Peripheral route (emotional/celebrity) for low involvement.
>
> ✅ **Fear appeals** — effective only when Severity ≤ 0.8 AND Efficacy > 0.5; otherwise **boomerang effect**.
>
> ✅ **Meaning Transfer Model (McCracken)** — $M_{\text{celebrity}} \rightarrow M_{\text{brand}} \rightarrow M_{\text{consumer}}$ — explains how celebrity equity transfers to brand equity.
>
> ✅ **Comparative advertising** in India must follow **ASCI Code** — factual, verifiable, non-disparaging.
>
> ✅ **Execution formats**: Slice-of-life, Lifestyle, Demonstration, Fantasy, Testimonial, Scientific/Technical, Animation.
>
> ✅ **Copywriting devices**: Headline, sub-head, body copy, slogan, jingle — each with a specific function.
>
> ✅ **Humor rule of thumb**: ~20% humor + ~80% brand content — never let comedy overshadow the brand.
>
> ✅ **Bandwagon, Snob, and Plain-Folk** appeals tap *social influence* — conformity, exclusivity, relatability.
>
> ✅ Always tie **technique selection** to **target audience + AIDA stage + product involvement level**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advertising Media
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT742 - MARKETING COMMUNICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Advertising Media

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Advertising Media

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Advertising Media** refers to the various communication channels or vehicles that marketers and advertisers use to deliver promotional messages to their target audiences. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for Marketing Communications, advertising media encompasses the **paid, non-personal presentation of ideas, goods, or services by an identified sponsor** through mass self-communicated channels.

The American Marketing Association (AMA) defines advertising media as the **"means by which advertising messages are transmitted to the target audience, including print, broadcast, outdoor, digital, and direct response channels."**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Advertising Media**
> In the KTU 2024 PECBT742 syllabus, Advertising Media is covered as a core component of Module 3, focusing on the **classification, selection criteria, and effectiveness measurement** of media vehicles used in integrated marketing communications (IMC).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a fisherman trying to catch fish. You have a choice of tools:
- A **fishing rod** (targeted, personal reach)
- A **large net** (broad reach, less targeted)
- A **fish trap** (passive, requires the right location)

Each tool is a "medium" for reaching fish. Similarly, in advertising:
- **Print ads** (newspapers, magazines) = fishing rod → targeted, niche audiences
- **TV commercials** = large net → massive reach, broad impact
- **Billboards** = fish trap → placed in high-traffic "locations" (highways, cities)

The **art of advertising media selection** is choosing the right "fishing tool" based on:
- What kind of "fish" (audience) you want
- What "bait" (message) you have
- The "water conditions" (market environment)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction**: *Media* = the broad category (TV, Print, Digital). *Media Vehicle* = the specific carrier (e.g., Times of India, NDTV, Instagram Reels). KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.

## 1.3 Primary Classification of Advertising Media

| **Broad Category** | **Specific Media Types** | **Reach Characteristics** |
|---|---|---|
| **Print Media** | Newspapers, Magazines, Journals, Brochures, Direct Mail | Local/National, Long shelf life |
| **Broadcast Media** | Television, Radio | Mass reach, High impact |
| **Outdoor Media** | Billboards, Hoardings, Transit Ads, Posters | Geographic, Repetitive exposure |
| **Digital Media** | Social Media, Search Engines, Display Ads, Email, OTT | Targeted, Interactive, Measurable |
| **In-Store / Point-of-Purchase** | Shelf displays, Window displays, Counter displays | High purchase-intent audiences |
| **Cinema / Theatre** | Pre-roll ads, In-theatre promotions | Captive audience, Premium positioning |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Metric to Remember**: The **CPM (Cost Per Mille / Cost Per Thousand)** is the universal benchmark used in KTU 2024 assessments to compare media cost efficiency. Standard CPM benchmarks: TV = ₹200–₹500, Print = ₹80–₹200, Digital = ₹20–₹100.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Media Selection Process

The media selection process in advertising follows a structured, analytical workflow. Each step builds upon the previous one:

### Step 1: Identify the Target Audience
- **Why**: You cannot select media without knowing WHO you are reaching.
- **How**: Use demographic, psychographic, and behavioral segmentation. Tools include customer personas, market research, and CRM data.
- **KTU Insight**: This step is foundational and carries weight in Part A (3-mark) questions.

### Step 2: Define Media Objectives
- **Why**: Objectives determine whether the campaign needs reach, frequency, or continuity.
- **How**: Set SMART (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) goals such as:
  - Reach 70% of urban millennials in Kerala within 30 days
  - Generate 1 million impressions at CPM ≤ ₹150
  - Achieve 3+ exposures per viewer (effective frequency)

### Step 3: Determine Media Budget
- **Why**: Budget acts as a hard constraint on media choices.
- **How**: Apply budgeting methods:
  - **Percentage-of-Sales Method**: Budget = X% of projected sales
  - **Objective-and-Task Method**: Budget = Cost of activities to achieve objectives
  - **Competitive Parity Method**: Budget = Match competitor's spend
  - **Affordable Method**: Budget = What remains after other costs

### Step 4: Select Media Categories
- **Why**: Choose between broad categories (TV, Print, Digital, etc.).
- **How**: Apply the **3R Framework**:
  - **Reach** — % of target audience exposed
  - **Recency** — when was the audience exposed
  - **Relevance** — alignment with audience and message

### Step 5: Choose Specific Media Vehicles
- **Why**: Within each category, pick exact carriers (e.g., Mathrubhumi vs. Manorama).
- **How**: Evaluate based on cost, audience profile, editorial environment, and past performance.

### Step 6: Optimize the Media Schedule
- **Why**: Timing and continuity affect campaign effectiveness.
- **How**: Choose schedule patterns:
  - **Flighting**: Heavy bursts, then pauses
  - **Pulsing**: Continuous + bursts
  - **Continuous**: Steady, even exposure
  - **Concentrated**: Intense in a single period

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cost Per Mille (CPM)** | $CPM = \dfrac{\text{Cost of ad}}{\text{Impressions}} \times 1000$ | ₹ per 1000 impressions | Comparing cost efficiency across media |
| **Reach** | $R = \dfrac{\text{Unique Audience Exposed}}{\text{Total Target Population}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | Measures unduplicated audience |
| **Frequency (Average)** | $F = \dfrac{\text{Total Impressions (GRPs)}}{\text{Reach (\%)}}$ | Exposures per person | Average number of times a person sees the ad |
| **Gross Rating Points (GRPs)** | $GRP = R \times F$ | Numeric (no unit) | Total advertising weight delivered |
| **Target Rating Points (TRPs)** | $TRP = \dfrac{\text{Target Audience Exposures}}{\text{Target Population}} \times 100$ | Numeric | TV-specific metric for target audience |
| **Cost Per Click (CPC)** | $CPC = \dfrac{\text{Total Ad Spend}}{\text{Number of Clicks}}$ | ₹ per click | Digital media performance metric |
| **Click-Through Rate (CTR)** | $CTR = \dfrac{\text{Clicks}}{\text{Impressions}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | Digital ad engagement measure |
| **Effective Reach** | $ER = R_{\geq 3}$ (% exposed 3+ times) | Percentage (%) | Identifies optimal exposure threshold |
| **Share of Voice (SOV)** | $SOV = \dfrac{\text{Brand's Ad Spend}}{\text{Total Category Ad Spend}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | Competitive advertising presence |
| **Return on Ad Spend (ROAS)** | $ROAS = \dfrac{\text{Revenue from Ads}}{\text{Ad Spend}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) | Marketing profitability metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Warning**: In table-based answers, students often confuse **Reach × Frequency = GRPs** with **Impressions**. Remember: Impressions = Reach × Frequency × Population (as raw number), while GRPs = Reach × Frequency (as percentage points).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern marketing engineering, these formulas drive:
1. **Marketing Mix Modeling (MMM)** — Statistical models that quantify media impact on sales.
2. **Multi-Touch Attribution (MTA)** — Assigns credit to each media touchpoint in the customer journey.
3. **Programmatic Advertising Algorithms** — Real-time bidding systems use CPM, CTR, and conversion data to optimize ad delivery.
4. **Media Planning Software** (e.g., MediaPlan, Nielsen Ad Intel) — Automates calculations of GRPs, reach curves, and budget allocation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in KTU 2024**: The syllabus explicitly links advertising media to **digital transformation** in marketing. Understanding these formulas prepares you for case-study questions on FMCG, e-commerce, and tech industry media plans.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: CPM Calculation (Detailed Derivation)

**Problem Statement**: A leading FMCG brand (Kerala Banana Chips Co.) plans to insert a full-page color advertisement in *Mathrubhumi* (Kerala's leading Malayalam daily). The newspaper's advertising rate card specifies:
- Full-page color ad cost = **₹2,00,000**
- Average readership per issue = **8,00,000 readers**
- Estimated impressions per reader (multiple readers per copy) = **1.5**

**Step 1: Identify the Given Data**
- Cost of ad (C) = ₹2,00,000
- Readership (R) = 8,00,000
- Impressions per reader (k) = 1.5

**Step 2: Calculate Total Impressions**
Total impressions represent the total number of times the ad is potentially seen.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Impressions} &= \text{Readership} \times \text{Impressions per reader} \\
&= 8{,}00{,}000 \times 1.5 \\
&= 12{,}00{,}000 \text{ impressions}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the CPM Formula**
CPM is the cost incurred for every 1,000 impressions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPM &= \frac{\text{Cost of ad}}{\text{Total Impressions}} \times 1000 \\
&= \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} \times 1000 \\
&= \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{1200} \\
&= 166.67 \text{ ₹ per 1000 impressions}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Interpret the Result**
A CPM of ₹166.67 means the brand pays ₹166.67 to reach 1,000 readers through this newspaper ad. This can be benchmarked against:
- *Manorama* (competing daily): CPM = ₹155
- *Deshabhimani* (regional daily): CPM = ₹120
- Facebook Display Ad (digital): CPM = ₹45

**Conclusion**: *Mathrubhumi* is competitively priced but not the cheapest. The final choice depends on audience profile alignment and campaign objectives.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Reach, Frequency, and GRP (Detailed Derivation)

**Problem Statement**: A telecom company launches a 4-week TV campaign on Asianet (Malayalam entertainment channel) with the following data:
- Target population in Kerala = **60,00,000 viewers**
- Unique viewers reached (unduplicated) = **36,00,000**
- Total ad spots aired = **108** (3 spots/day × 6 days/week × 4 weeks × 1.5 effective spots)

**Step 1: Calculate Reach**
Reach is the percentage of the target audience exposed to the ad at least once.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reach (\%)} &= \frac{\text{Unique Viewers Exposed}}{\text{Total Target Population}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{36{,}00{,}000}{60{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 60\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Total Impressions (TRP × Population / 100 approach)**
For TV media, total impressions = TRP × Target Population / 100.

First, we need the **TRP per spot**. Given that the channel has an average TVR (Television Viewership Rating) of 4 for the time slot, and 108 spots were aired:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TRP Sum} &= \text{Average TVR} \times \text{Number of spots} \\
&= 4 \times 108 \\
&= 432 \text{ TRPs}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Impressions} &= \frac{TRP \times \text{Target Population}}{100} \\
&= \frac{432 \times 60{,}00{,}000}{100} \\
&= 2{,}59{,}20{,}00{,}000 \text{ impressions}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Average Frequency**
Frequency represents the average number of times each unique viewer sees the ad.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Frequency} &= \frac{\text{Total Impressions}}{\text{Unique Viewers}} \\
&= \frac{2{,}59{,}20{,}00{,}000}{36{,}00{,}000} \\
&= 7.2 \text{ exposures per viewer}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate GRPs**
GRP is the product of Reach (as a number) and Frequency.

$$
\begin{aligned}
GRP &= R \times F \\
&= 60 \times 7.2 \\
&= 432 \text{ GRPs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Cross-Verification**
GRP should equal the sum of TRPs (since TVR and Reach% are mathematically equivalent at the population level).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Verification: } GRP &= 432 = \text{TRP Sum} = 432 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion**: The campaign delivers 60% reach at 7.2 average frequency. According to the **Effective Frequency Theory** (Herbert Krugman: 3 exposures), this campaign exceeds the optimal threshold, indicating efficient media weight delivery.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Budget Allocation Using Objective-and-Task Method

**Problem Statement**: An EdTech startup in Kochi has ₹15,00,000 to allocate across three media:
- **Digital (Instagram + Google Ads)**: ₹6,00,000
- **Print (The Hindu Education supplement)**: ₹4,00,000
- **Radio (Red FM morning show)**: ₹3,00,000
- **Outdoor (Kochi Metro stations)**: ₹2,00,000

Expected reach and conversion metrics:

| **Media** | **Reach** | **CPC/Engagement Cost** | **Expected Clicks/Responses** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Digital | 5,00,000 | ₹20 per click | 30,000 |
