| Print | 2,00,000 | ₹50 per response | 8,000 |
| Radio | 8,00,000 | ₹100 per response | 3,000 |
| Outdoor | 1,50,000 | ₹40 per QR scan | 5,000 |

**Step 1: Calculate CPM for Each Medium**

For Digital:
$$
\begin{aligned}
CPM_{Digital} &= \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 1000 = ₹1200
\end{aligned}
$$

For Print:
$$
\begin{aligned}
CPM_{Print} &= \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} \times 1000 = ₹2000
\end{aligned}
$$

For Radio:
$$
\begin{aligned}
CPM_{Radio} &= \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} \times 1000 = ₹375
\end{aligned}
$$

For Outdoor:
$$
\begin{aligned}
CPM_{Outdoor} &= \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{1{,}50{,}000} \times 1000 = ₹1333.33
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Cost Per Acquisition (CPA) — Assuming a 5% conversion rate from responses**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPA_{Digital} &= \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{30{,}000 \times 0.05} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{1500} = ₹400 \\
CPA_{Print} &= \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{8{,}000 \times 0.05} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{400} = ₹1000 \\
CPA_{Radio} &= \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{3{,}000 \times 0.05} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{150} = ₹2000 \\
CPA_{Outdoor} &= \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{5{,}000 \times 0.05} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{250} = ₹800
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Analytical Interpretation**
- **Lowest CPA** = Digital (₹400) → Most efficient for direct conversions
- **Lowest CPM** = Radio (₹375) → Most cost-efficient for awareness
- **Best Reach-to-Cost Ratio** = Radio → Best for mass awareness
- **Most Balanced** = Digital → Combines reach, engagement, and measurability

**Step 4: Recommended Reallocation**
Shift 30% of Radio budget (₹90,000) to Digital to maximize conversions while maintaining brand awareness:
- New Digital budget = ₹6,90,000
- New Radio budget = ₹2,10,000

This reallocation increases expected conversions by 4,500 while keeping reach above 10,00,000.

# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Differentiate between Advertising Media and Media Vehicle with suitable examples."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

| **Aspect** | **Advertising Media** | **Media Vehicle** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | The broad category or channel of communication | The specific carrier within that category |
| Scope | General classification | Specific, identifiable product |
| Example (Category) | Television | Asianet, Manorama News, NDTV |
| Example (Category) | Newspaper | The Hindu, Mathrubhumi, Times of India |
| Example (Category) | Digital/Social Media | Facebook, Instagram, YouTube |

**1-Mark Justification**: Media is the **macro** category; Vehicle is the **micro** specific choice. KTU expects students to give at least 2 paired examples for full marks.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Explain the concept of CPM and its significance in media planning."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
- **Definition (1 Mark)**: CPM stands for **Cost Per Mille** (or Cost Per Thousand). It represents the cost incurred to deliver an advertisement to 1,000 members of the target audience.
- **Formula (1 Mark)**:

$$
CPM = \frac{\text{Cost of Advertisement}}{\text{Total Impressions}} \times 1000
$$

- **Significance (1 Mark)**: CPM enables **cross-media comparison**. A marketer can compare the cost-efficiency of a ₹2 lakh newspaper ad versus a ₹2 lakh TV ad by computing both CPMs and choosing the lower one, assuming the target audience is reached effectively by both.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3 Choice]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**"Discuss the various types of advertising media with their advantages and limitations. (7 Marks)"**

**Model Answer**:

**1. Print Media** (Newspapers, Magazines, Journals)
- **Advantages**: High credibility, long shelf life, detailed information, targeted local reach, lower cost for regional markets.
- **Limitations**: Short attention span of readers, no audio/video, declining circulation, passive consumption.

**2. Broadcast Media** (Television, Radio)
- **Advantages**: TV offers mass reach, audio-visual impact, emotional storytelling. Radio is cost-effective, mobile-friendly, and excellent for rural markets.
- **Limitations**: High production costs (TV), no permanent record, ad-skipping, ad clutter, intrusive perception.

**3. Outdoor Media** (Billboards, Hoardings, Transit)
- **Advantages**: 24/7 visibility, geographic targeting, repetitive exposure, cost-effective over time.
- **Limitations**: Limited message length, no detailed information, weather/lighting issues, no interaction.

**4. Digital Media** (Social Media, Search, Email, OTT)
- **Advantages**: Precise targeting, measurability, interactivity, real-time optimization, global reach at low cost.
- **Limitations**: Ad fatigue, ad blockers, fraud/bot traffic, privacy regulations, short attention spans, requires digital literacy.

**5. In-Store / Point-of-Purchase Media** (Displays, Shelf Talkers)
- **Advantages**: Captures buyer at decision moment, impulse-trigger, no media cost for store-owned displays.
- **Limitations**: Limited to retail locations, requires staff training, passive medium.

**6. Cinema / Theatre Media**
- **Advantages**: Captive audience, premium positioning, high recall, immersive large-screen experience.
- **Limitations**: Limited audience size, high relative cost per viewer, restricted to urban multiplexes.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Tip**: For full 7 marks, list at least 5 media types with both an advantage and a limitation. Add 1 example of an Indian brand for each to score extra.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**"Calculate the GRP, Reach, and Average Frequency for the following advertising campaign and interpret the results. (7 Marks)"**

**Given Data**:
- Total target population = 50,00,000
- Number of ad spots aired = 50
- Average TVR per spot = 5
- Unique audience reached = 25,00,000

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1: Calculate Total Impressions (1 Mark)**

$$
\text{Total Impressions} = \frac{TRP \times \text{Target Population}}{100}
$$

First, TRP sum = Average TVR × Number of spots = 5 × 50 = 250

$$
\text{Total Impressions} = \frac{250 \times 50{,}00{,}000}{100} = 12{,}50{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Reach % (1 Mark)**

$$
\text{Reach (\%)} = \frac{25{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 50\%
$$

**Step 3: Calculate GRP (1 Mark)**

$$
GRP = R \times F
$$

We need frequency first.

**Step 4: Calculate Average Frequency (2 Marks)**

$$
\text{Frequency} = \frac{\text{Total Impressions}}{\text{Unique Audience}} = \frac{12{,}50{,}00{,}000}{25{,}00{,}000} = 5
$$

**Step 5: Calculate GRP (1 Mark)**

$$
GRP = 50 \times 5 = 250
$$

**Step 6: Interpretation (1 Mark)**
- The campaign reaches **50% of the target population** with an average exposure of **5 times per person**.
- Total advertising weight = **250 GRPs**, which is considered a **medium-to-high weight campaign** for a regional launch.
- Since frequency is 5, it exceeds Herbert Krugman's **3-exposure effective frequency threshold**, ensuring strong message retention and recall.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [Alternative Choice for Internal Option]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**"Explain the media selection process. What are the key factors a media planner must consider? (7 Marks)"**

**Model Answer**:

**The Media Selection Process** is a systematic, multi-step methodology used by media planners to identify the optimal mix of media vehicles to deliver the advertising message effectively.

**Key Steps** (5 marks for steps + 2 marks for factors):

1. **Define the Target Audience**: Identify demographics (age, income, gender), psychographics (lifestyle, values), and media consumption habits.

2. **Establish Media Objectives**: Set clear goals — awareness (high reach), persuasion (high frequency), or sales conversion (action-driven).

3. **Determine the Media Budget**: Apply budgeting methods such as **Objective-and-Task**, **Percentage-of-Sales**, or **Competitive Parity**.

4. **Select Media Categories**: Choose broad media types (TV, Print, Digital, OOH, Radio) based on audience-media fit.

5. **Choose Specific Media Vehicles**: Within each category, evaluate and select specific carriers (e.g., Mathrubhumi vs. Manorama).

6. **Optimize the Media Schedule**: Decide on schedule type (Continuous, Flighting, Pulsing, Concentrated).

**Key Factors for Consideration** (2 marks):
- **Cost (CPM)**: Cost efficiency per thousand impressions.
- **Reach & Frequency**: Coverage and repetition targets.
- **Target Audience Match**: Whether the media's audience profile aligns with the target consumer.
- **Message-Media Fit**: Complex messages need print/digital; emotional stories need TV.
- **Competitive Activity**: Share of voice relative to competitors.
- **Editorial Environment**: Brand safety and context relevance.
- **Geographic Coverage**: Local vs. national vs. global.
- **Flexibility & Lead Time**: How quickly the ad can be launched.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**"A cosmetics brand plans to launch a new lipstick line targeting urban women aged 25–40. Design a media plan with a budget of ₹20 lakhs. Justify your media mix. (7 Marks)"**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1: Audience Analysis (1 Mark)**
- Urban women, 25–40, middle-to-high income, digital-first, value aesthetics and trends.
- Media habits: Heavy social media (Instagram, YouTube), OTT streaming, metro outdoor exposure, selective magazine readers.

**Step 2: Recommended Media Mix (4 Marks)**:

| **Media** | **Budget (₹)** | **% Allocation** | **Rationale** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Instagram & YouTube Influencer Ads | 7,00,000 | 35% | Highly visual platform; ideal for cosmetic product demos |
| Google Search & Display Ads | 4,00,000 | 20% | Captures intent-driven beauty searchers |
| OTT Platforms (Hotstar, Netflix pre-roll) | 3,00,000 | 15% | Reaches affluent female viewers during prime time |
| Magazines (Femina, Vogue India) | 3,00,000 | 15% | Premium positioning, aspirational audience |
| Outdoor (Metro stations, malls) | 2,00,000 | 10% | Urban visibility, repeated exposure |
| Radio (Red FM, Radio Mirchi) | 1,00,000 | 5% | Reinforcement and frequency building |

**Step 3: Media Schedule (1 Mark)**
- **Schedule Type**: Pulsing
- **Base Spend**: 60% continuous across 3 months
- **Peak Spend**: 40% during festive/launch weeks

**Step 4: Expected Outcomes (1 Mark)**
- **Reach**: ~70% of urban women 25–40 in target metros
- **Average Frequency**: 4 exposures
- **GRPs**: 280
- **Digital CPM benchmark**: ₹80–₹150 (efficient for a ₹20L budget)

**Justification**: Digital-first allocation (70%) aligns with the audience's media consumption. Print and outdoor provide premium brand cues, while radio supplements frequency. The pulsing schedule ensures sustained awareness with concentrated launch impact.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing Reach with Impressions**: Reach is unduplicated audience; Impressions include repetitions.
> 2. **Forgetting the ×1000 in CPM**: Students often compute cost per impression and forget to multiply by 1,000. This loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up GRP and TRP**: GRP = general audience exposure; TRP = target audience only. KTU frequently tests this distinction.
> 4. **Skipping Interpretation in Calculations**: Showing numbers without interpreting them ("campaign reaches 50% of the audience") is the #1 reason students lose 1–2 marks in numerical questions.
> 5. **Ignoring Internal Choice Pattern**: Always write BOTH Question A and Question B in the answer book initially, then tick the one you attempt. Examiners may deduct marks for attempting both fully without clear choice.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Definition**: Advertising Media = paid channels used to deliver promotional messages to target audiences.
- ✅ **Primary Classification**: Print, Broadcast, Outdoor, Digital, In-Store, Cinema.
- ✅ **Media vs. Vehicle**: Media = broad category; Vehicle = specific carrier.
- ✅ **Key Formulas**: CPM, Reach, Frequency, GRP, TRP, CTR, CPC, ROAS, SOV.
- ✅ **CPM Formula**: Cost / Impressions × 1000 — always remember the ×1000.
- ✅ **Reach × Frequency = GRPs** (using % values).
- ✅ **Effective Frequency**: 3+ exposures per viewer (Herbert Krugman).
- ✅ **3R Framework**: Reach, Recency, Relevance.
- ✅ **Budgeting Methods**: Objective-and-Task, Percentage-of-Sales, Competitive Parity, Affordable.
- ✅ **Schedule Patterns**: Continuous, Flighting, Pulsing, Concentrated.
- ✅ **Digital KPIs**: CPM, CTR, CPC, CPA, ROAS, Conversion Rate.
- ✅ **TV Metric**: TRP (target audience) vs. TVR (general audience).
- ✅ **Media Selection Process**: 6 systematic steps from audience to execution.
- ✅ **Kerala Context**: Asianet (TV), Mathrubhumi/Manorama (Print), Red FM (Radio), Kochi Metro (OOH).
- ✅ **Examination Focus**: Numerical questions (CPM, GRP), conceptual questions (media types, scheduling), and case-based media plan design.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: International Promotion and Advertising
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT742 - MARKETING COMMUNICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** International Promotion and Advertising

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**International Promotion** is the strategic process of communicating the value proposition of a firm's products or services to customers, intermediaries, and other publics across national borders, employing a coordinated blend of advertising, sales promotion, personal selling, public relations, and direct marketing tools within the constraints of diverse cultural, legal, economic, and competitive environments.

**International Advertising** is a specific, non-personal, paid form of presentation and promotion of ideas, goods, or services by an identified sponsor across one or more national markets, executed through mass media (print, broadcast, digital, outdoor) with the objective of informing, persuading, and reminding target audiences — thereby influencing cross-border consumer behaviour and brand equity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition Snapshot**
> The term *International Promotion* is **broader** than *International Advertising*. Promotion is the entire **communications mix** (IMC — Integrated Marketing Communications), while Advertising is merely **one element** within that mix. Students must NEVER use these terms interchangeably in exam answers.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine you are a chef who has written a fantastic recipe for chicken curry. Now you want to sell it worldwide.

- **In India**, you sell it as "Chicken Curry" — served with roti, spicy, in a steel plate.
- **In Japan**, you must rebrand it as "Creamy Coconut Chicken Stew" — milder, served with rice, in a ceramic bowl, because Japanese palates prefer less heat.
- **In the USA**, you call it "Southern-Style Chicken Gravy" — sold in a microwaveable cup.
- **In the Middle East**, you cannot even call it "curry" because of religious dietary preferences — you re-label it as "Halal Chicken Specialty."

The **core dish is identical**, but the **promotion, packaging, naming, advertising imagery, and even the colours used on the wrapper differ dramatically** because of cultural, religious, and economic differences.

> That is the essence of **International Promotion and Advertising** — *what* to communicate stays strategic, but *how*, *where*, and *to whom* must be adapted to the local market reality.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Terms You MUST Know for KTU Board Exams**

| Term | Definition | Relevance |
|------|------------|-----------|
| **Standardization** | Using a uniform promotional message across all global markets | Cost-effective, brand consistency |
| **Adaptation** | Modifying promotional content for local cultural fit | Higher local relevance, costlier |
| **Glocalization** | Global strategy + Local execution | Best of both worlds (KTU favourite concept) |
| **IMC** | Integrated Marketing Communications | Unifying all promotional tools |
| **CPM** | Cost Per Mille (per thousand impressions) | Media buying efficiency metric |
| **Share of Voice (SOV)** | Brand's ad spend ÷ Total category ad spend | Competitive advertising intensity |
| **Pull Strategy** | Stimulating end-user demand directly | Consumer goods typical |
| **Push Strategy** | Persuading channel intermediaries first | Industrial goods typical |

---

## 1.4 Scope of the Topic (KTU Module 3 Mapping)

```
Module 3: International Promotion and Advertising
│
├── 1. International Marketing Communications Mix
├── 2. International Advertising — Standardization vs. Adaptation
├── 3. Factors Affecting International Promotion Decisions
├── 4. International Advertising Agency Selection
├── 5. International Media Planning & Selection
├── 6. International Sales Promotion
├── 7. International Public Relations & Sponsorship
├── 8. International Direct Marketing & Digital Promotion
└── 9. Coordinating International Promotion Campaigns
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The International Marketing Communications (Promotion) Mix

The International Promotion Mix is the strategic combination of five communication tools a firm uses to deliver its value proposition across borders:

### A. Advertising
- Non-personal, paid form of mass communication.
- Uses media: TV, radio, print, outdoor, digital.
- Examples: Nike's "Just Do It" — globally standardized tagline with locally adapted execution.

### B. Personal Selling
- Face-to-face, oral communication with prospective buyers.
- Critical in B2B international markets (industrial machinery, aerospace).
- High cost per contact, high conversion rate.

### C. Sales Promotion
- Short-term incentives to stimulate purchase.
- Includes: coupons, samples, contests, trade discounts, point-of-sale displays.
- Must comply with local regulations (e.g., couponing banned in some EU countries).

### D. Public Relations (PR)
- Building favourable public image through press releases, events, CSR.
- Crucial during international crises (e.g., Toyota recalls, BP oil spill).

### E. Direct Marketing
- Direct communication with individual customers using mail, email, catalogues, telemarketing.
- Digital transformation has made this the fastest-growing segment globally.

---

## 2.2 The Standardization vs. Adaptation Decision (THE KTU FAVOURITE FRAMEWORK)

This is the **single most important analytical framework** in Module 3 and is repeatedly asked in KTU exams.

### A. Standardization Argument
**Definition:** Using the **same promotional message, copy, visuals, and media** across all international markets.

**Advantages:**
1. **Economies of scale** in creative production.
2. **Consistent global brand image** (Coca-Cola, Apple, Nike).
3. Lower creative development costs.
4. Faster rollout.
5. Centralized control by headquarters.

**Disadvantages:**
1. Ignores cultural differences.
2. Risk of cultural insensitivity or offence.
3. May violate local advertising laws.
4. Lower local-market relevance and resonance.

### B. Adaptation Argument
**Definition:** **Modifying** promotional content (language, imagery, claims, media mix) to suit each local market.

**Advantages:**
1. Higher local relevance and cultural fit.
2. Better legal compliance.
3. Stronger emotional connection.
4. Allows for local creativity.

**Disadvantages:**
1. High production cost.
2. Loss of brand consistency.
3. Difficult to coordinate globally.
4. Longer rollout time.

### C. Glocalization — The Hybrid Approach
**Definition:** Developing a **global strategic positioning** while allowing **local tactical execution**.

**Example:** McDonald's
- **Global idea:** "I'm Lovin' It"
- **Local execution:** Menu varies (McAloo Tikki in India, Teriyaki Burger in Japan, McRice in Philippines).

---

## 2.3 Factors Affecting International Promotion Decisions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a high-weightage 7-mark question area in KTU Module 3 exams.**

### Factor 1: Cultural Factors
- **Language:** Translation pitfalls (e.g., KFC's "Finger-Lickin' Good" lost meaning in Chinese).
- **Values & Beliefs:** Religious taboos (no pork imagery in Muslim markets).
- **Aesthetics:** Colour symbolism (white = death in East Asia, purity in West).
- **Hofstede's Cultural Dimensions** directly impact ad appeals.

### Factor 2: Economic Factors
- Stage of economic development dictates media availability.
- Per capita income influences media choice (digital vs. print vs. outdoor).
- Currency fluctuations affect budget allocation.

### Factor 3: Legal & Regulatory Factors
- **Comparative advertising** banned in Germany, France.
- **Advertising to children** restricted in Sweden, Norway.
- **Tobacco/alcohol** advertising restricted in most countries.
- **Data privacy laws:** GDPR (Europe), CCPA (California).

### Factor 4: Competitive Factors
- Market share leadership determines SOV (Share of Voice) targets.
- Clutter level affects message penetration.

### Factor 5: Media Availability & Cost
- TV penetration varies (high in USA, low in rural India).
- Digital penetration varies (high in Korea, low in parts of Africa).
- CPM rates differ dramatically.

### Factor 6: Product/Industry Factors
- Industrial products → Personal selling + Trade shows.
- Consumer goods → Mass advertising + Sales promotion.
- High-tech products → Direct marketing + Digital.

---

## 2.4 International Advertising Agency Selection

A multinational firm has three structural options:

| Option | Description | Pros | Cons |
|--------|-------------|------|------|
| **In-house Agency** | Firm builds its own advertising department | Full control, deep brand knowledge | High cost, limited expertise |
| **Domestic Agency with Foreign Branches** | Home country agency with overseas offices | Cultural bridge | Coordination challenges |
| **Global/Local Agency** | Local agency in each country | Maximum local relevance | Loss of brand consistency |

**Best Practice:** Many MNCs use a **"Lead Agency"** model — one global agency coordinates with local agencies in each market.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Marketing Math)

> [!NOTE]
> Although Marketing is a non-quantitative subject, KTU 2024 scheme tests **specific numerical competencies** in promotion planning. The following formulas are mandatory.

### Formula 1: Cost Per Mille (CPM)

$$
\text{CPM} = \frac{\text{Cost of the Advertisement}}{\text{Total Audience Reached}} \times 1000
$$

**Unit:** Cost per **1,000** viewers/readers.
**Use:** Comparing efficiency of different media options internationally.

---

### Formula 2: Share of Voice (SOV)

$$
\text{SOV} = \frac{\text{Total Brand Advertising Spend}}{\text{Total Category Advertising Spend}} \times 100
$$

**Use:** Indicates competitive advertising pressure.

---

### Formula 3: Reach × Frequency

$$
\text{Gross Rating Points (GRP)} = \text{Reach (\%)} \times \text{Frequency}
$$

**Use:** Measures total advertising impact in international media planning.

---

### Formula 4: Advertising-to-Sales Ratio

$$
\text{A/S Ratio} = \frac{\text{Advertising Expenditure}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100
$$

**Use:** Benchmarks promotional intensity across markets.

---

### Formula 5: Budget Allocation Across Markets (Proportional Method)

$$
\text{Market Budget}_i = \text{Total Budget} \times \frac{\text{Market Potential}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Market Potential}_i}
$$

**Use:** Allocates international promotion budget to country markets.

---

### Formula 6: Sales Promotion Redemption Rate

$$
\text{Redemption Rate} = \frac{\text{Number of Coupons Redeemed}}{\text{Number of Coupons Distributed}} \times 100
$$

**Use:** Measures effectiveness of international sales promotion campaigns.

---

### Formula 7: Advertising Elasticity of Sales

$$
E_{ad} = \frac{\% \text{ Change in Sales}}{\% \text{ Change in Advertising Spend}}
$$

**Use:** Measures responsiveness of sales to advertising investment across markets.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering / Industry Utility

While "International Promotion" is inherently a marketing domain, the **decision frameworks** taught here have **direct application** in:

1. **B2B Industrial Marketing** — Engineering firms (L&T, Tata Steel, Bosch) promoting capital equipment globally.
2. **Technology Transfer & Licensing** — Promoting IP across borders.
3. **International Project Bidding** — Communication strategy for global tenders.
4. **IT Services Exports** — How Infosys/Wipro promote to Fortune 500 clients.
5. **Consumer Tech Products** — How Xiaomi adapts its global launch strategy.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Decision Tree for Promotion Standardization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The following decision-tree derivation is a **guaranteed 14-mark long-answer framework**. Practice drawing this from memory.

### STEP 1: Identify the Strategic Goal
- **Brand Consistency Goal** → Lean towards Standardization.
- **Local Market Share Goal** → Lean towards Adaptation.

### STEP 2: Assess Cultural Distance
- **Low Cultural Distance** (e.g., USA ↔ Canada) → Standardize.
- **High Cultural Distance** (e.g., USA ↔ Japan) → Adapt.

### STEP 3: Evaluate Product Nature
- **Functional / Universal Products** (Coca-Cola, Levi Jeans) → Standardize.
- **Culturally-Sensitive Products** (Food, Fashion, Religion) → Adapt.

### STEP 4: Examine Legal Environment
- **Permissive Regulations** (USA, UK) → Standardize.
- **Restrictive Regulations** (EU, Middle East) → Adapt.

### STEP 5: Assess Competitive Intensity
- **Fragmented Competition** → Standardize.
- **Local Strong Competitors** → Adapt to counter-attack.

### STEP 6: Decide on the Final Strategy

$$
\text{Strategy} = f(\text{Culture}, \text{Product}, \text{Law}, \text{Competition}, \text{Cost})
$$

If **four or more factors point towards adaptation**, choose **Glocalization**.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Worked Example: International CPM Comparison (Analytical Derivation)

**Problem:** An Indian FMCG company plans to launch a shampoo in three markets with the following data:

| Market | Ad Cost (₹ / $) | Audience Reached |
|--------|-----------------|------------------|
| India (TV) | ₹ 5,00,000 | 5,00,000 viewers |
| USA (TV) | $ 10,000 | 1,00,000 viewers |
| UK (Digital) | £ 4,000 | 2,00,000 viewers |

Assume **1 USD = ₹ 83**, **1 GBP = ₹ 105**. Calculate CPM in **INR-equivalent** for each market and recommend the most cost-efficient market.

---

### SOLUTION — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Convert all ad costs to INR (Indian Rupees).**

India: Cost = ₹ 5,00,000 (no conversion)

USA: Cost = $ 10,000 × 83 = ₹ 8,30,000

UK: Cost = £ 4,000 × 105 = ₹ 4,20,000

**Step 2: Apply the CPM formula for each market.**

$$
\text{CPM}_{\text{India}} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 1000
$$

$$
\text{CPM}_{\text{India}} = 1 \times 1000 = ₹ 1{,}000 \text{ per 1,000 viewers}
$$

$$
\text{CPM}_{\text{USA}} = \frac{8{,}30{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} \times 1000
$$

$$
\text{CPM}_{\text{USA}} = 8.3 \times 1000 = ₹ 8{,}300 \text{ per 1,000 viewers}
$$

$$
\text{CPM}_{\text{UK}} = \frac{4{,}20{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} \times 1000
$$

$$
\text{CPM}_{\text{UK}} = 2.1 \times 1000 = ₹ 2{,}100 \text{ per 1,000 viewers}
$$

**Step 3: Compare and Rank.**

| Market | CPM (₹ per 1,000) | Rank |
|--------|-------------------|------|
| India | ₹ 1,000 | 🥇 1st (Most efficient) |
| UK | ₹ 2,100 | 🥈 2nd |
| USA | ₹ 8,300 | 🥉 3rd (Least efficient) |

**Step 4: Conclusion.**
India provides the **lowest CPM** and the **highest media efficiency** in absolute terms. However, the **strategy decision** must also consider **target audience size, purchasing power, and brand objectives**. The recommendation would be to allocate the **majority of the budget to India**, use the **UK as a digital pilot**, and **limit the USA spend to niche premium audiences**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Takeaway:** A low CPM is **not always** the best market. KTU exams often test whether the student looks **beyond CPM** to factors like reach, target audience fit, and brand positioning.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Worked Example: International Budget Allocation (Proportional Method)

**Problem:** A European luxury brand plans a **€ 10 million** global promotion budget across four markets based on market potential. Allocate the budget.

| Market | Market Potential Index (MPI) |
|--------|------------------------------|
| France | 40 |
| Germany | 30 |
| Italy | 20 |
| Spain | 10 |
| **Total** | **100** |

---

### SOLUTION — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Apply the Proportional Allocation Formula.**

$$
\text{Market Budget}_i = \text{Total Budget} \times \frac{\text{MPI}_i}{\sum \text{MPI}}
$$

**Step 2: Compute each market's allocation.**

$$
\text{France Budget} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{40}{100} = € 4{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Germany Budget} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{30}{100} = € 3{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Italy Budget} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{20}{100} = € 2{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Spain Budget} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times \frac{10}{100} = € 1{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3: Verification.**

$$
4{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 = € 10{,}00{,}000 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 4: Result Matrix.**

| Market | MPI | Allocation (€) | % of Budget |
|--------|-----|----------------|-------------|
| France | 40 | 4,00,000 | 40% |
| Germany | 30 | 3,00,000 | 30% |
| Italy | 20 | 2,00,000 | 20% |
| Spain | 10 | 1,00,000 | 10% |
| **Total** | **100** | **10,00,000** | **100%** |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: AIDA Model in International Advertising

The **AIDA Model** (Attention → Interest → Desire → Action) is a universally applied framework. In international advertising, each stage must be **culturally decoded**:

| Stage | Universal Goal | India-Specific | Japan-Specific | USA-Specific |
|-------|---------------|----------------|----------------|--------------|
| **Attention** | Capture eyeball | Bollywood celebrities | Anime mascots | Super Bowl spectacle |
| **Interest** | Sustain engagement | Family-oriented stories | Group harmony themes | Individual achievement |
| **Desire** | Create want | Social status + value | Quality + tradition | Convenience + novelty |
| **Action** | Drive purchase | Price-off + family appeal | Quality proof + subtlety | Direct call-to-action |

---

## 3.5 The DAGMAR Model (Defining Advertising Goals for Measured Advertising Results)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU syllabus mentions DAGMAR in the IMC context.** This 7-step communication-effect sequence is frequently tested.

$$
\text{Unknown} \rightarrow \text{Awareness} \rightarrow \text{Comprehension} \rightarrow \text{Conviction} \rightarrow \text{Action}
$$

In international markets, the **time taken to traverse these stages varies** with media literacy, language proficiency, and consumer trust.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Standardization and Adaptation in international advertising with one example each.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Standardization** is the strategy of using **uniform promotional messages, visuals, and copy** across all international markets without modification. It provides brand consistency and economies of scale. *Example:* Coca-Cola uses the same "Taste the Feeling" campaign globally.

**Adaptation** is the strategy of **modifying promotional content** to suit local cultural, linguistic, legal, and consumer preferences in each target market. It enhances local relevance. *Example:* McDonald's offers McAloo Tikki in India and Teriyaki Burger in Japan.

> The **key difference** lies in *uniformity of message* vs. *localization of message*. *(1 mark for definition each, 1 mark for example + difference)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is Glocalization in international marketing communications? Give an example.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Glocalization** is a hybrid strategy that combines a **global strategic positioning** with **local tactical execution** of marketing communications. The brand's *core idea* remains uniform, but the *creative execution* is adapted to local cultural sensitivities.

**Example:** Nike's "Just Do It" campaign is globally consistent in spirit, but features **local sports icons** (Sachin Tendulkar in India, Neymar in Brazil) and locally relevant storylines in each country.

> *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for hybrid nature, 1 mark for example)*

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the **International Marketing Communications (Promotion) Mix** in detail. Discuss the **factors affecting international promotion decisions** with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Q. (b)** Discuss the **Standardization vs. Adaptation debate** in international advertising. Illustrate with **real-world examples** and recommend a strategic framework for decision-making. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The International Promotion Mix is the combination of communication tools a firm uses to deliver its message across borders. The five core elements are:

1. **Advertising (1 mark):**
   - Non-personal, paid form of mass communication.
   - Uses mass media: TV, radio, print, digital, outdoor.
   - Example: Apple's "Think Different" campaign runs globally with consistent creative.

2. **Personal Selling (1 mark):**
   - Face-to-face oral communication.
   - Critical in B2B/industrial international markets.
   - Example: Airbus using sales engineers to sell aircraft to airlines globally.

3. **Sales Promotion (1 mark):**
   - Short-term incentives: coupons, samples, contests, point-of-sale displays.
   - Example: Free coffee samples in Chinese supermarkets to launch Starbucks.

4. **Public Relations (1 mark):**
   - Building favourable public image through press releases, CSR, events.
   - Example: Tesla's global media coverage of Cybertruck launch.

5. **Direct Marketing (1 mark):**
   - Direct communication with individual customers: email, catalogues, telemarketing.
   - Example: Amazon's personalized email campaigns across 20+ countries.

**Factors Affecting International Promotion Decisions (1 mark for mention + 0.5 for example):**

| Factor | Impact | Example |
|--------|--------|---------|
| **Cultural** | Language, religion, values | Pepsi failed in Taiwan with slogan "Come Alive with Pepsi" translated as "Pepsi brings your ancestors back from the dead" |
| **Economic** | Media cost, income levels | Higher TV CPM in India vs. USA |
| **Legal** | Advertising restrictions | Alcohol ads banned in many Muslim countries |
| **Competitive** | SOV targets | Coca-Cola's heavy SOV in Latin America |
| **Media Availability** | TV vs. digital vs. outdoor | Rural India still relies on radio and outdoor |

**Conclusion:** A balanced promotion mix aligned with each market's unique environment is essential for international success. *(0.5 marks)*

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The Standardization vs. Adaptation debate centres on whether to use a uniform global message or modify it for local markets. This is the **central strategic question** in international advertising.

**Standardization Argument (1.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** Uniform message across all markets.
- **Advantages:** Cost efficiency, brand consistency, faster rollout.
- **Examples:** Coca-Cola, Apple iPhone, Levi's.

**Adaptation Argument (1.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** Localized message for each market.
- **Advantages:** Cultural fit, legal compliance, stronger local relevance.
- **Examples:** McDonald's menus, KFC China's rice-based menu.

**Real-World Examples (1 mark):**
- **Nike:** Standardized tagline + Localized athletes.
- **P&G:** Global brand strategy + Local product variants.
- **Cadbury:** Different chocolate formulations for different countries.

**Recommended Strategic Framework (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{Strategy Choice} = f(\text{Culture}, \text{Product}, \text{Law}, \text{Competition}, \text{Cost})
$$

- If **cultural distance is high** → Adapt.
- If **product is universal** → Standardize.
- If **legal restrictions exist** → Adapt.
- In most real-world cases → **Glocalize** (best of both).

**Conclusion (0.5 marks):**
A pure standardization or pure adaptation is rare. Most successful MNCs use **glocalization** — global positioning with local execution.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Describe the **process of international media selection and planning**. How does the **choice of advertising agency** differ in international markets compared to domestic markets? **(7 Marks)**

**Q. (b)** Explain the **role of public relations, sales promotion, and direct marketing** in international markets. What are the **legal and ethical challenges** faced by global marketers? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**International Media Selection Process (3.5 marks):**

Step 1: **Define Target Audience** — Demographics, psychographics, media habits in each country.

Step 2: **Establish Media Objectives** — Reach, frequency, GRP targets.

Step 3: **Determine Media Budget** — Based on market potential and CPM.

Step 4: **Evaluate Media Alternatives:**
- **Print:** Newspapers, magazines (high literacy markets).
- **Broadcast:** TV, radio (mass reach markets).
- **Digital:** Social media, search, display (developed markets).
- **Outdoor:** Billboards, transit (urban emerging markets).
- **In-store:** POS displays, packaging (point-of-purchase markets).

Step 5: **Media Mix Optimization** — Combine media to maximize reach and minimize cost.

Step 6: **Execute and Measure** — Track GRPs, redemption, conversion.

**International vs. Domestic Agency Choice (3.5 marks):**

| Aspect | Domestic | International |
|--------|----------|---------------|
| **Agency Pool** | Local | Global network + Local |
| **Coordination** | Simple | Complex across time zones |
| **Cultural Knowledge** | Deep local | Multi-market expertise needed |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher due to global reach |
| **Models** | Single agency | Lead agency / Multiple local |
| **Examples** | Mudra (India) | Ogilvy, WPP, Publicis (Global networks) |

**Best Practice:** MNCs use a **"Lead Agency Model"** — one global agency (e.g., Ogilvy) coordinates with local agencies in each market.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Role of PR in International Markets (1.5 marks):**
- Crisis management (e.g., Toyota recall handling).
- CSR programs (Unilever's Sustainable Living Plan).
- Event sponsorships (Coca-Cola at Olympics).
- Press relations with global media.

**Role of Sales Promotion in International Markets (1.5 marks):**
- Coupons, samples, contests, premiums.
- Trade promotions to gain channel support abroad.
- Must comply with local regulations (e.g., EU restrictions).

**Role of Direct Marketing in International Markets (1.5 marks):**
- Email campaigns, catalogues, telemarketing.
- Rapid growth due to digital transformation.
- Personalization at scale (Amazon, Alibaba).

**Legal & Ethical Challenges (2 marks):**

| Challenge | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Comparative Advertising** | Banned in Germany, France, Japan |
| **Advertising to Children** | Restricted in Sweden, Norway |
| **Tobacco/Alcohol** | Restricted in Middle East, EU |
| **Data Privacy** | GDPR (Europe), CCPA (USA) |
| **Puffery vs. False Claims** | Strict in USA (FTC), looser in some Asian markets |
| **Cultural Insensitivity** | Risk of offence and boycott |

**Conclusion:** International marketers must balance global brand objectives with deep respect for local laws, cultures, and ethics.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Interchanging "Promotion" and "Advertising"** — Promotion is broader; Advertising is one tool. *(−1 mark)*
> 2. **Missing the Glocalization option** — Most real-world answers are hybrid. Pure standardization or pure adaptation is rare. *(−1 mark)*
> 3. **Forgetting examples** — KTU 2024 scheme demands **real-world brand examples** in every 7-mark answer. Generic theory alone fetches partial marks. *(−1 to −2 marks)*
> 4. **Skipping diagrams/flowcharts** — The 14-mark answers **MUST** include a structured framework or diagram to score full marks. *(−1 to −2 marks)*
> 5. **Ignoring legal/ethical factors** — A complete answer to "factors affecting international promotion" **must** include legal/regulatory dimension. *(−1 mark)*

---

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Advertising Research** is the systematic, objective, and scientific process of gathering, recording, and analyzing qualitative as well as quantitative information related to advertising, with the objective of providing marketers and creative teams with actionable inputs for developing, evaluating, and refining advertising strategies, messages, and media decisions.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, Advertising Research is positioned as the **diagnostic backbone** of the entire Integrated Marketing Communication (IMC) cycle. It ensures that advertising decisions are not based on intuition, personal bias, or guesswork, but on **empirical evidence** drawn from the target audience, the competitive environment, and the media landscape.

### Components of the Definition
- **Systematic** → Follows a pre-defined, structured methodology (not ad-hoc).
- **Objective** → Free from personal opinion or political influence inside the agency.
- **Scientific** → Uses validated scales, sampling techniques, and statistical tools.
- **Actionable** → The end-output must lead to a **decision** (continue, change, or kill the ad).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Advertising Research bridges the gap between the **Advertising Plan** (Module 3) and the **Advertising Budget & Effectiveness** (Module 5). It is the *feedback mechanism* that validates or invalidates the creative and media plan.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Think of Advertising Research as a **medical diagnosis before surgery**:

| Medical World | Advertising Research World |
|---|---|
| Patient reports symptoms | Brand reports weak sales / low recall |
| Doctor orders blood tests, scans | Researcher orders surveys, focus groups, eye-tracking |
| Diagnosis determines treatment | Research findings determine creative change, media change |
| Post-surgery follow-up | Post-launch ad testing (post-testing) |

> [!TIP]
> A surgeon who operates without diagnosis is a quack. **An advertiser who launches a campaign without research is gambling with the company's money.** Advertising Research is the *stethoscope* of the marketing manager.

### Why It Matters in KTU Examinations
- It is one of the **most frequently tested application questions** because it tests the student's ability to *link research → creative → media → outcome*.
- Examiners often give a **case study** and ask: *"Which type of advertising research would you recommend and why?"*

---

## 1.3 Standard Classification Terminology (KTU Standardized)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Pre-Testing** | Research conducted *before* the ad is run in the main media. |
| **Concurrent Testing** | Research done *during* the actual campaign run. |
| **Post-Testing** | Research conducted *after* the campaign has been aired. |
| **Copy Research** | Research focused on the advertisement's message, visuals, and wording. |
| **Media Research** | Research focused on the selection of media vehicles and scheduling. |
| **Sales-Effect Research** | Measures the *direct* impact on sales/revenue. |
| **Communication-Effect Research** | Measures *awareness, attitude, recall, comprehension*. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant to Remember:** The **Core Metrics** in any KTU question on Advertising Research effectiveness are: **Recall, Recognition, Comprehension, Attitude Change, and Sales Lift.**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Objectives of Advertising Research (Hierarchical)

Advertising Research serves **four principal objectives**, often remembered by the acronym **MICE**:

1. **M – Message Evaluation:** To test whether the advertising copy communicates the intended message clearly.
2. **I – Impact Measurement:** To measure the ad's effect on consumer awareness, attitudes, and behavior.
3. **C – Competitive Benchmarking:** To compare the ad's effectiveness versus competitor campaigns.
4. **E – Efficiency Assessment:** To verify whether the *cost-per-thousand impressions (CPM)* and *return on advertising spend (ROAS)* are optimized.

---

## 2.2 Types of Advertising Research – Detailed Taxonomy

### A) Based on Stage of Campaign

| Type | When | Purpose | KTU Favourite Question |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pre-Testing** | Before launch | To eliminate weak ads; save money | *"Suggest 3 pre-testing methods for a new TV commercial."* |
| **Concurrent Testing** | During run | To make mid-course corrections | Used for long campaigns |
| **Post-Testing** | After campaign | To measure actual impact | *"Measure effectiveness of your ad campaign."* |

### B) Based on Subject of Research

- **Product Research** → Is the product itself acceptable?
- **Copy Research** → Is the message well-crafted?
- **Media Research** → Which vehicle reaches the audience best?
- **Campaign Effectiveness Research** → Did the campaign achieve objectives?
- **Sales-Effect Research** → Did sales go up?

### C) Based on Methodology

- **Qualitative Research** (focus groups, in-depth interviews, projective techniques)
- **Quantitative Research** (surveys, A/B testing, ratings)

---

## 2.3 Pre-Testing Methods – The KTU Toolkit

| Method | Medium | Key Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Inquire Method** | Print | Recall of specific elements |
| **Portfolio Test** | Print | Recall + Recognition |
| **Mock Magazine Test** | Print | Ad recognition in a dummy issue |
| **Theater Test** | TV/Radio | Recall of ad content |
| **Live Telephone Test** | Radio | Same-day recall |
| **On-Air Test** | TV | Real-time ad reaction |
| **Sales Wave Test** | Multiple | Tracks trial/repurchase behavior |
| **Trained-Viewer Test** | TV | Measures attention, brand-name recall, selling-points recall |
| **Scrambled-Ad Test** | TV | Compares target ad vs control ad |

> [!NOTE]
> **Starch Readership Survey** is a famous **print pre-testing** method that measures *Noted, Associated, Read-Most, and Read All* scores for magazine/newspaper advertisements.

---

## 2.4 Post-Testing Methods

| Method | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Aided Recall** | Show the ad, then ask "Did you see this?" |
| **Unaided Recall** | Ask "What ads do you remember from yesterday's paper?" |
| **Recognition Test** | Reader identifies the ad they saw |
| **Recall Test** (Day-After Recall, DAR) | Used heavily in TV advertising |
| **Attitude Tests** | Measures change in brand perception |
| **Sales-Response Tests** | Correlates ad spend with sales lift |
| **Inquiry Tests** | Measures number of coupon/website responses |
| **Communication Audit** | Holistic review of all brand communications |

---

## 2.5 The DAGMAR Model (Foundational Theory)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DAGMAR** = *Defining Advertising Goals for Measured Advertising Results* — proposed by **Russell H. Colley (1961)**. KTU examiners expect you to know both the model AND the author.

**The Hierarchy of Effects:**

| Step | Stage | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Awareness** | Consumer knows brand exists | "I've heard of Lux" |
| 2 | **Comprehension** | Consumer understands what it offers | "Lux gives shine" |
| 3 | **Conviction** | Consumer believes in its superiority | "Lux really is better" |
| 4 | **Action** | Consumer purchases | "I will buy Lux today" |

**Implication for Research:** Each stage requires a *different research instrument* — awareness is measured by recall, comprehension by questioning, conviction by attitude scales, action by sales data.

---

## 2.6 AIDA Model (Communication Stages)

- **A** – Attention (Is the ad noticed?)
- **I** – Interest (Does it engage?)
- **D** – Desire (Does it create want?)
- **A** – Action (Does it trigger purchase?)

Research is required at *each* stage to identify the drop-off point (e.g., attention is fine but interest is low → creative problem).

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> Since Advertising Research is a **Management** subject, the "formulas" are **analytical ratios** that appear frequently in KTU numerical/case questions.

| Formula | Purpose | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{Recall Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Number Who Recalled Ad}}{\text{Total Surveyed}} \times 100$ | Measure ad memorability | Post-testing |
| $\text{Recognition Score} = \dfrac{\text{Noted} + \text{Associated}}{\text{Total Readers}}$ | Starch-type survey | Print post-test |
| $\text{CPM} = \dfrac{\text{Cost of Ad}}{\text{Reach (in thousands)}}$ | Media cost efficiency | Media research |
| $\text{ROAS} = \dfrac{\text{Revenue Generated}}{\text{Ad Spend}} \times 100$ | Overall ad ROI | Effectiveness |
| $\text{GRP} = \text{Reach \%} \times \text{Frequency}$ | Gross Rating Points | Media planning |
| $\text{Effective Frequency} = \dfrac{\text{Exposure Threshold}}{\text{Noise Level}}$ | Minimum exposures for impact | Media research |
| $\text{Sales Lift \%} = \dfrac{\text{Post-Ad Sales} - \text{Pre-Ad Sales}}{\text{Pre-Ad Sales}} \times 100$ | Direct sales impact | Sales-effect test |
| $\text{Conversion Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Inquiries Generated}}{\text{Total Impressions}} \times 100$ | Inquiry test | Direct response ads |

> **KTU Examiner Note:** *Always remember to convert percentages and use thousands for CPM.* Students often lose 1–2 marks by writing the formula without the "× 100" or miscalculating the denominator.

---

## 2.8 The Advertising Research Process (Five-Stage Model)

1. **Define the Research Problem** → What decision does the marketer need to make?
2. **Develop the Research Plan** → Choose data source, methodology, tools, and budget.
3. **Collect Relevant Data** → Primary (surveys) or Secondary (existing reports).
4. **Analyze the Data** → Statistical tools, qualitative coding, content analysis.
5. **Present Findings & Take Action** → Research report with recommendations.

---

## 2.9 Real-World Utility in Marketing Engineering

In the **Indian marketing industry**, Advertising Research is applied by:
- **Media Buying Agencies** (using BARC, TAM, RAM data)
- **Creative Agencies** (using copy testing labs like Millward Brown, Ipsos)
- **Brand Teams** (using Nielsen retail audit + ad-tracking)
- **Digital Marketers** (using Google Analytics, Meta Ads Manager, Hotjar heatmaps)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-Life Engineering Analogy:** In software engineering, we run *unit tests* and *user acceptance tests* before release. In advertising, **pre-testing is the unit test** and **post-testing is the UAT** — both are non-negotiable for a successful release.

---

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Application of the Advertising Research Process

Below is the **exhaustive, board-exam-ready** expansion of the five-stage research process. KTU frequently asks for a *"design a research plan for a new shampoo ad"* type question worth 7–10 marks.

### STEP 1: Define the Research Problem

**Logic:** Without a clearly defined problem, the entire research is misdirected.

The problem statement must address the **6 W's**:

| W | Question | Example for a New Shampoo |
|---|---|---|
| **What** | What information is needed? | Effectiveness of TV ad |
| **Why** | Why is research needed? | To decide ad budget continuation |
| **Who** | Who is the target? | Urban women, 18–35 |
| **Where** | Where will data be collected? | Mumbai, Delhi, Bangalore |
| **When** | When? | Pre-launch + 30 days post-launch |
| **How** | Methodology? | Focus groups + surveys |

> **Conversion Logic:** The 6W framework ensures the research design is **scoped tightly** — this is the *first marker* examiners look for.

---

### STEP 2: Develop the Research Plan

Sub-steps are:

1. **Identify Data Sources**
   - *Primary Data*: New data collected through surveys, focus groups.
   - *Secondary Data*: Existing industry reports, syndicated research (e.g., TAM, BARC).

2. **Select Research Approach**
   - *Observational Research* (watching consumer behavior).
   - *Survey Research* (questionnaires).
   - *Experimental Research* (A/B testing ad versions).
   - *Qualitative Research* (focus groups, in-depth interviews).

3. **Choose Data Collection Method**
   - Personal interviews, telephone interviews, online surveys, mall intercepts.

4. **Design Sampling Plan**
   - Define *target population* → *sampling frame* → *sample size* → *sampling technique* (random, stratified, cluster, convenience).

5. **Estimate Budget & Timeline**

> **Conversion Logic:** KTU examiners expect students to write each sub-step as a *bullet point* with one-line justification. Skipping the *sampling plan* costs 2 marks.

---

### STEP 3: Collect the Data

The collection phase must follow the **PDCA cycle**:

- **P**lan → Field supervisor briefs enumerators.
- **D**o → Enumerators collect data.
- **C**heck → Quality audits (10% sample re-checked).
- **A**ct → Errors corrected, team re-trained.

> **Conversion Logic:** The PDCA analogy demonstrates **engineering thinking** to a non-engineering examiner. KTU frequently rewards this in B.Tech (Management) papers.

---

### STEP 4: Analyze the Data

Two main tracks:

**A) Quantitative Analysis:**
- Cross-tabulation (e.g., age group vs ad recall).
- Regression analysis (e.g., ad spend vs sales lift).
- Hypothesis testing (t-tests, chi-square).
- Conjoint analysis for ad-element preferences.

**B) Qualitative Analysis:**
- Thematic coding of focus-group transcripts.
- Sentiment analysis of social media comments.
- Content analysis of competitor ads.

---

### STEP 5: Present Findings & Recommendations

The final report follows a **standardized KTU structure**:

1. Executive Summary (1 page)
2. Research Objectives
3. Methodology Used
4. Key Findings (with charts/graphs)
5. Limitations of the Study
6. Conclusions
7. Recommendations
8. Appendices (questionnaire, raw data)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** If a question says *"Design a research plan"*, the above 8-point structure is worth the full 14 marks. Missing the *Limitations* section alone can cost 1 mark.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Numerical – Recall Rate Calculation

> **Problem:** An advertising agency conducts a post-test for a new detergent ad. 480 out of 600 surveyed women said they could recall at least one element of the ad. Calculate the **Recall Rate** and interpret.

**Solution:**

Given:
- Number who recalled ad: $N_r = 480$
- Total surveyed: $N_t = 600$

Apply the formula:

$$
\text{Recall Rate} = \frac{N_r}{N_t} \times 100
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
\text{Recall Rate} = \frac{480}{600} \times 100
$$

Simplify the fraction:

$$
\frac{480}{600} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.80
$$

Multiply by 100:

$$
\text{Recall Rate} = 0.80 \times 100 = 80\%
$$

**Interpretation:** A recall rate of 80% is **excellent** (industry benchmark is 60–70%). The ad is highly memorable. *Marketing Implication: Continue with the same creative; no changes needed.*

---

## 3.3 Detailed Numerical – CPM and ROAS

> **Problem:** A company spends ₹5,00,000 on a print ad that reaches 2,000,000 readers. The campaign directly generates ₹15,00,000 in attributable sales. Calculate **CPM** and **ROAS**.

**Solution – Step 1: Compute CPM**

$$
\text{CPM} = \frac{\text{Cost of Ad}}{\text{Reach (in thousands)}}
$$

Reach in thousands:

$$
2{,}000{,}000 = 2{,}000 \text{ thousand}
$$

Substitute:

$$
\text{CPM} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{2{,}000} = 250
$$

> **Interpretation:** The cost is **₹250 per thousand readers**.

**Solution – Step 2: Compute ROAS**

$$
\text{ROAS} = \frac{\text{Revenue Generated}}{\text{Ad Spend}} \times 100
$$

Substitute:

$$
\text{ROAS} = \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
= 3 \times 100 = 300\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** The campaign generated **₹3 for every ₹1 spent** — a strong positive return.

---

## 3.4 Detailed Numerical – Sales Lift Test

> **Problem:** A retailer sells 8,000 units of a snack brand in the month before a TV ad. In the month the ad airs, sales rise to 11,200 units. Calculate the **Sales Lift %** and comment.

**Solution:**

Given:
- Pre-Ad Sales: $S_{pre} = 8{,}000$
- Post-Ad Sales: $S_{post} = 11{,}200$

Apply the formula:

$$
\text{Sales Lift \%} = \frac{S_{post} - S_{pre}}{S_{pre}} \times 100
$$

Substitute:

$$
\text{Sales Lift \%} = \frac{11{,}200 - 8{,}000}{8{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
= \frac{3{,}200}{8{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
= 0.4 \times 100 = 40\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** A **40% sales lift** is exceptionally high — strongly indicates the ad's effectiveness. *Caveat: Always check for confounding factors like seasonality or competitor stock-outs before full credit.*

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix – Selecting the Right Research Method

| Scenario | Best Research Method | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| New ad before national launch | Portfolio Test + Theater Test | Tests multiple ads cheaply before scale-up |
| Long-running brand campaign | Continuous Tracking (e.g., Brand Track) | Monitors shifts in real-time |
| Comparing two TV commercial scripts | Focus Groups + A/B Testing | Qualitative + quantitative split |
| Print ad effectiveness | Starch Readership Survey | Industry-standard for print |
| Digital ad click-through analysis | Web Analytics + Heatmaps | Real-time data, low cost |
| Measuring long-term brand equity | Brand Equity Tracking Survey | Longitudinal, attribute-based |

> **Conversion Logic:** In KTU case-study answers, this matrix can be reproduced (truncated) to demonstrate **methodological depth** — a guaranteed 2-mark bonus in 14-mark questions.

## 4.1 The Advertising Research Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

```mermaid
graph TD
    INPUT[INPUT Brand Objectives Budget Audience Profile] --> DESIGN[RESEARCH DESIGN Objective Source Method Sampling]
    DESIGN --> COLLECTION[DATA COLLECTION Field Surveys Digital Tools Syndicated Data]
    COLLECTION --> PROCESSING[DATA PROCESSING Cleaning Coding Tabulation]
    PROCESSING --> ANALYSIS[DATA ANALYSIS Statistical Qualitative Tools]

```

> **Reading the Block Diagram:** The **feedback loop** (FEEDBACK → INPUT) demonstrates that advertising research is **cyclical**, not linear. Insights from one campaign *always* inform the next campaign's research design.
