# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

### Q1. Define Advertising Research. List any **four** objectives of advertising research. `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

> Advertising Research is the systematic gathering, recording, and analysis of information related to advertising decisions, with the objective of improving the effectiveness of advertising messages, media, and overall strategy.

**Four Objectives:**

1. **Message Evaluation** – To test whether the ad copy communicates the intended message.
2. **Impact Measurement** – To gauge effects on consumer awareness, attitudes, and behavior.
3. **Competitive Benchmarking** – To compare the ad's performance with competitor ads.
4. **Efficiency Assessment** – To ensure optimal use of advertising budget.

---

### Q2. What is **Post-Testing** of advertising? Mention **three** common post-testing methods. `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

> Post-Testing is the evaluation of an advertising campaign *after* it has been run, to measure its actual impact on the target audience and on sales.

**Three Post-Testing Methods:**

1. **Aided Recall Test** – Respondent is shown the ad and asked if they recall seeing it.
2. **Unaided Recall Test** (Day-After Recall) – Respondent is asked what ads they remember without any prompt.
3. **Sales-Response Test** – Measures the change in sales attributable to the ad campaign.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Pattern:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain the **DAGMAR model** in detail. How is it used to set advertising research goals? `[7 Marks]`

**(b)** Describe the **five-stage Advertising Research Process** with reference to a hypothetical launch of a new cold-drink brand in Kerala. `[7 Marks]`

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

**DAGMAR Model:**

DAGMAR stands for *Defining Advertising Goals for Measured Advertising Results*, proposed by **Russell H. Colley (1961)**. It is a hierarchical model that breaks down advertising communication into **four sequential stages**:

1. **Awareness** – The consumer becomes aware of the brand's existence.
2. **Comprehension** – The consumer understands what the brand offers and its benefits.
3. **Conviction** – The consumer develops a psychological preference and conviction to buy.
4. **Action** – The consumer actually purchases the product.

**Linkage with Advertising Research:**

- **Awareness → Measured by recall and recognition tests.**
- **Comprehension → Measured by comprehension questionnaires and copy-testing.**
- **Conviction → Measured by attitude scales and brand preference studies.**
- **Action → Measured by sales-lift tests and inquiry tests.**

> **Conversion Logic:** The DAGMAR model is essentially a *research-mapping framework*. It forces the advertiser to **specify measurable goals** for each stage, which then dictates the **research instrument** to be used.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Five-Stage Advertising Research Process for a Kerala Cold-Drink Launch:**

1. **Define the Research Problem:**
   *Will the new cold drink gain acceptance among Kerala's 15–30 year-olds? What flavor variants and bottle designs resonate best?*

2. **Develop the Research Plan:**
   - **Data Sources:** Primary (focus groups in Kochi, Trivandrum, Calicut) + Secondary (Nielsen retail data).
   - **Methodology:** Qualitative focus groups + Quantitative survey (sample size = 1,000).
   - **Sampling:** Stratified random sampling across urban/semi-urban Kerala.
   - **Budget & Timeline:** ₹5 lakh, 8 weeks.

3. **Collect the Data:**
   - Conduct 6 focus groups (10 participants each).
   - Run an online + offline survey of 1,000 respondents.
   - Use field enumerators for mall-intercept surveys.

4. **Analyze the Data:**
   - Cross-tabulate age vs flavor preference.
   - Run regression: price-sensitivity vs. ad-creative elements.
   - Thematic coding of focus-group transcripts.

5. **Present Findings & Take Action:**
   - Report recommends flavor "Mango-Kokum" and bottle design "tropical leaf pattern".
   - Marketing manager decides to proceed with the launch in metro Kerala first.

> **Conversion Logic:** This answer covers the *who, what, when, where, why, how* — a complete 7-mark response.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Pattern]`

**(a)** Differentiate between **Pre-Testing** and **Post-Testing** of advertising. List **four** pre-testing methods and explain any **two** in detail. `[7 Marks]`

**(b)** A company spends ₹8,00,000 on a TV ad that reaches 4 million viewers. The campaign generates ₹24,00,000 in attributable sales. A post-test survey of 1,000 viewers shows that 650 could recall the ad. Calculate **CPM**, **ROAS**, and **Recall Rate**. Interpret the results and give recommendations. `[7 Marks]`

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a)

| Basis | Pre-Testing | Post-Testing |
|---|---|---|
| **Timing** | Before the campaign is launched | After the campaign is run |
| **Objective** | To screen out weak ads and refine creative | To measure actual impact on audience and sales |
| **Cost** | Low (small sample) | High (large, real campaign) |
| **Risk** | Reduces risk of failure | Confirms whether money was well spent |
| **Sample** | Small, controlled | Large, natural audience |

**Four Pre-Testing Methods:**

1. **Theater Test** (for TV ads)
2. **Portfolio Test** (for print ads)
3. **Sales Wave Test** (tracks actual buying)
4. **Trained-Viewer Test** (measures attention and recall)

**Detailed Explanation — Theater Test:**
- A group of consumers is invited to a theater.
- They watch a program with the test advertisement embedded.
- After viewing, they fill a recall questionnaire.
- Recall scores are computed → Helps decide whether to air the ad nationally.

**Detailed Explanation — Sales Wave Test:**
- The ad is aired in a small test market.
- Consumers in the test market are offered the product at a discount over multiple "waves."
- Their trial and repeat-purchase behavior is tracked.
- Predicts future national sales response.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- Ad Spend = ₹8,00,000
- Reach = 4,000,000 viewers
- Revenue Generated = ₹24,00,000
- Surveyed = 1,000
- Recalled = 650

**Step 1: CPM Calculation**

$$
\text{Reach in thousands} = \frac{4{,}000{,}000}{1{,}000} = 4{,}000
$$

$$
\text{CPM} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{4{,}000} = 200
$$

> **Interpretation:** Cost is **₹200 per thousand viewers** — competitive for Indian TV advertising.

**Step 2: ROAS Calculation**

$$
\text{ROAS} = \frac{24{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 3 \times 100 = 300\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** The campaign generated **₹3 for every ₹1 spent** — a very healthy ROAS.

**Step 3: Recall Rate Calculation**

$$
\text{Recall Rate} = \frac{650}{1{,}000} \times 100 = 65\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** 65% recall is **above the industry benchmark of 50–60%** — the ad is effective.

**Step 4: Recommendations**

1. **Continue** the campaign as it shows strong ROAS (300%) and above-benchmark recall (65%).
2. **Test extending reach** to adjacent markets (e.g., other South Indian states).
3. **Investigate** the 35% non-recallers via follow-up qualitative research to refine creative.
4. **Run a second wave** with a slight creative variation to convert non-recallers.

> **Conversion Logic:** This answer blends **numerical analysis + managerial interpretation**, which is the *gold standard* for KTU 14-mark answers.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on Advertising Research questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Pre-Testing with Post-Testing** — Read the question carefully; "before" and "after" are the most common test points.
> 2. **Forgetting the DAGMAR author** — Always cite **Russell H. Colley (1961)** when mentioning DAGMAR.
> 3. **Skipping the Sampling Plan** in the 5-stage process — A common 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Using only definitions, no application** — A KTU 14-mark question *demands* a case/example. Always tie theory to a real brand.
> 5. **Not interpreting numerical results** — Computing CPM/ROAS is only half the answer. The *interpretation + recommendation* is the other 50% of the marks.
> 6. **Mixing AIDA and DAGMAR** — AIDA is a *customer-perspective* model; DAGMAR is an *advertiser-perspective* goal-setting model. KTU questions often ask to differentiate.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- 🔹 **Definition:** Advertising Research = systematic, objective, scientific study of advertising decisions.
- 🔹 **MICE objectives:** Message evaluation, Impact measurement, Competitive benchmarking, Efficiency assessment.
- 🔹 **Three Stages:** Pre-Testing, Concurrent Testing, Post-Testing.
- 🔹 **Pre-Testing Methods:** Theater Test, Portfolio Test, Starch Readership, Mock Magazine, Sales Wave, Trained-Viewer, Scrambled-Ad.
- 🔹 **Post-Testing Methods:** Aided Recall, Unaided Recall (DAR), Recognition, Attitude Test, Sales-Response, Inquiry, Communication Audit.
- 🔹 **DAGMAR** (Colley, 1961): Awareness → Comprehension → Conviction → Action.
- 🔹 **AIDA:** Attention → Interest → Desire → Action.
- 🔹 **Five-Stage Process:** Define Problem → Develop Plan → Collect Data → Analyze → Present & Recommend.
- 🔹 **Key Formulas:** Recall Rate, Recognition Score, CPM, ROAS, GRP, Sales Lift %, Conversion Rate.
- 🔹 **Starch Readership Survey** is the **gold standard** for print post-testing.
- 🔹 **Qualitative vs Quantitative:** Focus groups vs Surveys — KTU expects both in any research design.
- 🔹 **Research Report Structure:** Executive Summary → Objectives → Methodology → Findings → Limitations → Conclusions → Recommendations → Appendices.
- 🔹 **Real-World Tools:** BARC, TAM, RAM, Nielsen, Millward Brown, Google Analytics, Hotjar.
- 🔹 **Analogy to Remember:** Advertising Research = Doctor's stethoscope / Software Engineering's UAT.

> **🎯 Final Exam Mantra:** *"Always link the type of research → to the stage of campaign → to the marketing decision."* This 3-step chain is what examiners are hunting for in your answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Outdoor, Transit, and Corporate Advertising
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT742 - MARKETING COMMUNICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Outdoor, Transit, and Corporate Advertising

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Outdoor Advertising (OOH - Out-of-Home Advertising)

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
Outdoor Advertising, also termed **Out-of-Home (OOH) Advertising**, is a non-broadcast, non-print mass-media communication format that delivers brand messages to target audiences *while they are physically outside their residences*. It is characterized by fixed, location-bound visual display systems designed for high-impression, broad-reach exposure over fixed geographic markets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Outdoor Advertising is one of the *Above-the-Line (ATL)* mass-media tools studied under the broader Marketing Communications mix. It is distinct from print, broadcast, and digital media because of its **fixed location and continuous, non-interactive visibility**.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of Outdoor Advertising as the **"Silent Town Crier"** of modern cities. Imagine an old-time town crier standing at a busy intersection, holding a giant painted board about a merchant's goods. Whether you're walking, driving, or waiting at a signal, you involuntarily glance at the board. You didn't choose to listen — but the message registered. That is exactly how a billboard, hoarding, or transit panel works. The consumer cannot 'click away' from it.

**Standard Media Metrics in OOH:**

| Metric | Standard Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **DEC (Daily Effective Circulation)** | **Daily count of viewers** | Number of people who *see* an OOH unit per day |
| **OTS (Opportunity to See)** | **Impressions-based** | Total visual exposure opportunities |
| **GRP (Gross Rating Point)** | **Reach × Frequency / 100** | Aggregated impact metric |
| **CPM (Cost Per Mille)** | **Cost per 1,000 impressions** | Standard pricing unit |

---

## 1.2 Transit Advertising

**Formal Definition:**
Transit Advertising is a specialized sub-category of Outdoor Advertising that places promotional messages **on, within, or around public or private modes of transportation**, including buses, taxis, metro rail, trains, trams, airlines, ferries, and at transit hubs (airports, railway stations, bus terminals).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** While all Transit Advertising is technically *OOH*, not all OOH is Transit Advertising. Transit advertising is uniquely characterized by **mobility** — the medium itself moves through geographic markets.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Transit advertising is like a **"Moving Billboard that pays its own taxi fare."** Imagine a brand-painted auto-rickshaw weaving through Kerala's bustling streets of Kochi or Thiruvananthapuram. As it moves from the railway station to the mall to the IT park, it delivers repeated impressions to three different audience clusters *within a single trip*. The medium carries itself, multiplies its reach, and creates what marketers call **"moving impressions."**

---

## 1.3 Corporate Advertising

**Formal Definition:**
Corporate Advertising (also called **Institutional Advertising**) is a strategic form of paid communication wherein an organization promotes **its overall corporate identity, values, philosophy, and reputation** — rather than any specific product or service — to influence stakeholder perceptions and build long-term brand equity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Distinction:** Corporate Advertising targets **multiple stakeholder groups** (investors, employees, government, public, NGOs), whereas product advertising targets **end-consumers**. The unit of sale is *trust and reputation*, not a SKU.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Corporate Advertising is the company's **"Public Resume."** If product advertising is a candidate campaigning for a specific job (selling a specific feature), corporate advertising is a celebrity publishing an autobiography to cement their *overall legacy* in the public mind. Infosys's "Navigate Your Next" campaign doesn't sell a software — it sells Infosys the institution.

---

## 1.4 Classification Overview (Three Pillars at a Glance)

```
Outdoor Advertising (OOH)
├── Static / Traditional OOH (Billboards, Hoardings, Posters, Banners, Wall Paintings)
├── Digital OOH / DOOH (LED Displays, Digital Billboards, Airport LCDs)
└── Ambient OOH (Innovative street furniture, 3D anamorphic displays)

Transit Advertising
├── Exterior Transit (Bus wraps, taxi-toppers, train sides)
├── Interior Transit (Car cards, back-seat frames, tray tables)
└── Transit Hubs (Airport signage, metro station panels)

Corporate Advertising
├── Image / Identity Advertising
├── Financial / Investor Relations Advertising
├── Recruitment Advertising
├── Public Service / Issue Advertising
└── Crisis Management / Advocacy Advertising
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Outdoor Advertising — Theoretical Framework

### 2.1.1 Core Characteristics of OOH
The operational success of OOH rests on the **"Four Pillars of OOH Effectiveness"**:

1. **Location Dominance** — The medium's value is fundamentally tied to high-traffic, high-visibility geographic points (highways, junctions, malls, airports).
2. **Continuous 24/7 Exposure** — Unlike TV or radio, OOH delivers impressions *around the clock*, with no on-off switching.
3. **Mass-Market Reach with Demographic Skew** — Reaches a broad cross-section; audience quality depends on site selection.
4. **Limited Message Window** — Communication must be **ultra-condensed**: typically **6–8 words maximum** (the famous "7-second rule").

> [!NOTE]
> **The 7-Second Rule:** Average OOH exposure is 5–7 seconds. Therefore, copy must be readable at distance and instantly comprehensible.

### 2.1.2 Major Formats of OOH (with operational specifications)

| Format | Typical Size | Location | Cost Tier |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Billboard (Standard)** | 20 ft × 10 ft | Highways, arterial roads | High |
| **Hoarding (Large)** | 30 ft × 20 ft and above | Major junctions, city entries | Very High |
| **Poster** | 20" × 30" | Smaller roads, retail zones | Medium |
| **Banner** | Variable | Building exteriors, event venues | Low–Medium |
| **Wall Painting / Wallscape** | Building-sized | Urban ghats, rural roads | Low |
| **Digital Billboard (LED)** | 14' × 48' | Premium city centers | Premium |
| **Street Furniture** | Pole kiosks, bus shelters | Urban pedestrian zones | Variable |

### 2.1.3 Advantages and Limitations of OOH

**Advantages:**
- **Captive Audience:** Cannot be switched off or skipped.
- **Geographic Targeting:** Pinpoint market selection (city/zone/road).
- **High Frequency:** Daily repetition reinforces memory.
- **Brand Reinforcement:** Builds top-of-mind awareness for mass brands.
- **24/7 Visibility:** Continuous exposure without airtime costs.

**Limitations:**
- **No Demographic Precision:** Cannot selectively target age/income groups.
- **Limited Message Length:** Copy is severely constrained.
- **No Direct Response Mechanism:** No built-in call-to-action tracking.
- **Static Measurement Challenges:** Difficult to measure exact impressions (unlike digital).
- **Visual Pollution / Regulatory Constraints:** Many municipal bylaws restrict size and illumination.

---

## 2.2 Transit Advertising — Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 Classification by Surface

1. **Exterior Transit Advertising** — Painted or vinyl-wrapped surfaces of vehicles (full bus wraps, taxi panels, auto-rickshaw branding).
2. **Interior Transit Advertising** — Placed *inside* vehicles: car cards (above windows in buses/trains), back-seat frames (taxis), tray tables (airlines), and overhead luggage panels.
3. **Terminal / Hub Advertising** — Static or digital panels at airports, railway stations, metro stations, and bus depots.

### 2.2.2 The Transit Advertising Value Chain

Transit media's unique strength lies in **dynamic reach compounding** — a single vehicle delivers impressions across multiple geographies per day. This is quantified by the concept of **"Rolling DEC"** (Daily Effective Circulation in motion).

### 2.2.3 Advantages and Limitations of Transit Advertising

**Advantages:**
- **Mobility = Extended Reach:** One vehicle reaches multiple markets per day.
- **Captive In-Vehicle Audience:** Commuters have no alternative visual stimuli.
- **Geographic Penetration:** Reaches Tier 2/Tier 3 markets where TV penetration is low.
- **Demographic Targeting:** Auto-rickshaw ads in IT corridors = affluent techies; Metro ads = urban professionals.

**Limitations:**
- **Exposure Variability:** Depends on traffic density and weather.
- **Maintenance Issues:** Vinyl damage, defacement common.
- **Audience Fragmentation:** Short attention spans during commute.
- **Limited Recall:** Lower retention than static billboards.

---

## 2.3 Corporate Advertising — Theoretical Framework

### 2.3.1 Strategic Objectives of Corporate Advertising

Corporate advertising is governed by the **PRIM Model** (a KTU-aligned conceptual framework):

- **P — Public Image Building:** Establishing favorable brand personality.
- **R — Reputation Management:** Defending/enhancing corporate reputation.
- **I — Investor Relations:** Communicating financial stability and growth.
- **M — Message Advocacy:** Taking positions on social, economic, or political issues.

### 2.3.2 Sub-Types of Corporate Advertising

| Sub-Type | Primary Audience | Objective | Classic Indian Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Image Advertising** | General Public | Build corporate identity | Tata Group — "Values for All" |
| **Financial / IR Advertising** | Investors, Analysts | Communicate financial health | Reliance Annual Report ads |
| **Recruitment Advertising** | Job Seekers | Attract talent | Google India "Build with Me" |
| **Public Service Advertising** | Society at large | Support social causes | Infosys Foundation campaigns |
| **Advocacy / Issue Advertising** | Policymakers, Public | Influence policy debate | ITC's e-Choupal advocacy |
| **Crisis Management Advertising** | Stakeholders | Rebuild trust post-crisis | Nestlé Maggi re-launch ads |

### 2.3.3 Advantages and Limitations of Corporate Advertising

**Advantages:**
- **Long-term Equity Creation:** Builds intangible brand value.
- **Stakeholder Trust:** Reinforces investor and employee confidence.
- **Differentiation Beyond Product:** Insulates brand from product failures.
- **Defensive Capability:** Buffers during PR crises.

**Limitations:**
- **Intangible ROI:** Difficult to attribute direct sales impact.
- **High Cost:** Premium mass-media slots required.
- **Risk of Cynicism:** Poorly executed campaigns face backlash (e.g., "greenwashing").
- **Slow Impact:** Effects manifest over years, not weeks.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Daily Effective Circulation (DEC)** | $DEC = \sum (\text{Audience at each site per day})$ | OOH reach measurement |
| 2 | **Cost Per Mille (CPM)** | $CPM = \dfrac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Total Impressions}} \times 1000$ | Comparative media costing |
| 3 | **Gross Rating Points (GRP)** | $GRP = \text{Reach} \times \text{Frequency}$ | OOH campaign impact |
| 4 | **OTS (Opportunity to See)** | $OTS = DEC \times \text{Duration (days)}$ | Cumulative exposure |
| 5 | **Effective Frequency** | $EF \geq 3$ exposures for message retention | OOH frequency planning |
| 6 | **Frequency** | $F = \dfrac{OTS}{\text{Reach}}$ | Media plan audit |
| 7 | **Reach** | $R = \dfrac{OTS}{F}$ | Unique audience estimation |
| 8 | **Transit DEC Multiplier** | $TDEC = DEC_{\text{static}} \times \text{Route Factor}$ | Moving media reach |
| 9 | **Corporate Ad ROI Proxy** | $ROI_{corp} = \dfrac{\Delta \text{Brand Equity Score}}{\text{Ad Spend}}$ | Long-term valuation |
| 10 | **Share of Voice (SOV)** | $SOV = \dfrac{\text{Brand Ad Spend}}{\text{Category Ad Spend}} \times 100$ | Competitive positioning |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** These formulas are the *lingua franca* of media planning agencies (e.g., GroupM, Dentsu, Mindshare). KTU examiners often frame numerical problems around CPM, GRP, and DEC calculations.

---

## 2.5 Interrelationship: Where OOH, Transit, and Corporate Advertising Converge

The three formats are **complementary tools** in an integrated marketing communications (IMC) plan:

- An FMCG brand may combine **OOH (billboards)** for awareness, **Transit (auto-rickshaw wraps)** for last-mile penetration, and **Corporate (parent brand campaigns)** for trust-building.
- This stacked approach is termed **"Hyperlocal Brand Stacking"** — a concept widely applied in Kerala's regional markets.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Calculating DEC and OTS for an OOH Campaign

### Problem Statement (KTU-Style):
A beverage brand plans to install 5 billboards along the NH-66 corridor in Kerala. Each billboard has an estimated daily effective circulation (DEC) of **8,000 viewers**. The campaign will run for **30 days**.

**Calculate:**
(a) The cumulative Opportunity to See (OTS).
(b) The estimated Reach, assuming the Average Frequency is **4**.
(c) The CPM, given the total campaign cost is **₹2,40,000**.

---

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify Given Variables**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of Sites} &= 5 \\
\text{DEC per Site} &= 8{,}000 \text{ viewers/day} \\
\text{Campaign Duration} &= 30 \text{ days} \\
\text{Average Frequency (F)} &= 4 \\
\text{Total Cost} &= \text{₹}2{,}40{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Total Daily Effective Circulation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total DEC} &= \text{Number of Sites} \times \text{DEC per Site} \\
\text{Total DEC} &= 5 \times 8{,}000 \\
\text{Total DEC} &= 40{,}000 \text{ viewers/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Opportunity to See (OTS)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OTS} &= \text{Total DEC} \times \text{Duration (days)} \\
\text{OTS} &= 40{,}000 \times 30 \\
\text{OTS} &= 12{,}00{,}000 \text{ impressions (12 lakh)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Estimated Reach**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reach} &= \dfrac{\text{OTS}}{\text{Frequency}} \\
\text{Reach} &= \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{4} \\
\text{Reach} &= 3{,}00{,}000 \text{ unique viewers (3 lakh)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Cost Per Mille (CPM)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPM &= \dfrac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Total Impressions}} \times 1000 \\
CPM &= \dfrac{2{,}40{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} \times 1000 \\
CPM &= \dfrac{2{,}40{,}000}{1200} \\
CPM &= \text{₹}200 \text{ per 1,000 impressions}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Answer Summary

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| **Total DEC** | **40,000 / day** |
| **OTS** | **12,00,000 impressions** |
| **Reach** | **3,00,000 unique viewers** |
| **CPM** | **₹200** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concept Reinforcement:** Notice how *Reach* is *always* smaller than *OTS* — this is because one unique viewer may have multiple opportunities to see the ad (Frequency = 4 here means each viewer saw it on average 4 times).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Transit Advertising — Rolling DEC

### Problem Statement:
A branded metro train in Kochi travels an average of **120 km per day** across 8 stations. Each station has an estimated footfall of **3,000 commuters**. The train wraps (exterior) deliver additional impressions to on-road viewers at a ratio of **1.5 on-road impressions per station commuter**.

**Calculate:**
(a) The total Rolling DEC (in-station + on-road).
(b) The monthly OTS if the campaign runs for 60 days.

---

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute In-Station DEC**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{In-Station DEC} &= \text{Stations} \times \text{Footfall per Station} \\
\text{In-Station DEC} &= 8 \times 3{,}000 \\
\text{In-Station DEC} &= 24{,}000 \text{ commuters/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute On-Road DEC (Exterior Wraps)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{On-Road DEC} &= \text{In-Station DEC} \times \text{On-Road Ratio} \\
\text{On-Road DEC} &= 24{,}000 \times 1.5 \\
\text{On-Road DEC} &= 36{,}000 \text{ on-road viewers/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Total Rolling DEC**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Rolling DEC} &= \text{In-Station DEC} + \text{On-Road DEC} \\
\text{Total Rolling DEC} &= 24{,}000 + 36{,}000 \\
\text{Total Rolling DEC} &= 60{,}000 \text{ impressions/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Monthly OTS (60 days)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OTS} &= \text{Total Rolling DEC} \times \text{Duration} \\
\text{OTS} &= 60{,}000 \times 60 \\
\text{OTS} &= 36{,}00{,}000 \text{ impressions (36 lakh)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Corporate Advertising Effectiveness Analysis

### Problem Statement:
A corporate campaign by an IT firm in Kerala results in the following brand-trust scores (measured annually via a 100-point Likert scale):

| Stakeholder Group | Pre-Campaign Score | Post-Campaign Score |
|---|---|---|
| Investors | 62 | 74 |
| Employees | 70 | 82 |
| General Public | 55 | 63 |
| Government / Regulators | 58 | 71 |

The campaign cost ₹1 crore. Compute the **average uplift** and the **brand-equity ROI proxy**.

---

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Uplift per Stakeholder Group**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Uplift}_{\text{Investors}} &= 74 - 62 = 12 \\
\text{Uplift}_{\text{Employees}} &= 82 - 70 = 12 \\
\text{Uplift}_{\text{Public}} &= 63 - 55 = 8 \\
\text{Uplift}_{\text{Regulators}} &= 71 - 58 = 13
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Average Uplift**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Avg Uplift} &= \dfrac{12 + 12 + 8 + 13}{4} \\
\text{Avg Uplift} &= \dfrac{45}{4} \\
\text{Avg Uplift} &= 11.25 \text{ points}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Brand-Equity ROI Proxy**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ROI_{corp} &= \dfrac{\Delta \text{Brand Equity Score}}{\text{Ad Spend (in lakhs)}} \\
ROI_{corp} &= \dfrac{11.25}{100} \\
ROI_{corp} &= 0.1125 \text{ points per lakh invested}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Each ₹1 lakh invested generated a 0.1125-point increase in average stakeholder trust. While this seems small, corporate advertising compounds over years and across stakeholder groups — making it a long-term asset rather than a short-term sales driver.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix: OOH vs. Transit vs. Corporate Advertising

| Evaluation Parameter | Outdoor (OOH) | Transit | Corporate |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Objective** | Mass awareness | Mobile reach + frequency | Image & reputation |
| **Audience Type** | Broad public | Commuters, transit users | Multiple stakeholders |
| **Mobility** | Static | Mobile | Static (media-mix driven) |
| **Message Length** | Ultra-short (6–8 words) | Short (3–5 words) | Long-form (1–2 minutes) |
| **Measurement Difficulty** | High (DEC-based) | Very High (route-dependent) | Medium (survey-based) |
| **Cost Efficiency** | High CPM efficiency | Highest reach per rupee | Lowest direct ROI |
| **Persistence** | 24/7 (until dismantled) | Route-dependent (8–12 hrs active) | Media-mix dependent |
| **Crisis Sensitivity** | Low | Low | Very High |
| **KTU Cognitive Level** | Remember / Understand | Apply / Analyze | Analyze / Evaluate |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
