<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Outdoor Advertising. List any four formats of OOH advertising. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Outdoor Advertising (OOH) refers to any form of advertising that reaches consumers **while they are outside their homes** through visual media placed in public spaces. It is a mass-media communication tool designed to deliver brand messages at fixed geographic locations.

Four formats of OOH advertising are:
1. **Billboards** — Large panels along highways and arterial roads.
2. **Hoardings** — Oversized displays at major junctions and city entries.
3. **Posters** — Smaller printed displays in retail zones and secondary roads.
4. **Wall Paintings / Wallscapes** — Painted advertising on building exteriors, common in Tier 2/Tier 3 markets.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Outdoor Advertising and Transit Advertising. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Outdoor Advertising | Transit Advertising |
|---|---|---|
| **Mobility** | Static (fixed location) | Mobile (moves with vehicle) |
| **Primary Reach** | Pedestrians and motorists at a fixed point | Commuters across multiple routes |
| **Cost Structure** | Site rental + fabrication | Vehicle wrap cost + route fees |
| **Audience Behavior** | Passing glance (5–7 sec) | Captive in-transit audience |
| **Measurement** | DEC at site | Rolling DEC across routes |
| **Example** | Billboard at Marine Drive, Kochi | Branded KSRTC bus running NH-66 |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Answer `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Question:** 
(a) Explain the concept of **Corporate Advertising** in detail. Discuss its various types with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**
(b) Critically evaluate the **advantages and limitations of Outdoor Advertising** as a mass-media tool in modern marketing. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer to Part (a) — Corporate Advertising

Corporate Advertising, also referred to as **Institutional Advertising**, is a strategic form of paid communication in which an organization promotes its **overall corporate identity, values, philosophy, and reputation** rather than any specific product or service. The primary objective is to influence stakeholder perceptions and build long-term intangible brand equity.

#### Types of Corporate Advertising

1. **Image Advertising** — Aims to create a favorable perception of the company as a whole. *Example:* Tata Group's "Values for All" campaign reinforces its identity as a responsible Indian conglomerate.

2. **Financial / Investor Relations Advertising** — Communicates financial health, growth prospects, and stability to investors and analysts. *Example:* Reliance's annual report advertising in business publications.

3. **Recruitment Advertising** — Positions the company as an employer of choice to attract top talent. *Example:* Google's "Build with Me" recruitment campaign.

4. **Public Service / CSR Advertising** — Highlights the company's contribution to social causes. *Example:* Infosys Foundation's education and healthcare initiatives.

5. **Advocacy / Issue Advertising** — Takes a position on social, economic, or regulatory issues to influence public opinion or policy. *Example:* ITC's e-Choupal advocacy promoting agricultural reform.

6. **Crisis Management Advertising** — Used to rebuild trust and reputation following a PR crisis. *Example:* Maggi's re-launch campaign post the 2015 lead-content controversy.

---

### Model Answer to Part (b) — OOH: Advantages and Limitations

#### Advantages of Outdoor Advertising

1. **High Reach and Frequency** — A well-placed hoarding at a city junction can generate millions of impressions monthly at a relatively low CPM.
2. **Continuous 24/7 Exposure** — Unlike broadcast media, OOH delivers round-the-clock visibility without recurring airtime costs.
3. **Geographic Targeting** — Brands can hyper-localize their campaigns by selecting specific cities, zones, or even specific roads.
4. **Captive Audience** — Consumers cannot switch off or skip an OOH ad, ensuring involuntary brand exposure.
5. **Reinforces Other Media** — OOH amplifies TV and digital campaigns by providing contextual physical-world presence.

#### Limitations of Outdoor Advertising

1. **No Demographic Selectivity** — OOH reaches everyone passing by, regardless of whether they fit the target audience.
2. **Limited Message Length** — The 7-second rule constrains copy to 6–8 words maximum, limiting message complexity.
3. **Measurement Challenges** — Unlike digital media, exact impressions are estimated via DEC rather than tracked precisely.
4. **High Creative Production Costs** — Large-format printing, vinyl fabrication, and installation are capital-intensive.
5. **Regulatory and Environmental Constraints** — Municipal restrictions on size, illumination, and visual pollution limit deployment.
6. **No Direct Response Mechanism** — Lacks built-in call-to-action tracking (no clickable CTA, no QR-code habit historically).

#### Critical Evaluation

In modern integrated marketing communications, OOH functions best as a **support medium** rather than a primary driver. It excels at **awareness, recall, and brand reinforcement** but underperforms on **conversion and direct response**. Therefore, its strategic utility is highest when integrated with digital and broadcast media for omnichannel impact.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Question:**
(a) Define **Transit Advertising**. Explain its various forms with examples. Discuss its effectiveness in reaching Tier 2 and Tier 3 markets in India. **(7 Marks)**
(b) Compute the following for an OOH campaign: A jewelry brand installs **6 billboards** in Thrissur city. Each billboard delivers a **DEC of 5,000**. The campaign runs for **45 days**. The total cost is **₹2,70,000**. Calculate (i) Total DEC per day, (ii) Total OTS, (iii) Estimated Reach (assume Frequency = 3), and (iv) CPM. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer to Part (a) — Transit Advertising

#### Definition
Transit Advertising is a specialized form of Outdoor Advertising that places promotional messages **on, within, or around** public and private transportation vehicles and at transit hubs. Its defining feature is **mobility** — the medium physically moves through geographic markets, multiplying its reach.

#### Forms of Transit Advertising

1. **Exterior Transit Advertising**
   - **Bus Wraps** — Full vinyl wraps covering the exterior of public buses.
   - **Taxi Panels** — Branded panels on the sides and tops of cabs.
   - **Auto-Rickshaw Branding** — Common in Kerala and Tier 2 cities; cost-effective and ubiquitous.
   - **Train Sides** — Painted or vinyl-clad panels on metro and intercity trains.

2. **Interior Transit Advertising**
   - **Car Cards** — Posters placed above windows inside buses and trains.
   - **Back-Seat Frames** — Small video screens or printed inserts behind taxi seats.
   - **Tray Tables** — Advertising panels on airline seat-back trays.

3. **Transit Hub Advertising**
   - **Airport Signage** — Backlit panels and digital displays at terminals.
   - **Metro/Subway Station Panels** — Static and digital ads at platform walls.
   - **Bus Terminal Branding** — Standees and backlit panels at depots.

#### Effectiveness in Tier 2/Tier 3 Markets

Transit advertising is **exceptionally effective in Tier 2/Tier 3 markets** for several reasons:

- **Higher Commuter Dependency:** Public transport usage is significantly higher than in metros, ensuring a captive audience.
- **Lower TV/Digital Penetration:** Transit compensates for weak broadcast and digital media reach in smaller markets.
- **Hyperlocal Affinity:** Auto-rickshaw and local bus branding allows micro-targeting of specific neighborhoods and routes.
- **Cost Efficiency:** CPM is significantly lower than premium metro billboards, yielding superior cost-per-impression ratios.
- **Cultural Relevance:** In Kerala's smaller towns (e.g., Kottayam, Palakkad, Kannur), local-language transit advertising resonates deeply.

#### Conclusion
Transit advertising serves as the **"last-mile awareness vehicle"** in India's marketing mix, particularly potent in non-metro markets where traditional media penetration is constrained.

---

### Model Answer to Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of Sites} &= 6 \\
\text{DEC per Site} &= 5{,}000 \\
\text{Duration} &= 45 \text{ days} \\
\text{Frequency (F)} &= 3 \\
\text{Total Cost} &= \text{₹}2{,}70{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (i) — Total DEC per day:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total DEC} &= 6 \times 5{,}000 = 30{,}000 \text{ viewers/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii) — Total OTS:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OTS} &= 30{,}000 \times 45 = 13{,}50{,}000 \text{ impressions}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (iii) — Estimated Reach:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reach} &= \dfrac{\text{OTS}}{F} = \dfrac{13{,}50{,}000}{3} = 4{,}50{,}000 \text{ unique viewers}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (iv) — CPM:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPM &= \dfrac{2{,}70{,}000}{13{,}50{,}000} \times 1000 = \text{₹}200 \text{ per 1,000 impressions}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Summary Table:**

| Metric | Computed Value |
|---|---|
| Total DEC | 30,000/day |
| Total OTS | 13,50,000 impressions |
| Reach | 4,50,000 unique viewers |
| CPM | ₹200 |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse DEC with Reach.** DEC = total impressions (with repetition); Reach = unique audience. Examiners *specifically deduct marks* when students swap these two.
> - **Always state units** (per day, impressions, viewers, ₹). Losing 0.5–1 mark for missing units is the most common valuation pitfall.
> - **In definition questions**, students often *only* write textbook definitions without classifying types. Always include at least **3–4 sub-types** for full marks.
> - **In corporate advertising questions**, examiners expect explicit stakeholder mapping. Failing to mention *investors vs. employees vs. public* results in a 1–2 mark deduction.
> - **Numerical answers** must show *every formula substitution step*. A direct final answer without working fetches only 50% marks even if correct.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

### Core Conceptual Anchors
- **OOH** = Static, location-bound, 24/7 mass-media visibility.
- **Transit** = Mobile sub-segment of OOH; reaches commuters and transit hubs.
- **Corporate Advertising** = Reputation-driven; promotes the *institution*, not the *product*.

### Must-Remember Definitions
- **DEC (Daily Effective Circulation):** Estimated daily viewers of an OOH unit.
- **OTS (Opportunity to See):** Cumulative impressions over campaign duration.
- **CPM:** Cost per 1,000 impressions — standard media pricing unit.
- **GRP:** Reach × Frequency — aggregated campaign impact metric.
- **PRIM Model:** Public Image, Reputation, Investor Relations, Message Advocacy — the four pillars of corporate advertising.

### Must-Remember Formulas
$$
DEC_{\text{total}} = \text{Sites} \times DEC_{\text{per site}}
$$
$$
OTS = DEC_{\text{total}} \times \text{Days}
$$
$$
\text{Reach} = \dfrac{OTS}{\text{Frequency}}
$$
$$
CPM = \dfrac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Impressions}} \times 1000
$$

### Must-Remember Examples (KTU Favourite)
- **Tata Group** → Image Corporate Advertising.
- **Maggi Re-launch** → Crisis Management Corporate Advertising.
- **KSRTC / Private Bus Wraps in Kerala** → Transit Advertising.
- **Marine Drive Billboard, Kochi** → Static OOH.
- **Cochin International Airport Panels** → Hub Advertising.

### Critical Pitfalls to Avoid
- ❌ Treating OOH and Transit as synonymous.
- ❌ Forgetting that Corporate Advertising targets **multiple stakeholders**, not just consumers.
- ❌ Confusing Reach (unique) with Frequency (repetitions).
- ❌ Ignoring the **7-second rule** when designing OOH copy.

### KTU Cognitive-Level Mapping (Bloom's Taxonomy)
- **Remember/Understand:** Define OOH, Transit, Corporate; list formats.
- **Apply:** Compute DEC, OTS, CPM in numerical problems.
- **Analyze:** Compare OOH vs. Transit vs. Corporate on key parameters.
- **Evaluate:** Critically assess advantages and limitations in real-world contexts.

> **Final Exam Tip:** Structure every answer using the **"Definition → Classification → Examples → Advantages → Limitations → Conclusion"** flow. This six-part structure is the *de facto* KTU valuation benchmark and consistently earns full marks across all three sub-topics.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sales Promotion and Publicity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT742 - MARKETING COMMUNICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sales Promotion and Publicity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: SALES PROMOTION AND PUBLICITY

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Sales Promotion — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Sales Promotion (SP)** refers to a collection of short-term, incentive-driven marketing activities designed to stimulate immediate customer purchase, accelerate product trial, or encourage resellers/staff to push a brand's products more aggressively than they normally would.

According to the **American Marketing Association (AMA)**, sales promotion encompasses *"those marketing activities, other than personal selling, advertising, and publicity, that stimulate consumer purchasing and dealer effectiveness, such as displays, shows and expositions, demonstrations, and various other non-recurrent selling efforts not in the ordinary routine."*

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework, Sales Promotion is positioned as the **"Action Trigger"** of the Integrated Marketing Communications (IMC) mix — the tactical lever pulled to convert latent intent into actual buying behaviour.

### 1.2 Publicity — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Publicity** is the non-paid, non-personal stimulation of demand for a product, service, or business unit by securing favourable news coverage in mass media, or by presenting it favourably through a third-party endorsement, platform, or showcase — without the sponsor paying the media for the time/space.

Publicity is considered the **"Earned Voice"** of marketing communications — its credibility stems from being communicated by an **independent editorial source**, not the brand itself.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Movie Premiere Analogy**

Imagine you are releasing a new film. You would:

- **Run a Trailer (Advertising)** — You pay TV channels to broadcast a 30-second clip of your film.
- **Invite Film Critics for a Private Screening (Publicity)** — You do NOT pay them, but you give them exclusive access. If they like the film, they publish a *free* review in tomorrow's newspaper — a much more trusted voice than your own trailer.
- **Hand Out Free Popcorn Coupons at the Mall (Sales Promotion)** — A short-term, tangible incentive to convert onlookers into paying audience members TODAY.

**Sales Promotion** = *pushing the wallet open* (incentive-driven, paid by brand, short-term).
**Publicity** = *borrowing someone else's megaphone* (credibility-driven, unpaid by brand, third-party validated).

### 1.4 Distinguishing the Two from Other IMC Tools

| IMC Element | Paid? | Direct Control? | Time Horizon | Trigger |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Advertising | Yes | High | Long-term | Brand recall / awareness |
| Personal Selling | Yes (salary) | High | Medium-term | One-to-one conversion |
| **Sales Promotion** | **Yes** | **High** | **Short-term** | **Immediate action** |
| **Publicity** | **No** | **Low** | **Variable** | **Third-party validation** |
| Direct Marketing | Yes | High | Medium-term | Database-driven response |

The **trade-off** is unmistakable: Sales Promotion gives **speed and control**; Publicity gives **credibility and reach at zero media cost** but at the cost of message control.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Strategic Logic of Sales Promotion

Sales Promotion operates on the **Behavioural Response Model (BRM)** — the principle that any well-structured incentive temporarily **lowers the psychological price** of action, raising the probability of purchase at that moment.

The core mechanics can be unfolded into four operational steps:

- **Stimulus Generation** — The brand offers an extra value (coupon, free gift, contest).
- **Psychological Acceleration** — Consumer's perceived cost falls; perceived gain rises.
- **Trial / Repeat Conversion** — Customer tries the brand for the first time or stockpiles.
- **Lock-In Effect (or Attrition)** — Post-promotion, brand must convert the trial into loyalty.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why sales promotion is NOT a substitute for advertising:** A promotion without prior awareness is a *"discount on an empty shop."* Promotion exploits awareness built by advertising — it does NOT create it.

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Sales Promotion Tools

Sales Promotion splits into **three primary audiences**: the consumer, the trade channel, and the sales force.

### A. Consumer-Oriented Sales Promotion (Pull Strategy)

These tools motivate the **end consumer** to demand the brand from retailers.

- **Samples** — Free trial units (highest cost, fastest trial).
- **Coupons** — Digital or printed vouchers offering price reduction.
- **Premiums** — Free merchandise offered with purchase ("Buy 1 Get 1 Free", free keychain).
- **Price-Off / Discount** — Temporary reduction of MRP.
- **Loyalty Programs / Reward Points** — Cumulative benefits for repeat purchase.
- **Contests & Sweepstakes** — Chance or skill-based games with prizes.
- **Refund / Rebate Offers** — Post-purchase cash-back.
- **Bonus Packs** — Extra quantity at the same price ("33% Extra Free").
- **Demonstrations & Sampling at Point-of-Sale (POS)** — Live trials in store.

### B. Trade-Oriented Sales Promotion (Push Strategy)

These tools motivate **wholesalers, retailers, and distributors** to stock, display, and push the brand.

- **Trade Discounts** — Off-invoice margin enhancement.
- **Volume Bonuses / Off-Invoice Allowances** — Reward for ordering in bulk.
- **Display Allowances** — Pay for prime shelf space or end-cap displays.
- **Free Goods (Case-Giveaway)** — Extra units free on a minimum purchase.
- **Sales Contests** — Competition among dealers for prizes.
- **Dealer Training** — Educating retailers on product features.
- **Co-operative Advertising (Co-op Ads)** — Brand shares advertising cost with retailer.

### C. Sales Force-Oriented Promotion

- **Sales Contests** — Reward top performers with trips, prizes.
- **Bonus Structures** — SPIFFs (Sales Performance Incentive Fund).
- **Recognition Awards** — Trophies, certificates, public listing.

## 2.3 Publicity — Characteristics & Mechanism

Publicity is uniquely positioned because of the **Third-Party Endorsement Effect**. A news story in *The Hindu* or a review on YouTube carries the credibility of the platform, not the brand.

### 2.4 Distinguishing Publicity from Public Relations (PR)

> [!NOTE]
> Many students confuse Publicity with Public Relations. The KTU examiner distinguishes them sharply.

| Dimension | Publicity | Public Relations (PR) |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | A *tactic* within PR | A *strategic function* of the firm |
| Focus | Specific product/news item | Overall corporate image |
| Time-frame | Reactive / one-shot event | Continuous relationship-building |
| Tools | Press release, press conference | Press release + events + community + CSR + lobbying + internal communication |
| Output | Earned media coverage | Managed reputation |

### 2.5 Tools of Publicity

- **Press Releases** — Formal written announcement to journalists.
- **Press Conferences** — Live Q&A with the media.
- **Media Kits** — Background dossier with photos, data, and product samples.
- **Sponsorships & Events** — Brand-backed sports/cultural events attracting coverage.
- **Product Launches / Stunts** — Publicity-generating events (e.g., Red Bull Stratos).
- **Celebrity / Influencer Endorsements** — When unpaid.
- **Crisis Communication** — Damage control via press briefings.

## 2.6 Advantages & Limitations (Examiner's Favourite)

### Advantages of Sales Promotion
- Generates immediate, measurable response.
- Encourages product trial among non-users.
- Boosts short-term sales volume and stock turnover.
- Strengthens trade relationships and shelf presence.

### Limitations of Sales Promotion
- **Erosion of Brand Equity** — Frequent discounts teach customers to *never* buy at full price.
- **Promotional Dependency** — Sales dip immediately after the promotion ends.
- **Race-to-the-Bottom** — Competitors retaliate; industry margins collapse.
- **Image Dilution** — Premium brands risk losing their premium positioning.

### Advantages of Publicity
- **High Credibility** — Third-party endorsement carries 3× trust of paid advertising.
- **Zero Media Cost** — No payment for time/space.
- **Mass Reach** — A single *BBC* or *CNN* story can reach millions globally.
- **Image Enhancement** — Builds long-term brand goodwill.

### Limitations of Publicity
- **No Control** — Journalists may write unfavourable stories.
- **Timing Uncertain** — News cycles are unpredictable.
- **One-Shot Effect** — A story is quickly displaced by newer news.
- **Hard to Measure ROI** — Unlike coupons, publicity impact is qualitative.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in numerical/analytical Part B questions.**

| Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Promotion Effectiveness Ratio | $\text{PER} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Sales during promotion}}{\text{Sales before promotion}}$ | Baseline sales benchmark |
| Promotional ROI | $\text{ROI}_{p} \;=\; \dfrac{(\Delta \text{Profit} - \text{Promotion Cost})}{\text{Promotion Cost}} \times 100$ | Profitability check |
| Break-Even Quantity (Promotion) | $\text{BEQ} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Promotional Cost}}{\text{Offer Price} - \text{Variable Cost}}$ | Minimum units to recover promo spend |
| Trade Discount Effective Rate | $P_{r} \;=\; P_{m} \times (1 - d_{1})(1 - d_{2})\dots(1 - d_{n})$ | Successive discounts on MRP $P_{m}$ |
| Sell-Through Rate | $\text{STR} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Units Sold to Consumer}}{\text{Units Delivered to Retailer}} \times 100$ | Channel push effectiveness |
| Publicity Reach Score | $\text{PRS} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} (C_{i} \times W_{i})$ | $C_{i}$=circulation, $W_{i}$=credibility weight |
| Pull-to-Push Ratio | $\text{PPR} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Consumer Promo Spend}}{\text{Trade Promo Spend}}$ | Strategic mix balance |
| Brand Equity Decay Post-Promo | $B_{t} \;=\; B_{0} \cdot e^{-kt}$ | $B_{0}$=pre-promo equity, $k$=decay constant |

### Symbol Glossary
- $P_{m}$ = Maximum Retail Price (MRP) before discount
- $d_{i}$ = Each successive trade discount (in decimal)
- $P_{r}$ = Final realised price to retailer
- $C_{i}$ = Circulation of the $i^{th}$ media outlet
- $W_{i}$ = Editorial credibility weight of that outlet

## 2.8 Real-World Utility of These Formulas

The PER and ROI equations are used by **FMCG brand managers** (Hindustan Unilever, ITC) to decide whether a "Buy 1 Get 1" offer justifies the margin sacrifice. The Trade Discount chain equation is used in **B2B negotiations** where cascading discounts are common (pharma distribution, electronics). Publicity Reach Score is used by **PR agencies** to value earned media against paid equivalents (AVE — Advertising Value Equivalent).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Successive Trade Discount Realised Price

When multiple discounts are offered to a trade partner (e.g., *20% + 5% + 2%*), they are **not additive**. The correct formula must be derived.

**Given:**
- MRP (List Price) = $P_{m}$
- Successive trade discounts: $d_{1}, d_{2}, d_{3}, \dots, d_{n}$ (each expressed as a decimal)

**Step 1 — Apply the first discount:**

After $d_{1}$ is applied, the price becomes:

$$P_{1} \;=\; P_{m} - (d_{1} \cdot P_{m}) \;=\; P_{m}(1 - d_{1})$$

**Step 2 — Apply the second discount on $P_{1}$:**

Note that the second discount is applied on the *already reduced* price, not the original MRP:

$$P_{2} \;=\; P_{1}(1 - d_{2}) \;=\; P_{m}(1 - d_{1})(1 - d_{2})$$

**Step 3 — Continue the chain for $n$ successive discounts:**

The pattern generalises to:

$$\boxed{\,P_{r} \;=\; P_{m} \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - d_{i})\,}$$

This is the **Successive Trade Discount Realised Price Equation** — fundamental for any Part B numerical question on channel margins.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Question:** A product with MRP ₹1,000 is offered to a retailer with successive trade discounts of 20%, 10%, and 5%. What is the **final realised price** and the **effective single discount**?

**Solution — Step 1: Compute successive realised price.**

$$P_{r} \;=\; 1000 \times (1 - 0.20)(1 - 0.10)(1 - 0.05)$$

$$P_{r} \;=\; 1000 \times 0.80 \times 0.90 \times 0.95$$

$$P_{r} \;=\; 1000 \times 0.684 \;=\; \text{₹}684$$

**Step 2: Calculate the effective single discount.**

$$d_{\text{eff}} \;=\; \frac{P_{m} - P_{r}}{P_{m}} \;=\; \frac{1000 - 684}{1000} \;=\; 0.316$$

$$\boxed{\,d_{\text{eff}} \;=\; 31.6\%\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Common student error:** Adding 20 + 10 + 5 = 35%. The correct effective discount is **31.6%**, *not* 35%. Successive discounts compound multiplicatively, not additively.

## 3.2 Derivation: Break-Even Quantity for a Sales Promotion

A brand manager must determine the **minimum number of units** that must be sold during a promotional period to recover the promotional investment.

**Given Variables:**

- $C_{f}$ = Fixed promotional cost (printing coupons, POS material, contest prizes)
- $V$ = Variable cost per unit
- $P_{o}$ = Promotional offer price (discounted price)
- $\pi$ = Profit per unit during promotion = $P_{o} - V$

**Step 1 — Set the total profit equal to total promotional cost.**

At break-even, the incremental profit exactly offsets the promotional cost:

$$\text{Total Incremental Profit} \;=\; \text{Promotional Cost}$$

**Step 2 — Express total incremental profit in terms of units sold $Q$.**

$$\pi \cdot Q \;=\; C_{f}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $Q$.**

$$\boxed{\,Q^{*} \;=\; \dfrac{C_{f}}{P_{o} - V}\,}$$

### Worked Numerical Example

> **Question:** A FMCG company launches a "20% Off" promotion on a snack pack normally priced ₹50. Variable cost per pack is ₹30. Fixed promotional cost (coupon printing, displays) is ₹20,000. How many units must be sold to break even?

**Step 1: Compute promotional offer price.**

$$P_{o} \;=\; 50 \times (1 - 0.20) \;=\; \text{₹}40$$

**Step 2: Compute per-unit profit during promotion.**

$$\pi \;=\; P_{o} - V \;=\; 40 - 30 \;=\; \text{₹}10$$

**Step 3: Apply break-even equation.**

$$Q^{*} \;=\; \frac{20{,}000}{10} \;=\; 2{,}000 \text{ units}$$

$$\boxed{\,Q^{*} \;=\; 2{,}000 \text{ units}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If the company's **baseline weekly sales** are below 2,000 units, the promotion is a financial loss and should be redesigned (smaller discount or higher offer price).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Push vs. Pull Strategy

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners love this contrast as it integrates consumer and trade promotion concepts.

| Parameter | Push Strategy (Trade Promotion) | Pull Strategy (Consumer Promotion) |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Manufacturer $\rightarrow$ Channel $\rightarrow$ Consumer | Manufacturer $\rightarrow$ Consumer $\rightarrow$ Channel |
| Primary Tool | Trade discounts, allowances, free goods | Coupons, samples, ads, contests |
| Trigger | Retailer pushes brand to consumer | Consumer demands brand from retailer |
| Cost Burden | High margin sacrifice upfront | High media & sampling cost |
| Lag Time | Quick distribution push | Slower, awareness-led |
| Suitable For | Convenience goods, established brands | New launches, differentiated brands |
| Risk | Channel may not push, just stockpile | Consumer may stock up and not repeat |

The **Pull-to-Push Ratio (PPR)** computed earlier is the diagnostic metric to balance the two strategies:

$$\text{PPR} \;=\; \frac{\text{Consumer Promo Spend}}{\text{Trade Promo Spend}}$$

A **high PPR** indicates a brand is consumer-led (e.g., *Apple*, *Nike*). A **low PPR** indicates a channel-led brand (e.g., *local FMCG snacks*).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Sales Promotion vs Publicity

| Dimension | Sales Promotion | Publicity |
|---|---|---|
| Cost to brand | Direct, quantifiable | Zero media spend |
| Control over message | Full | Minimal |
| Credibility | Moderate (incentive-driven) | Very high (third-party) |
| Speed of impact | Immediate | Variable |
| Measurability | High (redemption codes, sales lift) | Low (qualitative, AVE) |
| Risk | Brand equity erosion | Negative press risk |
| Best for | Short-term sales boost | Long-term image building |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sales Promotion — Audience-Based Architecture

This functional block diagram maps how sales promotion flows through three audiences simultaneously:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Manufacturer / Brand] --> B[Consumer Promotion]
    A --> C[Trade Promotion]
    A --> D[Sales Force Promotion]

    B --> B1[Samples]
    B --> B2[Coupons]
    B --> B3[Premiums / Gifts]
    B --> B4[Contests / Sweepstakes]
    B --> B5[Loyalty Rewards]

    C --> C1[Trade Discounts]
    C --> C2[Volume Bonuses]
    C --> C3[Display Allowances]
    C --> C4[Free Goods]
    C --> C5[Dealer Contests]

    D --> D1[SPIFFs]
    D --> D2[Sales Contests]
    D --> D3[Recognition Awards]

    B1 --> OUT1[End Consumer Trial]
    B2 --> OUT1
    B3 --> OUT1
    B4 --> OUT1
    B5 --> OUT1

    C1 --> OUT2[Channel Push to Consumer]
    C2 --> OUT2
    C3 --> OUT2
    C4 --> OUT2
    C5 --> OUT2

    D1 --> OUT3[Aggressive Field Selling]
    D2 --> OUT3
    D3 --> OUT3
```

## 4.2 Push vs Pull Strategy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Manufacturer] -->|Trade Discounts / Allowances| R[Retailer / Channel]
    R -->|Shelf Push / End-cap Display| CON[End Consumer]
    CON -.->|Demand Pull| R
    R -.->|Reorder Pull| M

    M2[Manufacturer] -->|Consumer Ads / Coupons / Samples| CON2[End Consumer]
    CON2 -.->|Asks Retailer| R2[Retailer]
    R2 -.->|Orders from| M2
```

## 4.3 Publicity Process Flow — From News to Earned Coverage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Brand Identifies Newsworthy Event] --> S2[Drafts Press Release]
    S2 --> S3[Distributes to Journalists]
    S3 --> S4{Journalist Interest?}
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[Editorial Coverage Published]
    S4 -- No --> S6[Re-pitch via Press Conference]
    S6 --> S3

    S5 --> S7[Audience Reads / Shares]
    S7 --> S8[Brand Visibility and Credibility Gained]
    S8 --> S9[Long-Term Image Building]
```

## 4.4 Sales Promotion Decision Tree (Choosing the Right Tool)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What is the goal?}
    Q1 -- Trial of New Product --> T1[Use Samples or Coupons]
    Q1 -- Repeat Purchase --> T2[Use Loyalty Programs or Bonus Packs]
    Q1 -- Channel Push --> T3[Use Trade Discounts or Free Goods]
    Q1 -- Sales Force Push --> T4[Use SPIFFs or Contests]

    T1 --> R[Monitor ROI and Break-Even]
    T2 --> R
    T3 --> R
    T4 --> R

    R --> D{Profitable?}
    D -- Yes --> K[Continue Promotion]
    D -- No --> M[Redesign or Discontinue]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PECBT742]**
**Define Sales Promotion. List any four consumer-oriented sales promotion tools with one-line examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Sales Promotion refers to short-term, incentive-driven marketing activities designed to stimulate immediate customer purchase, encourage product trial, or induce resellers/sales force to push the brand. Unlike advertising, it offers a tangible extra-value incentive to trigger action.

> **Four Consumer Tools (1 Mark for naming + 0.5 Mark for example):**
>
> - **Coupons** — Digital/printed vouchers offering a price reduction, e.g., Amazon ₹200 off coupon.
> - **Samples** — Free trial-size units distributed at malls, e.g., free sachet of shampoo.
> - **Premium / Free Gift** — A free product offered on purchase, e.g., free shaving razor with Gillette cartridge pack.
> - **Contests / Sweepstakes** — Chance or skill games with prizes, e.g., "Spin the Wheel" at retail counters.

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECBT742]**
**Differentiate between Publicity and Public Relations. Mention any two tools of publicity.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Publicity vs Public Relations (2 Marks):**
>
> | Basis | Publicity | Public Relations |
> |---|---|---|
> | Scope | A specific *tactic* — obtaining media coverage | An overall strategic *function* of the firm |
> | Focus | Promoting a specific product/event/news | Building and maintaining total corporate image |
> | Timeframe | Reactive, often one-shot | Continuous, long-term |
>
> **Tools of Publicity (1 Mark — any two):**
>
> - **Press Releases** — Formal written announcements sent to journalists.
> - **Press Conferences** — Live media Q&A sessions to disseminate important news.
> - **Media Kits** — Dossiers with photographs, statistics, and product background.
> - **Sponsorships & Events** — Brand-backed events that attract media coverage.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PECBT742]**

> **(a)** Explain the concept of **Sales Promotion** in detail. Discuss any **five consumer-oriented** sales promotion tools with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Differentiate between **Push and Pull strategies** of sales promotion. Illustrate with a suitable real-world example. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Concept of Sales Promotion (3 Marks):**

Sales Promotion is a key element of the **Integrated Marketing Communications (IMC)** mix. It comprises **short-term, incentive-based tactics** designed to stimulate immediate purchase, accelerate product trial, and induce resellers and sales personnel to actively push the brand. Unlike advertising — which builds long-term awareness — sales promotion creates a **short-term sense of urgency** and offers tangible extra value to the buyer.

Sales promotion works on the principle of **Behavioural Response**: by lowering the perceived cost or raising the perceived benefit, the brand temporarily raises the probability of purchase.

**2. Five Consumer-Oriented Tools (4 Marks — 0.8 Mark each):**

- **Samples** — Free trial-size products given directly to consumers, e.g., a sachet of *Lakme* face wash distributed at malls to encourage trial. *High cost, fastest trial generation.*

- **Coupons** — Printed or digital vouchers offering a price discount on redemption, e.g., a ₹100 off coupon on *Flipkart*. *Easy to track redemption rates.*

- **Premiums** — Free merchandise offered with a purchase, e.g., a free *OnePlus* earbud case with smartphone purchase. *Enhances perceived value.*

- **Bonus Packs** — Extra quantity at the same price, e.g., "30% Extra Free" on *Surf Excel*. *Boosts volume consumption without explicit discounting.*

- **Contests / Sweepstakes** — Chance-based promotional games with prizes, e.g., "Scratch and Win a car" by *Pepsi*. *Generates excitement and engagement.*

#### Model Answer — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**1. Push Strategy (3 Marks):**

In a **Push Strategy**, the manufacturer directs promotional effort *toward the trade channel* (wholesalers, retailers, distributors). Trade discounts, free goods, display allowances, and dealer contests are used to incentivise the channel to aggressively stock, display, and recommend the brand. The brand is **pushed** through the channel to reach the consumer.

*Example:* A company like *Hindustan Unilever* offers retailers a 10% off-invoice discount and a free display rack for stocking *Dove* soap. The retailer, motivated by the higher margin, prominently displays and recommends the brand to customers.

**2. Pull Strategy (3 Marks):**

In a **Pull Strategy**, the manufacturer directs promotional effort *directly at the end consumer* through advertising, coupons, samples, and consumer contests. The consumer becomes aware, develops preference, and **demands** the brand from retailers — effectively **pulling** the product through the channel.

*Example:* A new smartphone brand runs mass-media TV ads and offers launch-day coupons. Consumers arrive at stores specifically asking for the brand, forcing retailers to reorder.

**3. Comparative Analysis (1 Mark):**

| Aspect | Push | Pull |
|---|---|---|
| Target | Trade channel | End consumer |
| Tools | Trade discounts, free goods | Coupons, samples, ads |
| Speed | Quick distribution push | Slower, awareness-led |
| Cost | Margin sacrifice | Media & sampling cost |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECBT742]**

> **(a)** Define **Publicity**. Explain its characteristics, advantages, and limitations. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A product has an MRP of **₹2,000** and is offered to a retailer with successive trade discounts of **15%, 10%, and 5%**. Calculate the **final realised price** to the retailer and the **effective single discount rate**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Definition of Publicity (1.5 Marks):**

Publicity is a non-paid, non-personal form of marketing communication in which a favourable news story or editorial mention about a product, service, or organisation is secured in mass media — without the brand paying for the time or space. The credibility of publicity comes from the independence of the editorial source.

**2. Characteristics of Publicity (2 Marks):**

- **Earned, not paid** — The media is not compensated for the coverage.
- **Third-party validated** — Carries the credibility of an independent editorial source.
- **Non-personal** — Reaches a mass audience through media channels.
- **One-way communication** — Limited or no direct dialogue with the audience.
- **Uncontrollable** — The brand has no editorial authority over how the story is framed.
- **Time-sensitive** — Stories have a short shelf-life before being displaced by newer news.

**3. Advantages (2 Marks):**

- **High Credibility** — Audiences trust editorial coverage far more than brand-paid advertising.
- **Zero Media Cost** — Frees up marketing budget for tactical deployment.
- **Mass Reach** — A single feature in a top newspaper can reach millions.
- **Long-term Image Building** — Repeated favourable coverage builds brand goodwill.

**4. Limitations (1.5 Marks):**

- **Loss of Control** — Journalists may twist the story unfavourably.
- **Hard to Measure** — Difficult to quantify the exact sales impact.
- **Timing Risk** — News cycles are unpredictable; bad news may coincide with good news.
- **One-shot Nature** — Coverage quickly disappears from the public mind.

#### Model Answer — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**

- MRP, $P_{m}$ = ₹2,000
- Successive trade discounts: $d_{1}$ = 15%, $d_{2}$ = 10%, $d_{3}$ = 5%

**Step 1: Apply the successive discount formula (3 Marks).**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{r} \;=\; P_{m} \times (1 - d_{1})(1 - d_{2})(1 - d_{3}) \\
P_{r} \;=\; 2000 \times (1 - 0.15)(1 - 0.10)(1 - 0.05) \\
P_{r} \;=\; 2000 \times 0.85 \times 0.90 \times 0.95
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compute intermediate products (2 Marks).**

$$\begin{aligned}
0.85 \times 0.90 \;=\; 0.765 \\
0.765 \times 0.95 \;=\; 0.72675
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Final realised price (1 Mark).**

$$\boxed{\,P_{r} \;=\; 2000 \times 0.72675 \;=\; \text{₹}1{,}453.50\,}$$

**Step 4: Effective single discount rate (1 Mark).**

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{eff}} \;=\; \frac{P_{m} - P_{r}}{P_{m}} \\
d_{\text{eff}} \;=\; \frac{2000 - 1453.50}{2000} \;=\; \frac{546.50}{2000} \;=\; 0.27325
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\,d_{\text{eff}} \;=\; 27.33\%\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification Insight:** The naïve sum of discounts (15 + 10 + 5 = **30%**) is **incorrect**. The true effective single discount is **27.33%** because successive discounts compound multiplicatively, not additively.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones — Read Carefully:**
>
> - **Successive Trade Discounts** — A majority of students add the percentages (15 + 10 + 5 = 30%) instead of applying the multiplicative formula. **Always use** $P_{r} = P_{m} \times (1-d_{1})(1-d_{2})(1-d_{3})$. You will lose **2 to 3 marks** for this single error.
>
> - **Publicity vs Public Relations** — Many students treat them as synonymous. Examiners award marks only when you **distinguish scope, focus, and timeframe**. Memorise the comparison table.
>
> - **Push vs Pull Examples** — A vague answer like *"Push is for trade and Pull is for consumer"* earns only partial credit. Always pair the strategy with a **named brand and a specific tool**. Use *Hindustan Unilever* or *Apple iPhone* as illustrative anchors.
>
> - **Confusing Sales Promotion with Advertising** — Sales promotion is *tactical, short-term, incentive-led*; Advertising is *strategic, long-term, message-led*. Examiners penalise the blurring of these two IMC elements.
>
> - **Skipping the Formula in Numerical Questions** — Even if the final answer is correct, marks are reserved for **showing the formula** before substituting values. Always write: *"Using the Successive Trade Discount Formula: $P_{r} = P_{m} \cdot (1-d_1)(1-d_2)\dots$"*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Sales Promotion and Publicity**

- **Sales Promotion = Short-term, incentive-driven, paid by brand, measurable, high control.**
- **Publicity = Earned media, unpaid, third-party validated, low control, high credibility.**
- **Three Audiences for SP:** Consumer (Pull), Trade (Push), Sales Force.
- **Consumer tools:** Samples, Coupons, Premiums, Bonus Packs, Contests, Loyalty Programs.
- **Trade tools:** Trade Discounts, Free Goods, Display Allowances, Volume Bonuses, Dealer Contests.
- **Sales force tools:** SPIFFs, Sales Contests, Recognition Awards.
- **Push Strategy = Manufacturer → Channel → Consumer.**
- **Pull Strategy = Manufacturer → Consumer → Channel.**
- **Successive Trade Discount Formula:** $P_{r} = P_{m} \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - d_i)$ — never add discounts.
- **Break-Even Quantity:** $Q^{*} = C_f / (P_o - V)$.
- **Promotional ROI:** $\text{ROI}_p = (\Delta\text{Profit} - \text{Promotion Cost}) / \text{Promotion Cost} \times 100$.
- **Publicity ≠ Public Relations.** Publicity is a *tactic*; PR is a *strategic function*.
- **Tools of Publicity:** Press Release, Press Conference, Media Kit, Sponsorships, Events.
- **Publicity Pros:** Zero cost, high credibility, mass reach, image building.
- **Publicity Cons:** No message control, unpredictable timing, hard to measure, one-shot effect.
- **Golden Rule:** Promotion *exploits* awareness built by advertising — it does *not* create awareness.
- **Examiner Favourite Trap:** Treating successive discounts as additive (15+10+5 = 30%) — the correct effective discount is **27.33%** for that sequence.
- **Pull-to-Push Ratio (PPR)** is a strategic metric to balance the two strategies in the promotional budget mix.
- **Brand Equity Decay Post-Promo** follows $B_t = B_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ — frequent promotions erode long-term brand value.
- **Publicity's AVE (Advertising Value Equivalent)** is used by PR agencies to assign a notional rupee value to earned media coverage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Personified Promotion (Personal Selling)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT742 - MARKETING COMMUNICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Personified Promotion (Personal Selling)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Personal Selling (Personified Promotion):** A form of person-to-person communication in which a seller attempts to assist and/or persuade prospective buyers to purchase a product, service, or idea. Unlike mass-mediated promotion, personal selling involves a **live, interactive, two-way exchange** between the sales representative (the *encoder*) and the prospective customer (the *decoder*), allowing for immediate feedback, customization of the message, and relationship building.

In the KTU 2024 Marketing Communications framework, Personal Selling is positioned under the **Personal/Direct Marketing Communication Mix** element, which also includes Direct Marketing, Telemarketing, and Digital/Interactive Marketing. It is often called **"Personified Promotion"** because it humanizes the brand — the salesperson literally *becomes* the promotional message.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Personal Selling is the **oldest form of promotion** and remains the most effective in **High-Involvement, High-Value, B2B (Business-to-Business)**, and technically complex purchase situations such as industrial machinery, real estate, insurance, automobiles, and pharmaceutical detailing.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (The "First-Time Student" View)

Imagine you walk into a showroom to buy a laptop. The internet shows you a thousand models with specs, but you still feel confused. Then a sales executive walks up, asks you *"What will you use it for?"* and based on your answer (gaming vs coding vs travel), tailors a recommendation, lets you touch the device, answers your doubts, offers a discount, and follows up the next week to check satisfaction.

**That is Personal Selling.**

> 🎯 **The Tailor Analogy:** Buying a ready-made shirt off a shelf is like **Advertising** (one-way, mass, identical message). Going to a tailor who measures you, discusses fabric, fits it to your body, and alters it later is like **Personal Selling** (two-way, individualized, relational, post-purchase service).

The tailor:
- **Talks with you** (two-way feedback)
- **Customizes** the offer
- **Builds trust** over time
- **Follows up** after delivery

That is *personified promotion* in action.

---

## 1.3 Core Characteristics at a Glance

| # | Characteristic | Meaning |
|---|----------------|---------|
| 1 | **Personal Contact** | Face-to-face or voice-to-voice interaction |
| 2 | **Two-Way Communication** | Both sender and receiver send/receive messages |
| 3 | **Immediate Feedback** | Objections and queries handled in real-time |
| 4 | **Flexibility** | Message adapted to each buyer's needs |
| 5 | **Relationship-Oriented** | Aims at long-term customer loyalty |
| 6 | **Costly Per Contact** | High cost per impression but high conversion |
| 7 | **Measurable** | Sales can be directly attributed to a salesperson |
| 8 | **Most Persuasive Tool** | Highest closing power in the IMC mix |

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of thumb for exams:** Personal Selling = *Highest cost per contact, but highest conversion rate and deepest customer relationship.* This trade-off is the heart of most KTU exam questions.

---

## 1.4 Role of Personal Selling in the Marketing Mix (4Ps)

Personal Selling primarily activates the **Place (Distribution)** and **Promotion** elements. It bridges the **Product** to the **Customer** by:
- Identifying prospects
- Communicating product value
- Providing after-sales service
- Gathering market intelligence
- Reducing buyer cognitive dissonance
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The 7 Steps of the Personal Selling Process (AIDAAS–PRO Model)

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the **standardized selling process** used in professional sales training. Memorize the 7 steps in order:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 7-Step Personal Selling Process:**
> **P**rospecting → **Q**ualifying → **A**pproach → **P**resentation → **H**andling Objections → **C**losing → **F**ollow-up

### Step-by-Step Logic (The "Why" and "How"):

1. **Prospecting (Lead Generation)**
   - *Why:* You cannot sell to an empty chair.
   - *How:* Identify potential buyers using referrals, cold calls, trade shows, social media (LinkedIn), company databases, and direct mail responses.
   - *Output:* A list of *suspects* (unqualified leads).

2. **Qualifying (Lead Filtering)**
   - *Why:* Not every suspect has money, authority, or need (BANT framework: **B**udget, **A**uthority, **N**eed, **T**imeline).
   - *How:* Ask probing questions to separate *prospects* (qualified leads) from *suspects* (disqualified).

3. **Pre-Approach (Homework)**
   - *Why:* Knowledge is power; customers respect prepared sellers.
   - *How:* Research the prospect's industry, competitors, pain points, and decision-makers. Plan the sales call.

4. **Approach (First Impression)**
   - *Why:* The first 7 seconds decide trust.
   - *How:* Greet professionally, use the prospect's name, build rapport, dress appropriately, and use a strong opening line.

5. **Sales Presentation (The Pitch)**
   - *Why:* This is where value is demonstrated.
   - *How:* Use the **AIDAS Model** (Attention → Interest → Desire → Action → Satisfaction) and tailor the *Feature–Advantage–Benefit (FAB)* approach to the prospect's specific need.

6. **Handling Objections**
   - *Why:* Objections are requests for more information, not rejections.
   - *How:* Listen actively, acknowledge, isolate the real concern, refute with evidence, and confirm resolution.

7. **Closing the Sale (Ask for the Order)**
   - *Why:* Many salespersons fail here due to the *fear of rejection*.
   - *How:* Use closing techniques — assumptive close, alternative close, urgency close, summary-of-benefits close.

8. **Follow-Up (Post-Sale Service)**
   - *Why:* Customer retention is 5x cheaper than acquisition.
   - *How:* Confirm delivery, check satisfaction, handle complaints, request referrals, and open cross-selling opportunities.

---

## 2.2 The AIDAS Model of Selling (Attention–Interest–Desire–Action–Satisfaction)

| Stage | Salesperson's Action | Customer's Mental State |
|-------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| **A**ttention | Strong opening hook | "Tell me more" |
| **I**nterest | FAB presentation, demos, stories | "This could help me" |
| **D**esire | Emotional triggers, testimonials, comparisons | "I want it" |
| **A**ction | Close technique, incentives, urgency | "Yes, I'll buy" |
| **S**atisfaction | Follow-up, after-sales service | "I'm glad I bought it" |

> [!NOTE]
> The fifth stage (**S**atisfaction) is what differentiates modern *relationship selling* from older *transactional selling*.

---

## 2.3 Types of Personal Selling

| Type | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **1. Order Taking** | Routine, repetitive selling of convenience goods. | Dairy delivery boy, FMCG re-order |
| **a. Indoor Order Taker** | Stays at store/office. | Retail counter salesperson |
| **b. Field Order Taker** | Visits customers on route. | Pepsi delivery salesman |
| **2. Order Getting (Creative Selling)** | Persuasive, consultative, high-effort selling. | Insurance agent, real estate broker |
| **3. Missionary Selling** | Builds goodwill, does not take direct orders. | Pharmaceutical medical representative |
| **4. Technical Selling** | Sells complex technical solutions. | IT solutions architect, industrial machinery |

---

## 2.4 Qualities of an Effective Salesperson (KTU Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 7 Qualities (Mnemonic: "SMART-CPR"):**
> - **S**elf-confidence & Self-motivation
> - **M**arket & product knowledge
> - **A**bility to empathize (EQ)
> - **R**esilience (handling rejection)
> - **T**enacity and discipline
> - **C**ommunication skills (verbal + non-verbal)
> - **P**roblem-solving attitude
> - **R**elationship-building integrity

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Comparison Matrix: Personal Selling vs Advertising

| Parameter | Personal Selling | Advertising |
|-----------|------------------|-------------|
| **Communication Type** | Two-way, interactive | One-way, mass |
| **Cost per Contact** | High | Low |
| **Reach** | Limited (one-to-one) | Wide (one-to-many) |
| **Feedback Speed** | Immediate | Delayed/lagging |
| **Message Flexibility** | Highly customizable | Standardized |
| **Targeting** | Precise | Broad/demographic |
| **Relationship Building** | Strong, long-term | Weak, transactional |
| **Best For** | High-value, complex products | Mass-market, low-involvement |
| **Measurability** | Direct (sales attributed) | Indirect (GRPs, recall) |
| **Inflexibility** | Not easily scalable | Easily scalable |

---

## 2.6 Advantages and Disadvantages (Examiner's Favourite List)

### ✅ Advantages
1. **Personal attention** to each customer
2. **Immediate feedback** and objection handling
3. **Strong relationship** building
4. **Highly persuasive** (highest closing rate)
5. **Flexible message** (customized)
6. **Reduces customer doubt** through demonstration
7. **Educates the customer** on technical features
8. **Direct source of market intelligence**

### ❌ Disadvantages
1. **Very expensive** per contact
2. **Limited reach** — cannot cover mass markets
3. **Difficult to maintain consistency** across salespeople
4. **Time-consuming** process
5. **Salesperson dependency** — high attrition affects brand
6. **Negative impression** if salesperson is pushy/aggressive

---

## 2.7 The Sales Force Size Formula (KTU Quantitative Must-Know)

For the rare numerical question, KTU may ask you to calculate **sales force size** using the standard workload method:

$$S = \frac{T \times C \times L}{W}$$

Where:

$$S = \text{Number of salespeople required}$$

$$T = \text{Total number of customers in the territory}$$

$$C = \text{Average number of calls per customer per year}$$

$$L = \text{Average time (in hours) per call}$$

$$W = \text{Total selling hours available per salesperson per year}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Workload Method intuition:** Total customer-touch hours needed ÷ Hours one salesperson can deliver = How many salespeople you need. Simple division of labor!

> **Unit Cheat Sheet:**

| Symbol | Unit |
|--------|------|
| $T$ | customers (count) |
| $C$ | calls per customer per year |
| $L$ | hours per call |
| $W$ | hours per salesperson per year |
| $S$ | dimensionless (count of people) |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Utility in Marketing Fields

- **B2B Industrial Marketing** — Selling ₹50 crore turbines to a power plant.
- **Pharmaceutical Detailing** — Medical reps convincing doctors to prescribe.
- **Automotive Sales** — Dealership showrooms for cars, bikes, EVs.
- **Insurance & Banking** — Agents selling LIC, home loans, credit cards.
- **Real Estate** — Builders closing flat bookings.
- **SaaS / IT Solutions** — Enterprise software consultative selling.
- **Luxury Goods** — Watches, jewelry, couture require experiential touch.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Analytical Walkthrough: The Personal Selling Process

Since this is a process-based topic (not a heavy mathematical one), the "derivation" here is the **logical justification of why each step exists and what would fail if it were skipped.**

### Derivation Logic: From Prospect to Loyal Customer

Let **$P$** = the customer pool, and **$L$** = loyalty state. We model the seller's journey as a filtering pipeline:

$$
P_{\text{suspect}} \xrightarrow{\text{Step 1: Prospecting}} P_{\text{qualified}} \xrightarrow{\text{Step 2: Qualifying}} P_{\text{hot}} \xrightarrow{\text{Step 3: Pre-approach}} P_{\text{ready}}
$$

$$
\xrightarrow{\text{Step 4: Approach}} P_{\text{engaged}} \xrightarrow{\text{Step 5: Presentation}} P_{\text{desirous}} \xrightarrow{\text{Step 6: Objection Handling}} P_{\text{convinced}}
$$

$$
\xrightarrow{\text{Step 7: Closing}} P_{\text{buyer}} \xrightarrow{\text{Step 8: Follow-up}} L_{\text{loyal}}
$$

Each arrow represents a **conversion point** where a customer may drop out. The salesperson's job is to maximize the conversion probability at each stage:

$$
\text{Conversion Probability} = P(\text{Stage}_{i+1} \mid \text{Stage}_{i}) = \frac{\text{Number moving from } i \text{ to } i+1}{\text{Number entering stage } i}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Skipping *Step 3 (Pre-approach)* drastically reduces conversion at *Step 4 (Approach)*. Skipping *Step 6 (Objection handling)* is the #1 reason deals are lost at *Step 7 (Closing)*.

---

## 3.2 The FAB Technique (Feature–Advantage–Benefit) — Step-by-Step

This is the **micro-derivation** of a single sales pitch, layer by layer.

1. **Feature (F):** A factual characteristic of the product.
   *Example:* "This laptop has a 16 GB RAM."

2. **Advantage (A):** What the feature *does* — its functional performance.
   *Example:* "It can run multiple heavy applications simultaneously without lag."

3. **Benefit (B):** What the advantage *means for the customer* — translated into their world.
   *Example:* "So you can compile your code in 2 minutes instead of 10, and finish your project by tonight."

> [!TIP]
> **Customers don't buy features; they buy benefits.** This is the most quoted KTU line on this topic. A good salesperson must translate *F → A → B* in every pitch.

---

## 3.3 Sample Numerical — Sales Force Size Calculation (Workload Method)

**Problem:** A pharmaceutical company has **2,000 doctors** in Kerala. Each doctor must be visited **6 times a year**. Each call takes **45 minutes** (0.75 hours). A medical representative works **250 days per year**, with **6 productive selling hours per day**.

**Find:** Number of MRs required.

**Step 1 — Identify variables:**

$$
T = 2000 \text{ doctors}
$$

$$
C = 6 \text{ calls per doctor per year}
$$

$$
L = 0.75 \text{ hours per call}
$$

$$
W = 250 \times 6 = 1500 \text{ hours per MR per year}
$$

**Step 2 — Total hours needed:**

$$
\text{Total Hours} = T \times C \times L = 2000 \times 6 \times 0.75
$$

$$
= 12000 \times 0.75 = 9000 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of MRs required:**

$$
S = \frac{T \times C \times L}{W} = \frac{9000}{1500} = 6
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** The company needs **6 Medical Representatives** to cover all 2,000 doctors in Kerala at the desired frequency.

**Sensitivity Insight (Examiner's Trick Question):**
- If $C$ rises to **8 calls/year**, then Total Hours = $2000 \times 8 \times 0.75 = 12000$ hours → $S = 12000 / 1500 = \mathbf{8}$ MRs.
- This shows **call frequency is a direct multiplier** on manpower cost.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison: Transactional vs Relationship Selling

| Parameter | Transactional Selling | Relationship Selling (Modern) |
|-----------|----------------------|--------------------------------|
| **Time Horizon** | Short-term, one-time sale | Long-term, repeat customer |
| **Focus** | Product features | Customer needs and value |
| **Customer Role** | Passive buyer | Active partner / co-creator |
| **Salesperson Role** | Order taker | Trusted advisor / consultant |
| **Post-Sale** | Minimal | Continuous follow-up, loyalty |
| **Profit Goal** | Single transaction margin | Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) |
| **Example** | Newspaper subscription seller | Key Account Manager at IBM |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Trend:** Modern Personal Selling emphasizes **Relationship Selling / Consultative Selling** over the older *Hard-Sell / Transactional* model. Exam answers using the old "pressurize-the-customer" tone will lose marks.

---

## 3.5 AIDAS Model — Detailed Mental-State Mapping

We can think of the customer as moving through **5 psychological states**. The salesperson must *engineer* the transition:

$$
\text{State}_1 (\text{Unaware}) \xrightarrow{A} \text{State}_2 (\text{Curious}) \xrightarrow{I} \text{State}_3 (\text{Interested})
$$

$$
\xrightarrow{D} \text{State}_4 (\text{Desiring}) \xrightarrow{A} \text{State}_5 (\text{Acting}) \xrightarrow{S} \text{State}_6 (\text{Satisfied \& Loyal})
$$

**Why the 5th 'S' matters:**
Post-purchase **cognitive dissonance** (buyer's remorse) is the biggest threat to repeat business. The salesperson's *follow-up* converts the buyer's satisfaction into **referrals and repeat purchases**, which is the essence of **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)**.

$$
\text{CLV} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{(\text{Revenue}_t - \text{Cost}_t)}{(1 + r)^t}
$$

Where $r$ = discount rate, $n$ = customer lifespan in years. *This shows that retaining a customer for 5 years is worth 5x the profit of acquiring one.*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
